body: The headers are sent over the wire as two individual kv pairs as is specified in the header list.
author: lucacasonato

body:
I don't think the fetch spec prescribes if headers need to be concatenated on the wire. We don't concatenate headers on the wire. Semantically these raw headers are equivalent in HTTP:

```
Foo: abc
Foo: xyz
```

```
Foo: abc, xyz
```

---

The reason the second example does what you expect, is because you are really doing `headers: [... new Headers([['Foo', 'bar'], ['Foo', 'baz']])]`. If you look at the output of that, you will see that the array is: `[ [ "foo", "bar, baz" ] ]`. For the first example, the two values in the array are added to the internal "header list" unmodified as is specified by the fetch spec.
author: lucacasonato

body: Checked in with @annevk, and this is behavior is spec compliant.

author: bartlomieju

body:
This was discussed a bit in #11317.

How this should work is a bit ambiguous—should local variables from the provided module become available in the repl or should only exports?

There's already a non-abiguous way to do this so I'm not sure how much value having an `--eval-file` flag would add:

```ts
deno repl -eval "import * as myModule from './relative-path-to-file.ts';"
deno repl -eval "import { someValue, otherValue } from 'file:///absolute/path-to-file.ts';"
deno repl -eval "import { someValue, otherValue } from 'https://deno.land/x/something.ts';"
```
author: dsherret

body:
as you said, there's already a non-ambiguous way to do the latter, so it's the former I'm interested in. especially being able to add global variables without the nasty `Object.defineProperty` hacks the node compatibility scripts use.

edit:
the former, aka *exactly* how `--eval` currently works

body: Is that done with awareness that Chrome deliberately cuts out locales, or does that come as a surprise? I certainly wasn't expecting that when I first noticed that Chrome was messing up collation.
author: anarchodin

body: @bnoordhuis Could we use the same ICU data file as Node? It seems to have more information for some languages. I thought Node was using the same ICU data file as Chromium (and thus we were using the same file as Node).
author: lucacasonato

body:
Node uses a custom ICU build maintained by IBM. We can't use that (well... we could but not really) because the data format isn't stable across ICU releases. Sooner or later we'd be barred from upgrading V8 until Node's ICU catches up.

edit: it's theoretically possible to fork Chromium's ICU and tweak its build to include more locales but ICU's build is... involved... I expect it's a (ongoing, not one-off) time sink.
author: bnoordhuis

body: Ok, that's unfortunate. I'll put this on the backlog for some rainy day (rainy week?) then.
author: lucacasonato

body: I had a reason to look into this again. The official ICU4C tarball contains a file with _all_ of the data, measuring 30 MB against the Chromium file's 10 or so. I altered `core/runtime.rs` to embed `icudt71l.dat` (from ICU4C) instead of `icudtl.dat` (from Chromium, I presume). As far as I can tell, the missing locales are supported in this binary. I recognise that this is a sizeable hit, but it does suggest that the solution might not be a major time sink.

This is not a bug about a crash report, it is about missing data. This issue prevents me from using Deno, since it can't sort text correctly. I can't compensate on the client side due to the popularity of Chrome, which is the origin of the deliberately crippled data.

Currently, this situation only impacts smaller languages - those that don't have a full Chrome UI translation. I suspect you may see bigger hits if you remove everything except English.

body:
I see.

Node.js also throws the error: `TypeError [ERR_INVALID_CHAR]: Invalid character in header content ["Cookie"]`.

I'm wondering what to do in this case. Is it a web server problem that issues the cookie value with the invalid character, or the header value should be encoded somehow?
author: FSou1

body: To clarify: headers have traditionally been encoded in Latin-1, not ASCII, and both Deno and browsers support Latin-1 characters in headers, both for sending and receiving. Headers are byte sequences on the wire, and Latin-1 is a common enough assumption by HTTP clients that if you were to use a different encoding, the server will likely misunderstand it. There exists a protocol to encode arbitrary Unicode values in headers ([RFC2047](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2047)), but I don't think many servers support it, and so neither do Deno or browsers.

body: This seems to stem from the fact that Deno doesn't support `__proto__` from Annex B. @kt3k @kitsonk any ideas how we could handle that?
author: bartlomieju

body:
Caused by this:
https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/bd53567acf7d7bb28e51c0137054c5a9d9e19bf1/runtime/js/99_main.js#L2-L6

This is a blocker for running `tap` in Deno's compat mode.

`chalk` has over 130mm weekly downloads (https://www.npmjs.com/package/chalk) it seems like something we need to address.
author: bartlomieju

body:
The usage boils down to this: https://github.com/chalk/chalk/issues/32#issuecomment-48426969

Of course this was last looked at seriously back in 2014.  sindresorhus says in https://github.com/chalk/chalk/issues/124 that they are willing to accept a PR as long as it doesn't impact performance.  And that was in 2016.  Runtimes have moved on quite a bit, and even back in 2014, the comment of `Object.setPrototypeOf()` was flouted.  That of course wasn't really a consideration and `Object.setPrototypeOf()` didn't really exist at that point, and the performance certainly wasn't optimized.  As far as I can tell, no one has seriously looked at it in 8 years.

Node.js libraries are littered with `__proto__` exploits the create security advisories. It is one of the most common attack vectors.  We have eliminated it by deleting a deprecated and optional property from the runtime.

So there are two options:

- do the performance research and float a patch to do `Object.setPrototypeOf()`, which really is the proper and best way.
- create a shim for chalk or the compat mode that is a getter named `__proto__` on `Object.prototype` that calls `Object.setPrototypeOf()` with the value passed.

But don't decrease the security of the whole platform for some outdated code from 2014 that was using a "bad" and deprecated API back in 2014.  I can understand that reasoning back in 2014, but things move on.
author: kitsonk

body: Both version 4.0 and 5.0 of chalk do not use `__proto__`. The usage was removed in https://github.com/chalk/chalk/commit/63469d30e4514136651ae0bbe08e2d3046b02024.
author: lucacasonato

body: See https://github.com/chalk/chalk/pull/387. It actually mentions Deno disabling `__proto__` :-)
author: lucacasonato

body: 🤦  so we just need to upgrade dependencies then‽
author: kitsonk

body:
> * create a shim for chalk or the compat mode that is a getter named `__proto__` on `Object.prototype` that calls `Object.setPrototypeOf()` with the value passed.

Other than [some odd behavior in object literals](https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/ecmascript-language-expressions.html#_ref_15704), it seems like [`__proto__` _really is_ a getter/setter](https://tc39.es/ecma262/#sec-object.prototype.__proto__) that calls the internal equivalents of `Object.getProperty` and `Object.setProperty`. So you'd win nothing by defining it explicitly.
author: andreubotella

body: Okay, it seems I made a lot of buzz for no good reason. [`treport`](https://github.com/tapjs/treport/blob/10eb5d90ca3ca24442d2684b1385c0c754c35d49/package.json#L9) is using an outdated version of `chalk` (^3.0.0)
author: bartlomieju

body:
> Other than [some odd behavior in object literals](https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/ecmascript-language-expressions.html#_ref_15704), it seems like [`__proto__` _really is_ a getter/setter](https://tc39.es/ecma262/#sec-object.prototype.__proto__) that calls the internal equivalents of `Object.getProperty` and `Object.setProperty`. So you'd win nothing by defining it explicitly.

Other than only doing it in certain situations, instead of re-introducing it for everything, but it seems like it is an irrelevant issue.

I believe an older 1.x version of Deno supported Windows on ARM?
author: markhughes

body:
Parallels Desktop only supports installing Windows ARM operating system on Apple ARM hardware. There is a way to run x86 version of Windows on Paralles, but it's a huge step down in performance. Currently, installing deno in Windows 11 running on Parallels installs `x86_64-pc-windows-msvc` version of deno. 

```ts
$ deno --version
deno 2.5.1 (stable, release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
v8 14.0.365.4-rusty
typescript 5.9.2
```

When checking the architecture at runtime, get the following

```ts
$ deno eval "console.log({arch: Deno.build.arch, os: Deno.build.os})"
{ arch: "x86_64", os: "windows" }
```

This puts the whole setup into a funky state because using `.node` addons compiled for `x64` doesn't work, because it's not actually an `x64` platform and using `aarc64` version doesn't work because it doesn't match the `deno` runtime. 

[`aarch64-pc-windows-msvc`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/rustc/platform-support/windows-msvc.html) is one of the supported Rust compilation targets, but Deno doesn't seem to make this option available.
author: taras

author: taras

body:
I've been working on this issue in my spare time.

Since Deno 2.5, there's a new compile-time requirement that uses chromium-supplied `rust-toolchain` for compiling temporal.rs, but unfortunately, there's no Windows/ARM64 rust-toolchain available.

See `RustTargetTriple` in https://chromium.googlesource.com/chromium/src/+/main/tools/rust/build_rust.py

There are 2 directions to try: the longer-term thing is to work on `build_rust.py` and add WIndows/ARM64 support, and shorter-term is to try to set `rust_sysroot_absolute` to a prebuilt nightly rust toolchain and see if that'll work. 

I tried setting `sysroot`, but I think there might be some conflations of `host_cpu` vs `target_cpu`, resulting in the wrong build flags (x64-specific) being used in an arm64 build. I should look deeper.

Patching `build_rust.py` is the right thing to do, but I haven't had time in the past week to tinker some more. I've messaged some of the people at Google, they're not interested in hosting the binaries, so someone will have to make a new GH repo and build it in the public and host the artifacts there.

My branches are here: 
https://github.com/duncanmak/rusty_v8/pull/1 (Build windows arm64 using the `windows-11-arm` runners)
https://github.com/duncanmak/rusty_v8/pull/2 (Cross compile windows arm64, using the x64 runners)

body:
`--output` for compile is optional... so I am not sure what you are suggesting.

If you are suggesting that if `--output` is not used that it defaults to writing out a file versus sending the output to stdout for `deno bundle` I don't think that change in behaviour makes total sense.  `deno compile` doesn't output to stdout by default because piping a binary to stdout doesn't make sense, and while you may not use it, there are people who use the output of deno bundle piped for post processing.  Also, while some intelligence can be implied to infer a good name for a binary, it is less obvious what a good default would be for the the bundle name.
author: kitsonk

body:
What I was trying to explain is that `bundle` could make use of an `--output` option as well, instead of providing a path as an argument.

For instance, instead of `deno bundle src/main.ts dist/deno-sandbox.bundle.js`, it could be `deno bundle --output dist/deno-sandbox.bundle.js src/main.ts`.

Currently, both are optional in any case: `--output` for `compile` and the argument for `bundle`.

author: andreubotella

body: @andreubotella ah okay thanks for the answer, do you think there is another way?
author: JumpLink

body:
It might be possible to create a library with an API similar but not identical to `vm` that uses workers in that way. But it wouldn't be compatible with `std/node`, of course.

In the long run, however, I'm working on denoland/deno_core#911, a refactoring of the Deno internals that will make it possible to support realms (a.k.a. "contexts"), the JS feature that powers Node.js's `vm` module and web iframes. I'm working on this to make it possible to support `ShadowRealm`s, which are a proposal for a JS built-in that would provide a realm with a number of security guarantees. I suspect many of the use cases of `vm` would already be covered by `ShadowRealm`s as it is, but something like simulating iframes wouldn't be because of the security restrictions. In any case, although the refactorings needed to support `ShadowRealm`s would not be enough to make it possible to polyfill `vm`, they would definitely go a long way.

author: kitsonk

body:
> If a _vendor or output path already exists, the _vendor/import_map.json and output dependencies would be updated.

I just want to point out that this is a good and important characteristic of this design. It means I can do this:

```
$ deno vendor main.ts
$ deno vendor dynamic.ts
$ deno run --no-remote main.ts
```

(Where `dynamic.ts` is imported asynchronously by main.ts.)

The cumulative nature of `vendor` means I can handle all deps including the dynamically loaded ones, something that bundle and compile I think don't let me do.

author: aaronhuggins

body: When you call `deno cache` you can pick which dir it caches into... how is this different?
author: justinmchase

body:
> When you call `deno cache` you can pick which dir it caches into... how is this different?

1. `deno cache` lays out files in a not very usable way.
2. You shouldn't really edit cache files.
3. It isn't portable to other runtimes (e.g. Deno Deploy).
4. It is all or nothing, this proposal (specifically with the import map) allows individual/partial choices of vendoring.
5. Plus everything stated in the _other options considered_ above

> * Making access to `DENO_DIR` easier - currently, users can edit files in the `DENO_DIR`. There are significant risks to this, like depending on the dependency type and the state of the cache, files may or may not be invalidated in a way that causes the program to emit properly.  Also, it is a reasonable expectation that we should consider `DENO_DIR` and its schema "private" and while accessible, the layout and format may need to change as Deno evolves, and if people are tightly coupled to this, it becomes problematic and could easily hold back the development of Deno for fear of breaking integrations to non-public behaviours. Another risk is that it would not be (ever) compatible with Deno Deploy.
author: kitsonk

body:
> not very usable way

How do you intend to use them other than to just load them as cached files?

> You shouldn't edit cached files

You shouldn't edit vendor files either, no? This would constitute a license violation most of the time and if you want to edit 3rd party code you should fork it and release as an independent package and just cache it as any other library.

What kind of use or modification are you thinking about here? It honestly sounds like `cache` is sufficient but you just need to adjust some patterns you may have previously relied upon in the past. The "vendor" use case here sounds very C++ish, where modern modularity is not easy or common.
author: justinmchase

body:
> How do you intend to use them other than to just load them as cached files?

From the use cases:

> * As a developer, I want to be able to quickly edit a remote dependency to investigate a coding issue.

Specifically, users of Deno have come across several scenarios where this is a common pattern.  Like for example you include a remote dependency and are getting some sort of type error and you want to investigate, or you are consuming a 3rd party library and something isn't working and you would like to be able to drop into that code in the editor and do a console log statement or insert a `debugger;` statement to investigate an issue.

> This would constitute a license violation most of the time

Really? I am not a lawyer, but I am pretty sure you aren't either.

> It honestly sounds like `cache` is sufficient but you just need to adjust some patterns you may have previously relied upon in the past

The good thing, is if you don't like it, you won't have to use it. 😄
author: kitsonk

body:
> This would constitute a license violation most of the time

How would it do that? You're allowed to make changes to most open source license, the legality of it applies to distribution.
author: tmikaeld

body:
>  ...to investigate a coding issue.

Ok got it, that does make sense. I thought you mean actually altering it permanently and then checking it in which is probably unadvisable.

author: porsager

body:
It seems that rustls (or webpki) refuses to parse self signed X509 version 1 certificates and rejects them as invalid. 
I'm not sure that this is really a bug as this is really nonstandard (although still pretty common).

When I use a X509v3 certificate it works (provided `--unsafely-ignore-certificate-errors` is used). On mac you can generate one as follows:

```
openssl req -new -x509 -nodes -days 365 -text -subj "/CN=localhost" -extensions v3_req \
    -config <(cat /etc/ssl/openssl.cnf <(printf "\n[v3_req]\nbasicConstraints=critical,CA:TRUE\nkeyUsage=nonRepudiation,digitalSignature,keyEncipherment\nsubjectAltName=DNS:localhost")) \
    -keyout server.key -out server.crt 
```

I think that is not advisable though, it'd be better to use self signed root CA instead

body:
Yes, you are doing something wrong. By putting an `await` inside the outer `for await` loop, only one connection can be handled at once.

You should use `std/http`:
- https://deno.land/manual@v1.17.3/runtime/http_server_apis
- https://examples.deno.land/http-server

Where did you find this example in the documentation?
author: lucacasonato
jeremythille

body:
> However, why is it working the first time, and also for half the files the second time? It doesn't really make much sense, does it?

The additional requests were likely made on a second or third connection which was just hanging there because of the await. At some point the browser closed the first connection, which let the server handle the second connection.
author: lucacasonato

body:
Also, you really don't want to be using the low level HTTP APIs. They are [not faster than using std/http](https://deno.land/benchmarks#http-server-throughput), but are a lot easier to use wrong. std/http handles all kinds of edge cases for you, while the low level API does not.

There is pretty much no reason to use the low level API.

body: The WPT tests require `window` to be in the global, so if it's removed from Deno, it would have to be re-added either in the WPT bundle, or in `bootstrapMainRuntime` if the `--enable-testing-features-do-not-use` flag is passed.
author: andreubotella

body: @andreubotella can you give an example? I was trying to grep through wpt to see how difficult this would be but couldn’t find any usages that would block our tests.
author: ry

body: It's true that most WPT tests that we run, even the `*.window.js` ones, don't depend on the presence of `window`, but there are some that do. For example, the webstorage tests use the `window[name]` pattern all the time, where `name` can be either `"localStorage"` or `"sessionStorage"` (https://github.com/web-platform-tests/wpt/blob/master/webstorage/defineProperty.window.js). But it seems like they're few enough that they could be fixed upstream.
author: andreubotella

body:
I would disagree with this, because the unstable APIs can and do change from version to version.  That is why they are unstable.  If there was detection of it, people would make wrong assumptions about what is available, basically this would amount to UA sniffing which has not gone well.

If people need to determine if a feature is present, they should feature detect individual features.  If a feature is exposed in a way that it isn't determinable if the feature is present, then that is worth discussing to see if the API's design is correct or not.

mkdir: cannot create directory ‘/home’: Read-only file system
```

In my case however this could be fixed on deno's end by respecting the `$HOME` env variable rather than relying on `/home/$username` being available.

And certainty in quite a few university systems I've seen users homedirs are often set to things like `/home/students/$id` or even more exotic ones.
author: Jamesernator

author: bartlomieju

body: To slightly elaborate in case anyone wonders why the strict checking on env variables; I work in a corporate environment where key/value stores are managed by technical application managers who monitor and supports apps in production (like values.yml for kubernetes). They frequently enter wrong values, or make typos. I imagine this happens for many other developers as well. Clean errors in the logs and thus easy strict env checking is really helpful.
author: GerbenRampaart

body: I'm not familiar with either `joi` or `dotenb-typed`; but having `dotenv` as a `deno_std` module makes sense to me. If the author of that package would be willing to have it merged in `deno_std` we can discuss this topic.
author: bartlomieju

I think it’s a solid idea. I don’t know a project these days that doesn’t use dotenv of some sort. 

Let me know what needs to happen. Happy to assist.

author: pietvanzoen

body: @kt3k This issue should be reopened. A big part of it is not resolved: "computed env types".
author: rojvv

body:
Maybe computed env types was overstretching the scope of this issue. dotenv was useful enough to be the focus here. Plus when it comes to computed types in my experience there are lots of ways to solve that problem so it's probably harder to find consensus for adding that to std.

I wrote a lightweight npm for node to solve that years ago (https://www.npmjs.com/package/dotenv-typed) but Joi is a bigger, heavier and way more popular approach to computed types.

Also, there are computed types solutions already for deno, like https://deno.land/x/computed_types@v1.11.2

Though really, I'm not sure if std is then not just 'doing too much'. It might feel like a bit of a bloat. Don't know how the deno team feels.

tldr: dotenv is so common in the node/deno world that it was justified to standardise in std, computed types maybe not (imho).

author: sirikon

body:
177 documents in memory is quite a lot and the `signature_help` and the `update_diagnostics` are quite laggy... trying to address this isn't going to be straight forward, but I think we need to look at some optimisations, as both `op_script_version` and `op_exists` are getting called way more times than I would expect, and there were recent change in 4.5 that cause that.

Also, there is an import_map involved, which could mean that is adding another layer on top of it all of "complexity" that is causing it to perform slow.
author: kitsonk

body: I'll gladly help in anything I can :slightly_smiling_face:
author: sirikon

body:
Just discovered the origin of my problem within my project. Made a config builder that reads information from environment variables that kinda abused Typescript's type system, the mechanism seems to have really poor performance:

I was doing this: https://github.com/sirikon/bilbostack-app/blob/2ddb90f4cb7c7392b2083858f0b585f1fcc04f26/back/src/config/mod.ts#L6-L19
Using this: https://github.com/sirikon/denox/blob/e13ab86fd75f70435ab4a2183a663d5cfee8b7d9/config/environment.ts

Every `key()` call creates a new builder and passes the generic type making a concatenation of all the keys in a single object. Seems like LSP struggled hard with this script, today added a couple of new `key()` calls and it exploded, with the plugin saying that "computing the types was taking too long, maybe in an infinite loop" or something along the line.

Made a new implementation avoiding the cursed mecanism and all the performance problems vanished.

@kitsonk keep the issue open if you still consider that this scenario shouldn't happen, or close it if it was just my failure (Sorry for the noise!!!). As a suggestion, maybe displaying sooner some kind of notification like "this specific file/code block is taking too long to compute", before crashing, would be great in these scenarios, but that's up to you folks :heart: .

Anyway, thanks for your time :heart:
author: sirikon

body:
Thanks for following up!

> maybe displaying sooner some kind of notification like "this specific file/code block is taking too long to compute"

That is the domain/feature of TypeScript type checker (`tsc`) which is embedded in Deno.  It obviously uses internal information about how many types it is checking and how long that takes, where we can only "observe" and in that sense it is unlikely we will get it "correct", as it might be a slow machine or someone unavoidably has a large type system and they just suffer with it, so it is likely something we will continue to just let `tsc` "worry" about, while we focus on streamlining things around it.

You highlighted a couple of perf issues (though obviously they weren't the root cause of this issue) that we should address mentioned above, so will keep it open for that reason.
author: kitsonk

body: I also have the same issue, has anyone found a solution to this yet?
author: jaanjah

body: Are there any updates on this? Working with the Deno analyser/formatter/linter is extremely frustrating due to just how unbelievably slow it is, similar to what the author of this issue is experiencing, if not worse. I'd expect analysis to be instantaneous, meanwhile it currently takes ~2-3 seconds for anything to happen on-screen, often even longer. It's a real annoyance for development.
author: vxern

body:
> 177 documents in memory is quite a lot

 I laughed when I saw this, my project has 1613 documents, maybe Deno is for small projects only?
The slow response time of the LSP is the most frustrating issue I've encountered in Deno development.
Now I just uninstall vscode_deno and pretend I'm developing a Node.js + TypeScript project.

body: @joshpachner what version of Deno are you at?
author: ry
joshpachner

body: @joshpachner try upgrading to the latest Deno, currently 1.38.5.  It has a few additional LSP improvements.  You can also try disabling the code lens, which for me brings big improvements in performance.  To do so, add `"editor.codeLens": false` to your .vscode settings.json.
author: cknight

body:
Hey @cknight 
I upgraded, and removed code lens. 
I noticed its marginally quicker. (Previously if i was typing "const variableName"  i would get a red line saying " "c" not found" that wouldnt ever go away unless i did a  VC  Windows Reload.   With the new update it actually does go away).

But whats still not working is there's no intelisense when it comes to auto imports. 

Granted. I could just not have it configured properly....

are there any updates on this?
The LSP is slow indeed, I too have noticed this
initially thought it was my machine, but it's definitely got something to do with deno

Anyone figured out any workarounds for better performance?

body: Actually @aalwayslucky can you try running on `deno upgrade --canary`? This commit https://github.com/denoland/deno/commit/23adf99e83672bd846e75ee6390e3521a67bf6c3 made a substantial improvement for completions in one of our testing projects, but it hasn't been released yet.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
~~@nayeemrmn Thanks for pointing that out. It's made a big difference to me as well, the deno LSP is actually usable after upgrading to the latest canary. I will continue to use it and edit this comment if I see any further slowdown but for now it seems to be holding.~~

No it's crashing again now.

author: myobie

body:
@dsherret a couple thoughts I had on it.  The debounce triggers a calculation of diagnostics here:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/0f3a53e5d477ca2c422ed58a65bd368d1eb0a5b2/cli/lsp/diagnostics.rs#L197-L203

And in `update_diagnostics()` and the lint diagnostics are calculated here:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/0f3a53e5d477ca2c422ed58a65bd368d1eb0a5b2/cli/lsp/diagnostics.rs#L197-L203

Ideally only the ts diagnostics need to be debounced, and that the lint update can occur here:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/0f3a53e5d477ca2c422ed58a65bd368d1eb0a5b2/cli/lsp/diagnostics.rs#L211-L217

I think the challenge is though we track "dirty" diagnostics as a single entity per document and we need to make sure we don't end up in some situation where because we trigger them at different times we don't end up with an inconsistent state.  We may just want to limit the dirtiness to the ts diagnostics anyways, since the other two categories are calculated very quickly and can be calculated in a blocking way per change.

author: dsherret

body:
We did not land any changes between 1.17.2 and 1.17.3 that could have changed library dependencies.

However I did notice that the Github Actions windows-2019 image was updated between those releases (20211219 -> 20220110). This update included a Visual Studio upgrade which I suspect changed the vcruntime version that Deno links with.

* Deno v1.17.2: Visual Studio Enterprise 2017 version build 15.9.28307.1745 (released 2021-11-09)
* Deno v1.17.3: Visual Studio Enterprise 2017 version build 15.9.28307.1759 (released 2021-12-14)

As you can see the Visual Studio version was just a month old when 1.17.3 was released.
I would suggest you try to run windows update and see if that fixes the issue.

body:
Is someone still working on this? If not, I'd like to take over

I've got the sample test file covered

![2022-08-02_16-02](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12541974/182394010-6ef334b3-5944-4b8c-a6c7-bcc3b2f15c7c.png)

Open points  
- A test for the sample file
- Add `<ref *>` to non-top-most errors + Test
- Handle aggregate errors + Test
- Possibly sync with JavaScript. Not necessary so far.

Detecting circular references has proven challenging, because `cause` is a clone, rather than a reference as is in JavaScript. My current implementation copies `JsError`, except for the `cause` attribute, to form an `ErrorIdentity`. `ErrorIdentity` can then be compared to other `ErrorIdentity` to check for equality. This is memory intensive, but the best I could come up with so far. Help is welcome. in-dev: https://github.com/Cre3per/deno/blob/circular-cause-in-error/cli/fmt_errors.rs

@crowlKats I've looked at your circular detection for object inspection, thanks for the link! Am I right to assume that in this issue there is at most 1 circular reference, as the `Error.cause` chain is linear?

author: Dgarc359

body:
Feature is implemented in https://github.com/Cre3per/deno/tree/circular-cause-in-error  
My branch has a failing test case (disabled) for when the recursively referenced error is not the top-most error. The test is currently failing due to #15602, I'll re-run the test case once that's fixed.

There's a decent chance I've missing some scenarios when `AggregateError` is involved. Considering `node` doesn't output causes for `AggregateError`s at all, and `AggregateError` isn't all that common, I'd prefer to keep the implementation simple and not to look further into `AggregateError`s.

body:
@bartlomieju 
Thanks! It is great that you are taking a look at this!

Bear with me if I am stating the obvious, but maybe this could be helpful: 
- The imports with missing file extensions can be found all through the package, not only in the top level index.js file.
- Some files, like the _tools/output/categories.js_ file,  imported in _src/addons/unicode-categories.js_, only contains an **unnamed** object of the type _module.exports = [ {....}]_, meaning that the name of the variable/module must be inferred from the file name.

Let me know, if I can help with anything :-)
author: cfjello

body:
> Some files, like the tools/output/categories.js file, imported in src/addons/unicode-categories.js, only contains an unnamed object of the type module.exports = [ {....}], meaning that the name of the variable/module must be inferred from the file name.

@cfjello right, so this is the crux of the problem - since these files are CJS (because of `module.exports` syntax) when they are imported from ES module, they need to be translated on the fly into ES modules too. This is already covered in  https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12648 and I'm actively working on a fix.
author: bartlomieju

error: 'import', and 'export' cannot be used outside of module code at file:///Users/ib/dev/deno/node_modules/xregexp/src/index.js:1:1

```

It seems something is still of - there's `module` entry in `package.json` but Deno doesn't recognize that it's an ESM file and considers it as CommonJs.

author: Srabutdotcom

body:
Took a peek at where it is today with P-521 after @littledivy 's contribution

For externally generated keys

* ✅ `importKey` accepts JWK, and PKCS#8 and exports correctly where noted
* ✅ `importKey` accepts type `"raw"` and `"spki"` public keys and exports correctly

For keys imported from an external source via JWK or PKCS#8

* ✅ `exportKey` on a private key does support outputting to PKCS#8, the output is correct
* ❌ `exportKey` on a private key does not support outputting to JWK, throws `Uncaught NotSupported: Unsupported named curve`
* ✅ `exportKey` on a public key outputs the same `"spki"` format as other implementations and as was imported
* ✅ `exportKey` on a public key outputs the same `"raw"` format as other implementations and as was imported
* ✅ `exportKey` on a public key outputs the same JWK as was imported
* ✅ `exportKey` on a public key to the format "pkcs8" throws, which is consistent with other implementations

For keys generated internally with `generateKey`

* ❌ `exportKey` on a generated public key to the format `"spki"`, `"raw"`, `"jwk"` throws `Uncaught TypeError: expected valid private EC key` – I would have expected it to say "valid public EC key"
* ❌ `exportKey` on a generated public key to the format `"jwk"` throws `Uncaught TypeError: expected valid private EC key`
* ❌ `exportKey` on a generated public key to the format `"pkcs8"` throws, which is consistent with other implementations.
* ❌ `exportKey` on a generated privateKey to the format `"pkcs8"` returns an array buffer starting with `[0x01, 0x00, ...]` where the rest of the bytes are garbage. The output is too short and the output does not decode as a useful ASN.1 object.

This suggests `generateKey` is not implemented correctly. 

For other functions:

* ⚠️ `generateKey` _appears successful_, but is non-functional or corrupted in its ability to export.
* ❌ `sign` is not implemented, throws ``Uncaught TypeError: unknown variant `P-521`, expected `P-256` or `P-384` ``
* ❌ `verify` is not implemented, throws ``Uncaught TypeError: unknown variant `P-521`, expected `P-256` or `P-384` ``

author: hayd

body: Several of us have talked about support remote configuration files before, I believe we should.  I have opened #13488 to cover that.
author: kitsonk

body:
Summarizing hayd/deno-udd#59

1. They develop frontend app, and `deno.json` has `"lib": ["dom"]`
2. They try to update dependencies by `udd`. They run `deno run https://deno.land/x/udd/main.ts`
3. Here `deno.json` for frontend app is detected and applied to `udd` and causes `Cannot find name 'Deno'`

I feel the auto detection at step 3 is a questionable behavior. `deno.json` is usually for local files in my view. Is it correct to always auto-detect deno.json even when the entry script is a remote file?
author: kt3k

body: @kt3k in that scenario, I tend to agree.  If the main specifier is a remote specifier, then we shouldn't attempt to auto-resolve a configuration file.  The vision for the resolution was to resolve relative to the target of the script.  So either we attempt to resolve the remote target, or better for now, don't auto resolve.

body: There is only a single promise (which maps to only a single op); once it resolves, once of your futures is resolved. Why should the second future be polled again if the event loop is empty and there's no more work to do? I you schedule another timeout it should be polled and resolved.
author: bartlomieju

body:
There are two promises ( in the original program) and two futures (both in the original program and in the reduced testcase). The name get_fut was a bad choice, it should be create_fut.

The poll API requires that if Pending is returned, then something must have registered the waker. In here a single promise unblocks two futures. In the original program there are two promises that compete in a single call to poll_event_loop.

The net result is that the above program freezes, and it is not clear how to work around it.
author: espindola

body:
> The poll API requires that if Pending is returned, then something must have registered the waker. In here a single promise unblocks two futures. In the original program there are two promises that compete in a single call to poll_event_loop.

Futures created by you register the waker - `JsRuntime` can resolve multiple promises in a single tick - this is by design. But you use two different futures to poll a single future, in this case (if I'm not mistaken) one future overrides the registered waker for the other. 

Do you have some specific use case in mind? Maybe it's just a matter of restructuring the code?
author: bartlomieju

body: There are still two futures. They happen to both resolve in the same time, but there are two. In the original program each request becomes a promise, which gets mapped to a future. I will expand the reduced testcase a bit.

author: lucacasonato

body:
The import is invalid based on Deno's module resolution, but I agree it is surprising that this would be a hard failure.  We should either silently fail on these types of imports (which we never analysed in previous version of Deno) or better, print out a warning that they are missing.

That being said, the project is a Deno specific project, with Deno specific imports, written in TypeScript.  The JSDoc block comments with imports is a really bad way to write stuff in a `.ts` file and the package authors should really fix their code to a) not use JSDoc types in TypeScript files and b) amend their import specifiers to be compatible with Deno module resolution, which means they need to include the extensions.

I would open an issue with the package maintainers as well to update their code.
author: kitsonk

body:
Thank you for your reply. This comment is really not compliant. It is possible that the project is migrated from JS.
The recent versions of bundle optimization are what I'd like to see. Good job.
author: jiawei397

body:
In looking into this, it is only these types of imports in TypeScript files that cause the error.  JavaScript documents behave as I would expect, in that Deno runs them, just ignoring missing type only imports.

So while I didn't think denoland/deno_graph#118 is a full fix, it actually is, as I have checked and `tsc` does not attempt to resolve JSDoc imports in TypeScript files, and so we shouldn't be adding them to the module graph.  So having that fix merged and integrated into CLI will resolve the issue.

author: ghost

body:
Closing this as not planned.

Reading a user's GECOS / full display name is a fairly niche, platform-specific need, and there's no web-standard or `node:`-compat basis for it: Node's `os.userInfo()` only exposes `uid`, `gid`, `username`, `homedir`, and `shell`, not the GECOS field. It's also straightforward to do in userland, as the snippet in the original post shows (parsing `/etc/passwd` on Unix, `wmic`/PowerShell on Windows), so it doesn't need to be a built-in.

If there's renewed interest with a concrete, cross-platform use case, feel free to open a fresh issue. Thanks for the suggestion!

body:
Almost every command line tool does not display it licenses, with the license information available at the source of distribution, which it is for Deno.  Can you provide an example of a CLI tool that meets your requirement.

For context, are you a lawyer and by what information to you base your claims that Deno is violating the licenses?
author: kitsonk

body:
I'm not aware of any dependency that would require the Deno CLI itself to display licenses. However the majority of Deno dependencies require that some information (the license text and/or copyright statements) be distributed along with the binary in some form. Embedding it is one way but distributing a file that rolls-up all third party licenses can be more practical.

I'm not a lawyer but this isn't a complicated legal situation. When a license says you must give people a copy of it, well, you gotta do it.
author: LynnKirby

body:
> However the majority of Deno dependencies require that some information (the license text and/or copyright statements) be distributed along with the binary in some form. 

So if you aren't referring to the Deno CLI itself what are you referring to?

If you are suggesting that anyone using `deno compile` to create a stand alone binary and distribute needs to provide the license for Deno CLI plus the license for any software they included in the binary along with the binary, then I am not sure how that is an issue for the Deno CLI to address, other than possibly warning people that if they are redistributing a binary built with `deno compile` they need to ensure they are making the appropriate licenses for the software they include available as well.  But that would be the project providing legal advice, which it is probably shouldn't do.

> I'm not a lawyer but this isn't a complicated legal situation. When a license says you must give people a copy of it, well, you gotta do it.

And how is Deno CLI not doing it?  According to your legal advice, Deno CLI itself isn't required to display licenses.
author: kitsonk

body: The Deno CLI program itself isn't required to display licenses from the command line but you must, in some fashion, include license information with it when you distribute it. For example in the GitHub releases ZIP files and when using `deno_install`.
author: LynnKirby

body: So your legal advice is that the license be included in the binary distribution archive?
author: kitsonk

body: My advice is to examine the licenses of code included in the distributed binaries and then follow their terms and conditions. Each license is different but, in general, FOSS licenses require the license and copyright notices to be included when the software is distributed. Including those notices in the binary distribution archive would satisfy some, if not all, of them.
author: LynnKirby

body:
It's easy enough for us to generate a license list during CI and upload that as a separate artifact to GitHub releases by using `cargo-about` with the following config:

```toml
# about.toml
accepted = [
    "MIT",
    "Apache-2.0",
    "ISC",
    "BSD-2-Clause",
    "BSD-3-Clause",
    "BSL-1.0",
    "CC0-1.0",
    "MPL-2.0",
    "Zlib",
    "OpenSSL",
    "Apache-2.0 WITH LLVM-exception",
    "GPL-2.0",
    "NOASSERTION"
]

[private]
ignore = true
```

I threw up the generated HTML license document here for the time being: https://license.deno.dev/.
author: lucacasonato

body: I can tackle this next week, in time for the next release. Adding a simple `licenses.txt` file to the GitHub releases seems pretty doable.
author: lucacasonato

body:
Don't forget V8 (which also affects the `rusty_v8` releases), the bundled TypeScript compiler, and the various things that Rust compiles into binaries (like `std`).

`cargo-about` is great for collecting all the crate licenses but I'd still recommend going through each license once to see what obligations you have. License stuff is a real pain but it's important to get it right, especially since you're making a commercial product out of Deno.

I think we've found the root cause
In `hexf`, LLVM optimizes 2.0.powf(exponent) (https://github.com/lifthrasiir/hexf/blob/master/parse/src/lib.rs#L343) into exp2f.

body:
This is an unfortunate limitation in the [`aes_gcm`](https://docs.rs/aes-gcm/latest/aes_gcm/struct.AesGcm.html). The tag length is required to be 128 bits.
When encrypting, the tag is truncated to the specified tag length as per the spec, so encryption doesn't fail.

Ideally we should be throwing instead of panickin during decryption.

author: benjamingr

body: Why wouldn't dynamic `import(...)` be a solution in your case?  `deno bundle` does not touch these.  Especially because it makes no assumptions about the location at run-time.  Ideally we want to keep `deno bundle` minimalistic and straight forward way of creating single file library bundles, and nothing more, and intelligently eliding static imports sort of goes against the grain, where dynamic imports are part of the platform already.
author: kitsonk

body:
While `import(...)` can serve its purpose it still requires a relative, static path, making it a less elegant option.  Import maps allow bare modules to be specified separately and abstractly making feature switching, versions rollbacks, and adapter-like substitutions dead simple.  It makes modules pluggable in the same way as adapters or decorators are program pluggable.

I didn't think this would be a difficult implementation.  I thought one would simply pass in something like `--external 'jquery','lodash','my-custom-mod'` and the bundler would skip the mentioned external imports.  Bare modules thus become abstract dependencies and import maps become declarative configurations.  And all the better when import maps are finally implemented in browsers. 

An even simpler implementation would be to ignore bare modules which are not specified in an import map.  It should be nothing to ignore unmapped bare modules during bundling, as the mapping only actually matters during execution.
author: mlanza

body:
> While `import(...)` can serve its purpose it still requires a relative, static path, making it a less elegant option.

Import maps apply to dynamic imports.
author: kitsonk

body:
All I am saying is leaving bare modules alone as part of a bundling process benefits the use case I described.  
 Presently I am already doing this with Rollup.  Others must be doing it too, since the feature exists there.  Using Deno could eliminate the need for Rollup provided this one feature.  I guess I figured optionally ignoring bare modules (during bundling) would not be a significant effort.  If you're not seeing the value, feel free to close the request.
author: mlanza

body:
Bare specifiers tend to be external `node_modules` dependencies in Node. Probably why rollup has that, it would be a strange category in Deno. The equivalent would usually be remote modules.

Being able to exclude remote or arbitrary modules from bundles would be useful: `deno bundle main.ts --exclude-remote`, `deno bundle main.ts --exclude=https://example.com/remote.ts,./relative.ts,bare`. The former lets you distribute libraries as bundles for example. The latter provides granular control over split bundle structures, e.g. a frontend (or native I suppose) app with two entrypoints that share a large part of their module graphs could be cut into 3 optimised bundles by some client tool. Similar concept to @mlanza's use case.

The imports for excluded modules would be rewritten to be relative to the main module if applicable and left in the bundle. @mlanza's use case would be achieved with `deno bundle main.ts --exclude=foo,bar`.
author: nayeemrmn

body: Import maps is the killer feature I'm trying to preserve, at least in the context of bundling. Being able to provide actual locations for bare modules later is incredibly useful.  They provide a means of rewiring sources without a build pipeline.  You just swap out the import map.
author: mlanza

body: `deno bundle` has been removed in Deno 2.
author: lucacasonato

body:
In a discussion related to (un)safety of passing buffers into FFI calls marked `nonblocking` @andreubotella suggested an `AsyncBuffer` object that would own an `ArrayBuffer` and work as a parameter in FFI calls. The reasoning for this was that a dedicated object would facilitate detaching the buffer when it is being sent to a Tokio spawn thread for the nonblocking FFI call. The idea for the `AsyncBuffer` was inspired by BYOB streams.

Currently, sending a buffer into a nonblocking FFI call is kind of UB. JavaScript retains the ability to read and write into the buffer while in Rust terms the buffer is sent exclusively to another thread (I think?). This is fertile ground for data races and UB of all sorts.

This ties nicely into this `UnsafeOwnedPointer` proposal: The `UnsafeOwnedPointer` could be made to work like `AsyncBuffer` in nonblocking FFI calls in that it would detach the buffer when the FFI call is initiated and would replace its internal buffer when the async call finishes with a new buffer pointing to the same backing memory. This would eliminate the possibility of data races and would facilitate correct Rust language semantics for sending the buffer to the Tokio spawn thread.

The current `UnsafePointer` API shows itself as a potential issue here, specifically the `UnsafePointer.of` static method. If it is possible for JavaScript to get a pointer value to its own `ArrayBuffer`, then it can read data from that pointer using `UnsafePointerView` even when the buffer is detached. Based on this I might argue that eventually the FFI pointers should be split into two cases:

1. `OwnedPointer`: Creation would usually be through the constructor with only length provided. Optionally provide a static method `from` to copy from (or unsafely take ownership of) a given buffer, facilitating pre-initialized memory use-cases.
2. `ForeignPointer`: This is the current `UnsafePointer`, except that no creation would be allowed. These would only appear as return values from FFI calls. Reading is always done using a `PointerView` class.

As it comes to passing `ArrayBuffer`s into FFI calls directly: For synchronous functions this could still be allowed, but for nonblocking calls I believe it should throw an error. There is no way to detach the buffer and return it to the caller once the Promise resolves, and there is no way to guarantee memory safety while the Promise is ongoing.

body:
I believe this is impractical to do. We should be converging on a single configuration instead of adding possibility to use separate configs per file. 

Besides very hard implementation it would be also very hard to configure for users. I can see the appeal of this feature, but I believe it's the opposite direction the ecosystem should be going. This is actually the same problem as import maps per "library" - there is no support for merging import maps and it's questionable how that would work - ie. how do you specify that your library/package requires concrete import map (or TS configuration file) to work properly.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> We should be converging on a single configuration instead of adding possibility to use separate configs per file.

> I believe it's the opposite direction the ecosystem should be going.

@bartlomieju Can you link to information about the project that informs your opinion (or will you explain that here)? I'd like to better understand why you don't think that Deno should provide the ability to execute scripts (in a type-safe way) which act as a top-level instruction set for orchestration of tasks (which may or may not execute in other runtimes). I was always under the impression that Deno existed to fulfill purposes just like this.

I agree that implementation wouldn't be trivial (and — AFAIK — it would actually be novel in regard to a compilation ability: making Deno even more useful/desirable as a runtime).

In regard to your import map analogy: I expect this to be used by end-users: meaning that if a library provider utilizes this feature somehow, it would be only an implementation detail (e.g. running in a forked process), opaque to any downstream users (for exactly the reason you mentioned).
author: jsejcksn

body:
I haven't thought extensively about implementation, but I imagine that it would require the ability to instruct the compiler to "reset" its type environment at certain points in the module graph (using either a config file or some kind of inline module directive, like a deno directive or yet-to-be-invented triple-slash reference): so that, in those branches downstream of those points in the graph (for those modules and their participating dependencies), an isolated type environment can exist which would potentially have conflicts with the upstream type environment.

According to my current (limited) understanding of the the TypeScript compiler API, this is impossible: so it would mean forking new compiler instances at these points in the graph, and then somehow forwarding the results upstream.

This would mean that, even though the conflicting ambient types could be used without issue in the modules being evaluated in the forked compiler process, they probably couldn't be used in the public-facing types of any exports (what would appear in a compiled declaration file) because those would need to be forwarded back upstream.

(I can put together a simple example of modules from such a conflicting graph: just let me know if you think it's needed.)

Some options, like jsx, can already be configured on a per file basis. It could make sense for other options too.

TypeScript has the [Project References](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/project-references.html) feature, which together with [skipLibCheck](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#skipLibCheck) allows for compilation isolation.

I wish something like type narrowing for the global scope existed 😅

body:
> I would say this, FFI security fall under module integrity security, Deno currently provide an integrity checksum by running `deno cache --lock=lock.json --lock-write mod.ts`.
> 
> So, if we could get Deno to also enforce this for FFI, we can call it a day and say Deno load only secure modules.
> 
> Assuming an attacker can edit files but not the `lock.json` file, then any attack that edit files will not work, meaning you are responsible to only use secure FFI.

This could be an acceptable solution and seems more is somewhat more in line with current Deno uses, though I do have some concerns. The lock option is only used when a lock entry is found unless the `--cached-only` flag is used. If the same model was applied to FFI libs, then any "side loading" of FFI libraries would be allowed without a hitch unless the very strict (though definitely preferable) `--cached-only` flag was being used.

Additionally, I am not sure how this might work with "compiled" executables pointing to symlinks and at the very least any library version differences would cause load failures. This is fair to a degree, since the lock file tries to guarantee integrity and a version difference is a potential integrity failure but it does impose a significant price on distributions.

To a degree, I could perhaps hit back by asking if `--allow-run` should not have a comma separated allowlist associated with it and should instead rely on the lockfile for determining safe-to-run executables?

> For your first point, my understanding is this, if I know that an FFI export named "readSettings" reads a file, it is feasible to think about use the same permission model on FFI, like this:
> 
> ```ts
> const library = Deno.dlopen("./lib.so", {
>   readSettings: {
>     // this api does not exits yet
>     readPermission: true // deno throw whitout --allow-read
>   },
> });
> ```
> 
> This will introduce more ambiguity though, because now if an export is not marked with this kind of permission flag I could assume it is safe to use.

I agree that this proposal is not a particularly good one. It is why I also postfixed it with a pondering on its usefulness.

> On a final note, only function that are executed by the Deno process itself can be considered `"secure"` as long as the process is not altered and does not contain bug or exploits, which is a guarantee that Deno does not claim in the first place.

The point here of course is to reduce the number of possible exploits.
author: aapoalas

body:
The main actionable improvement proposed here has shipped: `--allow-ffi` now accepts a path allowlist, so you can scope FFI to specific libraries instead of an all-or-nothing toggle:

```
--allow-ffi[=<PATH>...]   Allow loading dynamic libraries. Optionally specify
                          allowed directories or files.
                          --allow-ffi  |  --allow-ffi="./libfoo.so"
```

This covers the original "trust my given library, not all system libraries" use case.

The other two ideas (separating "load library" from "call symbol" permissions, and per-symbol permissions in the `dlopen` definitions) are essentially moot given how FFI works: once a native library is loaded and a symbol is called, the native code runs entirely outside Deno's sandbox and can do anything regardless of permission checks. Splitting load-vs-call or annotating symbols can't be enforced against native code, so they wouldn't provide real security guarantees. FFI is, by design, the unsafe escape hatch; the realistic mitigation is integrity-pinning the loaded artifacts, which is already available via `deno.lock`.

Closing since the meaningful part is implemented and the remainder isn't enforceable. If there's a concrete, enforceable FFI permission refinement you'd like, please open a focused issue.

I managed to trigger a breakpoint from code run in a worker.
- In `chrome://inspect/#devices` I clicked 'inspect' below 'deno - worker-0'
- Then I opened the file I wanted to debug in the inspector
- A notification popped up stating 'This script is on the debugger's ignore list'
- I clicked 'Remove from ignore list' which seems to untoggle the 'Content scripts injected by extensions' preference
- Then I placed my breakpoint and the debugger paused once it hit that breakpoint

body:
To my surprise I found a workaround to get worker breakpoints to hit in VSCode. 
- Use a launch compound with an attach request to debug and "restart": false
- Have a wait to make sure debugger has time to attach to worker
- Use console.log inside worker onmessage which seems to be the only position where the debugger can bind?

Unfortunately this is still highly inconsistent, I think the debugger accidentally attaches to the same process twice a lot of the time. If anyone knows how to explicitly have the ports between main and workers be different or different ws addresses let me know.

body:
I very much like this idea. I also toyed around with the idea of trying to do this with a reader pattern but that probably doesn't really make much sense.

One option for the `return` and might be to include the callback in the constructor instead of using the explicit `ThreadSafeCallbackContext` class to "simulate" the callback. If one thinks of callbacks in FFI, there is no way to "cram in" multiple callbacks into that one callback parameter. The API proposed here makes it possible for one FFI call with one callback parameter to split into multiple handlers, calling multiple returns which would definitely be an error of some sort.

A point of optimization is that a lot of FFI callbacks will likely not have a return value. For these, I would expect Deno to internally optimize to not wait for any async callback finishing.
author: aapoalas

body:
One further thought I had: At present I believe that #13162 callbacks do not survive beyond the actual FFI call, ie. even if an FFI call takes a callback that will be called from the same thread, it cannot keep that callback and call it later.

This seems like an understandable limitation based on my limited understanding of the Rust code underneath and how it needs to manage the lifetime of the callback info etc. However, a lot of FFI libraries will not function with this limitation.

For these cases in order to make the lifetimes explicitly handled from JS-side, an explicit callback class like `ThreadSafeCallback` might be needed.
author: aapoalas

body:
> One option for the `return` and might be to include the callback in the constructor instead of using the explicit `ThreadSafeCallbackContext` class to "simulate" the callback.

Sounds good to me, it would make more sense to just do that than a explicit call context

> A point of optimization is that a lot of FFI callbacks will likely not have a return value. For these, I would expect Deno to internally optimize to not wait for any async callback finishing.

I agree in most use cases callbacks don't even need to return something. In case we don't have a return value, there wouldn't even be a deadlock (which is caused by waiting for return value on same thread) as I mentioned in issue. But that *might* cause issues because a library may expect callback to be executed synchronously, which would not really be if we don't wait for the function to return.

> This seems like an understandable limitation based on my limited understanding of the Rust code underneath and how it needs to manage the lifetime of the callback info etc.

I don't think there's any limitation, we can just have a callback resource for that using Deno's resource table.

I actually have a POC for this API built using FFI, only thing it is missing is `unref`ing the async op is not possible, so a `next` call will prevent Deno from exiting.. which can likely be fixed if its implemented into Deno itself since I think there is an internal API to unref async ops.

body:
> I agree in most use cases callbacks don't even need to return something. In case we don't have a return value, there wouldn't even be a deadlock (which is caused by waiting for return value on same thread) as I mentioned in issue. Yeah, what I meant by this was that if a `ThreadSafeCallback` was provided an `async` function for the callback while the definition of the callback expected no return value, then Deno would just call the JS callback and disregard the Promise returned, yielding to the caller immediately after without waiting for the Promise to finish first.

That is, if `async` functions as callbacks are allowed.

body:
I finished writing up a proposal for a stable Deno FFI API, including thread-safe callbacks though I called them `StoredCallback`s. In the end I decided that there is so little separating a thread-safe callback and just, in general, stored callback that it's probably simpler for the two to be the same thing with just an options flag for thread-safe features.

You can read my proposal here: https://github.com/aapoalas/deno-stable-ffi If you have any comments, complaints or improvements I'd be happy to hear them.

body:
I would expect that it would take significantly longer and not produce initial results faster.  Whenever you calculate diagnostics, tsc will check the entire dependency graph of the project is fresh, make any updates to the project, etc before calculating diagnostics.

Way back when we saw a significant performance improvement by batching all requested specifiers into a single request, which is why we went down that path.

It is worth investigating, but whatever upside to splitting them out would is unlikely to be noticeable to the user, and only likely "backup" the queue of work to calculate would be my hypothesis.

body:
After some investigation I was able to nail down the two issues that cause this behavior:

1. Attemting to acquire an exclusive file lock using `Deno.flockSync(rid, true)` after `Deno.flockSync(rid, false)` has been called on the same file hangs on Windows, but not on Unix.
2. Attempting to terminate a worker that is currently waiting on a file lock during `Deno.flockSync` hangs the main thread on both Unix and Windows.

body:
In Zsh you can use `_alternative` to specify different functions to run for the same argument. For example, for the `open` command it ends up suggesting files and URLs like so:
```sh
_alternative 'files: :_files' 'urls: :_urls'
```
![completion suggestions for open](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1441704/157266836-ebf960af-52e5-4406-9438-d562a34f8217.png)

I think clap can implement that for Zsh but I don't know if fish supports that (`ValueHint` already seem to only be supported for zsh and fish).

In any case, file-only suggestions seems like a satisfactory solution, it's very rarely that you type a URL, so if you want to go with https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/10166#issuecomment-1060616635 that works.

author: dsherret

body:
I think we need to define what resource timings we would expose before we figure out how to clear them, right?

If you look at the [specification](https://w3c.github.io/resource-timing/#resources-included-in-the-performanceresourcetiming-interface) there really isn't anything that Deno loads that we would tag as a `"resource"` entry type, which is the main reason we never implemented them.

I think there is a deeper question of an approach for something like this, where an API is non-sensical in a Deno context should we add it as effectively a noop API just for compatibility or if userland would just "polyfill" it at runtime.  There is a "cost" to maintaining a noop API, as well as could easily be misleading in this case for people to assume that Deno has performance timings tagged as `"resource"`, which it doesn't and likely shouldn't.

My vote would be we don't bake it in and that the community would polyfill it.

@lucacasonato @bartlomieju @crowlKats thoughts?
author: kitsonk

body: My vote would be to provide and API and noop in that case.
author: bartlomieju

body:
ReadableStreams are quite slow as compared to the old Reader/Writer interface, because the buffer is not being reused. Allocating a new `Uint8Array` on every chunk is one of the reasons of the slow performance on big uploads.

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/d7b27ed63bf74c19b0bc961a52665960c199c53a/ext/http/01_http.js#L383-L390

By reusing the buffer (not using ReadableStream) uploading a 2gb file takes around 3 seconds, the same upload using a ReadableStream takes 4.8 seconds on my computer.

I love having spec compliant `Response` and `Request`, but when dealing with a lot of data is nice to have some lower level APIs. I think we should expose lower level APIs for users that really need that speed. 

The same bottleneck happens in `fetch` when downloading large files.

body:
Revisiting this on the latest canary (`deno 2.8.2+ecc3053`) vs Node 24.8.0, uploading a 1 GB file via `curl -T` to a server that pipes the request body to disk.

**TL;DR:** The catastrophic part of this report (1 GB taking "20s–5m", CPU saturation, and getting progressively slower on each repeat) is no longer reproducible. The "~2x slower than Node" part was an apples-to-oranges comparison: the original Deno script uses **Web Streams** (`req.body.pipeTo(file.writable)`) while the Node script uses **native Node streams** (`stream.pipeline(req, fs.createWriteStream)`). When the API is held constant, the runtime gap disappears.

I decomposed it into a 2×2 matrix (same API on both runtimes), 1 GB upload, typical warm time:

| | API | Runtime | Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | node:http + native fs streams | Node | ~0.52 s |
| B | node:http + native fs streams | Deno | ~0.57 s |
| C | node:http + Web Streams (`Readable.toWeb`/`Writable.toWeb`) | Node | ~1.05 s |
| D | node:http + Web Streams | Deno | ~0.76 s |
| E | `Deno.serve` + `file.writable` (Web Streams) | Deno | ~0.80 s |

What this isolates:

1. **Same API → no runtime gap.** B vs A (both native streams): 0.57 s vs 0.52 s, within noise. Deno's socket read / file write / I/O plumbing is on par with Node.
2. **The cost is the Web Streams abstraction, not the runtime.** On Node alone, A→C goes 0.52 s → 1.05 s just by switching the same sockets/files to Web Streams. Node pays this penalty too. This matches @marcosc90's earlier observation: Web Streams allocate a fresh `Uint8Array` per chunk instead of reusing a buffer, plus promise-based backpressure overhead.
3. **On the actual apples-to-apples comparison (Web Streams on both), Deno is faster than Node:** D vs C is 0.76 s vs 1.05 s, and `Deno.serve` (E) lands right alongside it.

So the original 2x figure came from comparing Deno's spec-compliant Web Streams against Node's hand-optimized native streams. With the API held constant, Deno is on par with Node (native) and ahead of Node (Web Streams). The only remaining delta is the inherent per-chunk allocation cost of the Web Streams spec, which is tracked separately as a streams-perf optimization (BYOB / buffer reuse), not an HTTP-server or runtime issue.

Closing as the reported pathology is resolved.

(Benchmarked on Apple Silicon over loopback with an SSD; absolute numbers will differ from the original 2-core VPS, but the relative decomposition holds.)

body:
+1 to this FR, and to mirror dprint's semicolon options. Plenty of js/ts coding standards actively discourage routine use of semicolons and `asi` mode deals with edge cases where necessary.

As a side note, if deno uses dprint under the hood, why not expose dprint's complete config via deno's config file. Maybe a `dprint` subkey under `fmt` or `fmt..options`?
author: qgates

body: I am using Dprint directly precisely because Deno appears to be hiding options. Not clear why Deno would do that. Is it just out of sync? Or is it deliberate?
author: chasm

body: (That said, I also use Dprint instead of Prettier on Node apps.)
author: chasm

body:
> Not clear why Deno would do that.

Deno's philosophy is that having 1 way to do things is better than multiple ways to do the same thing. So that includes formatting and deno has the opinion there that it is better to be explicit about semi's rather than relying on ASI
author: MierenManz

body:

body:
> Deno's philosophy is that having 1 way to do things is better than multiple ways to do the same thing. So that includes formatting and deno has the opinion there that it is better to be explicit about semi's rather than relying on ASI.

If that's true, it's sad. It means that they've chosen to lock in their own biases rather than trusting their users to make their own decisions. And over something as trivial as ASI, too. I really hope that's not the case. It would lower my opinion of Deno and Dahl quite significantly.

@[qgates](https://github.com/qgates), @[chasm](https://github.com/chasm)
I think that it is good thing to expose custom options. This enables to change the underlying formatter if needed. From *Deno* point of view, *dprint* is just an implementation dependency.
author: Conaclos

body:
I am currently using dprint to achieve this. But the issue still stands, the majority 99.9 % of the Deno code that's published now has not needed visual noise semicolons. Simply because it's not trivial to avoid adding them. 

Hope Deno team considers adding this option.
author: nikivdev

body:
> > Not clear why Deno would do that.
> 
> Deno's philosophy is that having 1 way to do things is better than multiple ways to do the same thing. So that includes formatting and deno has the opinion there that it is better to be explicit about semi's rather than relying on ASI

Thus, other options such as `--options-single-quot`, `--options-indent-width`, `--options-use-tabs`, and `--options-line-width` should be removed.

I agree that the number of options should be "minimal". However, there exists endless debates in the community (single vs double quotes, tab vs space indentation, and semicolon vs no semicolon). In these case options should be provided.
author: Conaclos

body:
This discussion is the perfect example why we will not be introducing additional options to the built in formatter. All source code written for Deno should use the same formatting options for consistency, and to reduce this kind of discussion. In our eyes ecosystem consistency is much more important than opinions of individuals. This is also why we limit configuration options of the runtime and type checker as much as we do.

Thank you for all the comments on the issue.

author: amatsagu

body:
> Network related elements makes Rust side to launch new threads in order to make it "non blocking", right? We can start new TCP server or going higher - WS/HTTP and those can work on single thread on small usage but once our service gets heavily used - it starts to spawn new threads to manage that.

That's not true, all of Deno's networking runs on a single thread, Rust side doesn't spawn new threads for anything besides FS operations. 

> During such heavy usage - Deno's TCP server started to have issues with memory - it simply grows infinitely cause it couldn't handle such pressure at once.

Can you provide an heap profile for such case? It's hard to debug what's the problem in this situation.

> My suggestion is adding a flag like --max-threads to the deno run/compile commands that would allow user to define how many threads can Deno use on their machine. Of course perfect solution would be optimizing Deno's TCP/WebSocket API but increasing number of max threads sounds good enough.

Again, Deno only uses threadpool for FS operations and this number is fixed. The only other case where there are multiple threads is when you spawn workers using `new Worker()` API.

body:
Closing as resolved -- this works today.

The CSS-wide keywords \`unset\`, \`initial\`, \`revert\` (and \`inherit\`) all reset
\`%c\` styling back to the terminal's default color, which is the
terminal-agnostic behavior requested here and matches the Firefox example:

\`\`\`js
console.log("%cRED%cUNSET", "color: red", "color: unset");
// "RED" in red, then "UNSET" reset to the terminal default
\`\`\`

Under the hood these values aren't a named/hex/rgb/hsl color, so the console
emits the ANSI "default foreground color" code \`\x1b[39m\` (or \`\x1b[49m\` for
\`background-color\`), which restores the terminal's own default regardless of
its background. This is covered by unit tests in \`tests/unit/console_test.ts\`:

\`\`\`js
cssToAnsiEsc({ ...DEFAULT_CSS, color: "inherit" })           // => "_[39m"
cssToAnsiEsc({ ...DEFAULT_CSS, backgroundColor: "inherit" }) // => "_[49m"
\`\`\`

\`unset\`/\`initial\`/\`revert\` travel the identical code path. (Strict CSS
semantics for \`initial\` differ -- e.g. \`color: initial\` is black on the web --
but "reset to terminal default" is the sensible behavior for a terminal and is
what Firefox does too.)

body:
Hi @a-lafrance, thanks for looking into this.

> Is my understanding correct? I'm under the impression that I'm basically just setting the check flag to either None or Local as a default for repl, so I'd just like to make sure I'm on the right track

Yes, this is right. In `repl_parse` in `cli/flags.rs` set `flags.no_check` to `None` and that should be it.

body:
@kt3k I encountered the same (or similar) issue when processing a file that is about 1.13 Gb unzipped. The following is the message:
```
thread 'main' panicked at 'called `Option::unwrap()` on a `None` value', serde_v8/magic/u16string.rs:54:10
stack backtrace:
   0: _rust_begin_unwind
   1: core::panicking::panic_fmt
   2: core::panicking::panic
   3: <serde_v8::ser::StructSerializers as serde::ser::SerializeStruct>::end
   4: <extern "C" fn(A0) .> R as v8::support::CFnFrom<F>>::mapping::c_fn
   5: __ZN2v88internal25FunctionCallbackArguments4CallENS0_15CallHandlerInfoE
   6: __ZN2v88internal12_GLOBAL__N_119HandleApiCallHelperILb0EEENS0_11MaybeHandleINS0_6ObjectEEEPNS0_7IsolateENS0_6HandleINS0_10HeapObjectEEESA_NS8_INS0_20FunctionTemplateInfoEEENS8_IS4_EENS0_16BuiltinArgumentsE
   7: __ZN2v88internal21Builtin_HandleApiCallEiPmPNS0_7IsolateE
note: Some details are omitted, run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=full` for a verbose backtrace.
```

Here is the script that reproduces the error.
```ts
import { gunzip } from "https://deno.land/x/compress@v0.4.1/mod.ts";

const this_path = "0013_part_00.gz";
const curr_obj = await Deno.readFile(this_path);
const curr_file = gunzip(curr_obj);
const curr_string = new TextDecoder().decode(curr_file);
```
The error occurs at `TextDecoder().decode()`.

Here is the link to the file: https://s3.amazonaws.com/openalex/data/authors/updated_date=2022-02-08/0013_part_00.gz

Also, I tried prunning the file to various sizes and found that the error starts occurring for this particular file at more than 221104 lines, or about 512 megabytes.

CC @bradenmacdonald
author: ritec03

body:
Looks like the buffer needed to create the String is larger than the maximum buffer length. As @ritec03 mentioned the max length that works is 512MB. This is also the length in bytes for the (internal) value `v8::String::max_length()`. It is causing a panic! in Rust but without much info on what went wrong. It might be good to make the error message a bit more descriptive.

The limit in Node is also 512MB and given by `buffer.constants.MAX_STRING_LENGTH` ([link](https://nodejs.org/api/buffer.html#buffer_buffer_constants_max_length)). Maybe it would be useful to add something to Deno to retrieve this limit as well?

body:
This proposal is in direct contradiction to what import assertions are supposed to do (assert the content type, not influence evaluation of the specifier) and thus Deno has no plans to support it. 

However your use case is very desirable and there is another proposal that @lucacasonato champions that was presented to solve this use case: https://github.com/tc39/proposal-import-reflection.

author: bartlomieju

body: @bartlomieju I see, interesting.  According to https://deno.land/manual@v1.18.2/typescript/configuration I should be able to set `checkJs` to `false` and that should stop the TS check.  I'm just not sure how to set that through LSP.  The closest I can come up with is with a `deno.json` file, but as you can see I've already tried that. I even tried a `jsconfig.json` file with the same setting, but denols does not seem to be reading either of these files.  Are you aware of a another way to disable this?
author: socketwiz

body:
> @socketwiz the errors you are seeing are coming from Deno's TS compiler not `deno lint`.

Actually it is coming from a dependency analysis of Deno, it doesn't touch the TypeScript compiler.

@socketwiz these are hard errors.  You have Deno enabled on the project, so you are getting errors in the code, because they are errors, pointing to code the Deno cannot run.

If you want Deno to be able to run this code (and therefore not have errors) you would need to use an import map to map the bare specifiers and configure the import map to be used by the language server (the `importMap` option).  If Deno shouldn't be analysing and running this code, you should disable Deno.  The language server supports multi-root workspaces to enable/disable parts of projects, but it specific to the language server integration/client, meaning you would need to have a `enable: false` setting for specific files you don't want errors for.
