author: vicary
lucsoft

body:
I am not supportive of this suggestion, as well as I think there are lots of semantics that would need to be defined to even really consider it.

Reasons I am not supportive:

- Special sigils in specifiers are a problem.  Node.js has implemented `node:` for built-ins and get around the security risk that built-in shadowing causes.  TC39 has debated builtin modules and sigils in specifiers for long time with [no conclusion on how to specify them](https://github.com/tc39/proposal-built-in-modules#appendices).  I am not sure we would just declare it as a special protocol.
- This totally makes the code not portable to other platforms.  Currently to get bare specifiers to work (in non-compat mode), users have to define an import map, and by defining an import map, they effectively keep it isomorphic with browsers and eventually Deno Deploy.  We already have custom resolution logic when it comes to TypeScript type definitions which makes Deno TypeScript code incompatible with `tsc`, adding custom resolution logic baked into the runtime for runtime code seems like a really bad idea to me. No one else would be able to run that code.
- While this might seem like a productive suggestion, it would require users to change or adapt any code.  What happens with the code that is loaded that doesn't include the `npm:` in loading a dependency, how is that resolved?
- How would this work with Deno Deploy?
- I think there are big questions about even if it is viable to turn `--compat` mode on by default, which this suggestion implies.  We do not yet have a clear story on how to mix Deno-first code with Node.js in compat mode, let alone having to deal with all the other eco-system problems that are present in npm/Node.js.
- What about remote dependencies that contain these specifiers, do I suddenly include a 3rd party library and end up with a `node_modules` folder magically appearing?  Something like this would at minimum need to be an opt-in, but I can't see a way of implementing it where it isn't an opt in, unless we turn it off for remote dependencies, but then that seems totally limiting for the feature as a whole.
- The Node.js/npm ecosystem with CommonJS and ESM is [a bit of a dumpster fire to put it kindly](https://gist.github.com/sindresorhus/a39789f98801d908bbc7ff3ecc99d99c).  Shoehorning a Deno CLI only, possibly not forward compatible way to access that eco-systems does not seem logical to me.
- This doesn't address "which" npm registry will be used.  In corporate environments it is often common to use a "private" npm registry to provide allow/block list capability as well as ensure security and compliance.
- Interactions with `deno compile` and `deno bundle` are undefined at the moment.  It is logical that this will cause issues.
- So while aspects of this might be opt-in, a feature like this doesn't go away, is likely to be littered with bugs that the core team would have to address and distracts us from working on other things.

The current widely used solution in Deno to access npm packages, is to use a CDN like esm.sh, esm.run and skypack.dev. Admittedly there are some rough edges and the usability and something we need to work on, but it helps ensure that Deno first and npm code work together without having to bring the whole of the npm/Node.js ecosystem with it.  While esm.sh is great, there are lots of improvements that could be made with something like npm.deno.land that helps ensure a smooth frictionless journey and helps ensure people can move smoothly from a _legacy_ Node.js world to a more modern runtime.

While there are some challenges and limitations with import maps, they are a) a web standard, b) provide transparent and user editable configuration.  Being transparent on resolution and auditable is a big thing versus some sort of opaque mechanism, because we are likely to get it wrong sometimes, and a user editing a bit in an import map (or having better and richer tools to manipulate it) is a lot better than something locked away in the binary you can't influence.
author: kitsonk
elycheikhsmail

body:
@kitsonk 

> Special sigils in specifiers are a problem. Node.js has implemented node: for built-ins and get around the security risk that built-in shadowing causes. TC39 has debated builtin modules and sigils in specifiers for long time with no conclusion on how to specify them. I am not sure we would just declare it as a special protocol.

The `npm:` specifiers are simply another URL. This does not violate any standard.

> This totally makes the code not portable to other platforms. Currently to get bare specifiers to work (in non-compat mode), users have to define an import map, and by defining an import map, they effectively keep it isomorphic with browsers and eventually Deno Deploy. We already have custom resolution logic when it comes to TypeScript type definitions which makes Deno TypeScript code incompatible with tsc, adding custom resolution logic baked into the runtime for runtime code seems like a really bad idea to me. No one else would be able to run that code.

Deno code is not portable to other platforms. Deno code is generally Deno-specific typescript (with extension imports) - so does not work directly in browsers, nor directly with canonical TypeScript.

> While this might seem like a productive suggestion, it would require users to change or adapt any code. What happens with the code that is loaded that doesn't include the npm: in loading a dependency, how is that resolved?

The resolution algorithm will be changed inside of npm packages, so bare specifiers will be resolved via the node resolution algorithm. In normal Deno scripts, bare specifiers will work as they currently do.

> How would this work with Deno Deploy?

Transparently - I see no difficulties here.

> I think there are big questions about even if it is viable to turn --compat mode on by default, which this suggestion implies. We do not yet have a clear story on how to mix Deno-first code with Node.js in compat mode, let alone having to deal with all the other eco-system problems that are present in npm/Node.js.

This suggestion does not imply `--compat` by default.

> What about remote dependencies that contain these specifiers, do I suddenly include a 3rd party library and end up with a node_modules folder magically appearing? Something like this would at minimum need to be an opt-in, but I can't see a way of implementing it where it isn't an opt in, unless we turn it off for remote dependencies, but then that seems totally limiting for the feature as a whole.

Probably remote dependencies with `npm:` specifers will work. When using an `npm:` specifier, the `node_modules` folder will not be created - the package instead is loaded into `DENO_DIR`, similar to how yarn2 works.

> The Node.js/npm ecosystem with CommonJS and ESM is a bit of a dumpster fire to put it kindly. Shoehorning a Deno CLI only, possibly not forward compatible way to access that eco-systems does not seem logical to me.

You should read [Strategy Letter II: Chicken and Egg Problems](https://www.joelonsoftware.com/2000/05/24/strategy-letter-ii-chicken-and-egg-problems/)

> This doesn't address "which" npm registry will be used. In corporate environments it is often common to use a "private" npm registry to provide allow/block list capability as well as ensure security and compliance.

It would probably use the default registry and maybe we'd add a `NPM_REGISTRY` env var that could be set.

> Interactions with deno compile and deno bundle are undefined at the moment. It is logical that this will cause issues.

I'm confident they can be made to work.

> So while aspects of this might be opt-in, a feature like this doesn't go away, is likely to be littered with bugs that the core team would have to address and distracts us from working on other things.

It will not be littered with bugs - this is a straightforward feature. Most of the infrastructure is already in place.
author: ry

body:
> Deno code is not portable to other platforms. Deno code is generally Deno-specific typescript (with extension imports) - so does not work directly in browsers, nor directly with canonical TypeScript.

For dotland there is a [requested feature](https://github.com/denoland/website_feedback/issues/19) and [PR](https://github.com/denoland/dotland/pull/1902) to transpile ts to js, so browsers can consume /x/ modules. This suggestion would quite hinder that (on top of the deno ns, but thats another topic)
author: crowlKats

body:
I like this proposal better than the current `--compat` mode. This blends Deno ecosystem and npm ecosystem in a more reasonable way.

This doesn't expose `fs` or `http` Node.js modules to Deno-first module. So we can still write Deno code as it is, but also optionally use any npm modules. So this addresses the most frequent complaints about Deno (unavailability of npm) very elegantly in my view.

@lucsoft 
> Why have an built-in import-map?

This proposal suggests we switch the runtime to what the current `--compat` mode provides for npm modules. That means expressions like `require("fs")` works only inside of npm modules (npm modules works in a simulated node.js context)
author: kt3k

body:
> For dotland there is a [requested feature](https://github.com/denoland/website_feedback/issues/19) and [PR](https://github.com/denoland/dotland/pull/1902) to transpile ts to js, so browsers can consume /x/ modules. This suggestion would quite hinder that (on top of the deno ns, but thats another topic)

@crowlKats I'm a huge fan of that proposal so I'm curious why this proposal would hinder it? I was under the impression that the all the node.js compatibility polyfills are in ts, so it would be able to run in browser after being compiled to js (assuming the npm module doesn't e.g. use file system / native addons / etc., but same can be said of Deno modules)? I.e. I'd have thought that the node stuff could essentially be "browserified". I'm probably misunderstanding something here.
author: josephrocca
lucsoft
kt3k

body:
> This proposal suggests we switch the runtime to what the current --compat mode provides for npm modules. That means expressions like require("fs") works only inside of npm modules (npm modules works in a simulated node.js context)

@kt3k Why not just make npm accessible via something like `https://deno.land/npm/package-name`? That way the complexity is added to the building and serving of the Deno-compatible npm packages instead of adding extra complexity and browser-incompatibility to the Deno runtime itself.

Or is it not possible to solve this via some sort of transpilation/shimming of npm packages? That seems unlikely, but I might be misunderstanding something. If it *is* possible, then the cost of this `npm:` idea (specifically RE reducing browser compatibility) seems to far outweigh the benefits.

I think it'd be really bad idea to hurt browser compatibility here if there were some other viable way to get npm packages working in Deno.
author: josephrocca

body:
> Why not just make npm accessible via something like https://deno.land/npm/package-name

We already have that its esm.sh/package-name
author: lucsoft

body:
> > Why have an built-in import-map?
> > ```json
> > {
> >     "imports": {
> >         "npm:": "https://unpkg.com/"
> >     }
> > }
> > ```
> > 
> > 
> >     
> >       
> >     
> > 
> >       
> >     
> > 
> >     
> >   
> > this already solves the problem?
> 
> But how --compat will know this is code writen for node ? I suggest to have convention about npm import file config like deno.json convention

By adding a query parameter of [?module](https://unpkg.com/#query-params) to unpkg.com URLs, any bare import specifiers will automatically be expanded to absolute URLs, and it will server ES6 modules of all packages that have that specify a "module" in their package.json.

This way there doesn't need to be any extra configuration like a package.json or any non-web compatible changes to deno.

If a package isn't offereing an ES6 compatible version, usually there is already an issue on their github asking for it and it's only a matter of time.

I've been using this a lot, and it works well for most packages but requires suffixing all imported modules with ```?module``` which is a little annoying.

Also, trying to import```npm:x.js?module``` with 
```json
{ "imports": { "npm:": "https://unpkg.com/" } }
```
on deno version 1.21.2
returns an error for me:
```
error: Unsupported scheme "npm" for module "npm:x.js". Supported schemes: [
    "data",
    "blob",
    "file",
    "http",
    "https",
]
```

but importing ```npm/x.js?module``` with
```json
{ "imports": { "npm/": "https://unpkg.com/" } }
```
works fine.

If you don't want to suffix all of your imports with "?module" you have to include a separate mapping for every single package you want from npm/unpkg.com specifying the entire URL, so if you just want to ```import "npm/foo.js```, You need 

```json
{ "imports": { "npm/foo.js": "https://unpkg.com/foo/bax/bar/foo.js?module" } }
```

It would be nice if importmaps passed on query parameters to redirects/mappings like

```json
{ "imports": { "npm/": "https://unpkg.com/?module" } }
```
I saw some discussion about this on [WICG/import-maps](https://github.com/WICG/import-maps/issues/173#issue-480997346) but currently trying this results in 

```
Import map diagnostics:
  - Invalid target address "https://unpkg.com/?module" for package specifier "npm/". Package address targets must end with "/".
error: Blocked by null entry for ""npm/""
```

[esm.sh](https://esm.sh/) works in a similar way, where the returned resource can be changed depending on the  query parameter passed to it but with a lot more options.
author: ryanwhite04

body:
>> Why not just make npm accessible via something like https://deno.land/npm/package-name

> We already have that its esm.sh/package-name

@lucsoft I think this issue is about trying to avoid the need for the [`--compat` flag](https://deno.land/manual@v1.17.0/npm_nodejs/compatibility_mode) rather than just transforming the imports. So the "shim" that I mentioned would need to be doing *Deno-specific* stuff, and hence esm.sh/unpkg.com/etc. wouldn't be enough. It needs to set Node globals, make built-in modules like `fs` importable, etc.

So, naively (not suggesting as actual solution), `https://deno.land/npm/` would need to add something like `Deno.enableNodeCompatibility()` to the top of the script at the very least (as well as the import transformation stuff).
author: josephrocca

body:
With regards to whether or not this requires `--compat`, implies `--compat` by default, or as described by @kt3k only applies it to npm modules; note that Node has globals like `Buffer` as well. Is the suggested behaviour to put these in the global scope for the whole program whenever there is an `npm:` module in the graph? If so, it's a bit strange for that not to require a flag even for transitive `npm:` dependencies.

I think we should start by allowing `"compat": true` in the config file, and evaluate if it's too much burden to gate this feature by.

body:
@kitsonk @ry @lucacasonato @crowlKats Can I ask a couple of questions about the direction that's being taken here according to the latest blog post? If so:

1. With the chosen approach is there a way to create code that uses `npm:` modules, but works in the browser and in Deno? I'm hoping this is possible with import maps that change the "npm:" part to the unpkg base url or something like that? But then how would that work for dependencies (or dependencies of dependencies) that use `npm:`? I.e. how is browser-incompatibleness prevented from "infecting" all descendent modules? There are many npm modules that work fine in the browser, but (with my limited understanding of the details here) it seems like `npm:` would needlessly make dependent modules browser-incompatible?
2. What is the technical reason that something like `https://deno.land/npm/express@6` can't be used instead, where the deno.land cached version has been "fixed" to work in Deno (with some special init code added to the top of the script) only if it can't be "browserified"? That would seemingly maximise browser compatibility by only introducing Deno-specific/Node-specific stuff where it's actually needed.

My main worry here is that people will start using `npm:` very liberally, even when it's not necessary (i.e. they could have used an `unpkg.com` URL, for example), and so a bunch of packages (and, critically, all descendent packages) would be unnecessarily browser-incompatible, which goes against my main reason for using Deno in the first place, and why I'm so excited about it as a new JS runtime.

Sorry if these questions have been answered already in some other thread that I've missed. Thanks!
author: josephrocca

body:
Presumably a problem with the `npm:` idea is you would need to have a `NODE_ENV` environment variable populated in the Deno runtime environment set to `development` or `production` if you are importing things like `npm:react-dom/server`. With a CDN URL you can specify what dev or prod version of the module to load using a query string parameter like `?dev`:

```
https://esm.sh/react-dom@18.2.0/server?dev
```

React requires `NODE_ENV` to be `production` to opt-in to the production logic, or it falls back to the dev logic:

https://unpkg.com/react-dom@18.2.0/server.node.js

Other packages have it inverted, where prod logic is the default.

Either way, `npm:` approach would result in both dev and prod logic being downloaded, cached, and parsed in some situations like this:

https://unpkg.com/react-dom@18.2.0/client.js

That module is meant for client use, but it shows how packages use such logic with inline code instead of nested `require` calls.

Ideally over time npm packages are published as pure ESM, with dev and prod builds that can be selected via import maps and CDN's that don't do any build steps at all but just statically host the published files (like unpkg.com).

Imagined dev import map:

```json
{
  "imports": {
    "react/": "https://esm.sh/react@19.0.0/dev/",
    "react-dom/": "https://unpkg.com/react-dom@19.0.0/dev/",
  }
}
```

Imagined prod import map:

```json
{
  "imports": {
    "react/": "https://esm.sh/react@19.0.0/",
    "react-dom/": "https://unpkg.com/react-dom@19.0.0/",
  }
}
```

Imagined imports:

```js
import createElement from "react/createElement.mjs";
import hydrateRoot from "react-dom/hydrateRoot.mjs";
```

There would be no main index modules to map, if packages had [optimal JavaScript module design](https://jaydenseric.com/blog/optimal-javascript-module-design). No `/server/` or `/client/` subpaths.

Are projects like React so ossified, untouchable, and aloof that we would rather build non-standard features into JavaScript runtimes than simply fix the module format and structure of their packages so they align to web standards, allowing them to be used efficiently and universally with the tools we have today in Node.js, Deno, and browsers?

A JavaScript runtime should align to web standards, and not start cramming in proprietary features that will lead to a fractured ecosystem based on non web standard features that endorse arbitrary for-profit corporations. How would we feel if Chrome added support for an `npm:` URL scheme for JavaScript imports? It would rightfully get shot down in flames and never get past proposal stage.

We already have a solution for using legacy CJS npm packages in Deno and browsers until they clean up their act and publish ESM: CDNs like esm.sh. Why don't we just fix up the remaining bugs such CDNs have, and work with that? The engineering work to fix the CDN bugs is surely a lot less/cheaper than implementing the `npm:` scheme. Ideally we would have a highly reliable CDN maintained by Deno, e.g. `https://deno.land/npm/react-dom@18.2.0/server?dev`; I wouldn't mind paying for such a CDN.

As other commenters have pointed out, the `npm:` approach won't work in browsers, so for legacy CJS npm packages like `react` we would have to find a CDN that provides them in ESM format for the browser runtime anyway. Almost all of my Deno projects are isomorphic, i.e. dependencies are used in both server and browser environments.
author: jaydenseric

body:
@josephrocca there are certain npm packages that can't work in the browser, but will eventually work in Deno.

For packages that are possible to transpile for the browser, the npm specifiers can be mapped to a transpile server like esm.sh. `deno info --json` will provide enough information that it will be possible to automatically generate an import map, that maps `npm:<package-name>@<version-requirement>` style imports, to import map entries like `"npm:<package-name>@<version-requirement>": "https://esm.sh/<package-name>@<resolved-version>?deps=<resolved-deps-list-goes-here>"`. There's a proof of concept for this idea working in fresh [here](https://github.com/dsherret/fresh/commit/e7168143a220cd5e68117bd8596cd9f0ca6e76c6).

So, these specifiers provide a way for dependencies to state their version requirements, rather than depend on a single explicit version, then the runtime can do the work of automatically de-duplicating dependencies. For the browser, although this requires an extra step by using an import map, this can be auto-generated and you get the added benefit of probably less code delivered to the user because dependencies have started expressing version requirements instead.

There are several technical reasons why a transpile server like esm.sh doesn't always work. First, it's not always possible to convert cjs to esm. There can be stuff like synchronous deferred requires, or we've seen code that's non-analyzable using requires in an `eval` call. Additionally, there are sometimes data files in a package that are read synchronously and make including them in the graph difficult or cause some overhead. Sometimes there's also binary files in packages that need a way to be run synchronously, or with eventual support for Node API there could be extra files it uses in the package that would be impossible to statically analyze for.
author: dsherret

body:
@dsherret Thanks for your reply! So am I correct in understanding that library/package authors would ideally use unpkg.com/esm.sh/etc whenever possible (instead of `npm:`) so their package is automatically compatible with the browser without a build step?

If so, I'm wondering: Would it somehow be possible to give a warning if `npm:` is used when it's not actually necessary (i.e. compat mode isn't required for that particular npm package), or is there at least some other way to try to push authors away from making a module accidentally-incompatible-for-no-good-reason with build-step-less direct/simple `import` in the browser?

Or can we just solve this "browser-incompatible-for-no-good-reason" case with on-the-fly (and cached, of course) pre-compilation/transpilation when a `deno.land/x` module is downloaded to a browser, [as is going to be done for `.ts` files anyway](https://github.com/denoland/website_feedback/issues/19)?

If that's the case, then that's somewhat relieving.

Thanks again for your reply!
author: josephrocca

body:
If you have an import map that maps npm specifiers to something like unpkg, then this is not a build step. If a package ships with typescript, then that's browser incompatible and actually requires a build step—we don't have a warning for that so I don't think we would give a warning for this (though we'll discuss it internally).

> Or can we just solve this "browser-incompatible-for-no-good-reason" case with on-the-fly (and cached, of course) pre-compilation/transpilation when a deno.land/x module is downloaded to a browser, https://github.com/denoland/website_feedback/issues/19?

Yes, I think this is something that could be better solved by registries where if you specify you want a browser compatible module, then it will convert npm specifiers to an esm package, handle deduplication for you, and convert ts to js.
author: dsherret

body:
Similar ugly UI/UX occurs whenever "node/util.ts" is imported.

Because of a combination of requirements/decisions, "node/util.ts" can no longer be imported without an undesired prompt, unless `--no-prompt` is used or permissions are, by default, just granted.

```shell
$ deno run "https://deno.land/std@0.151.0/node/util.ts"
⚠️  ️Deno requests env access to "NODE_DEBUG". Run again with --allow-env to bypass this prompt.
   Allow? [y/n (y = yes allow, n = no deny)]
```

Choosing allow or deny doesn't make a difference in the success of the import (just later util debug functionality), but this is a UI/UX wart which makes it impossible to present a more user-friendly UI/UX (as opposed to the now default dev-centered UI/UX).

Before https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/commit/8b34f7363501d17b059ab65575b985902ef190ba was merged, a quiet permission query was used, avoiding the prompt. But, because the query was async, it led to unexpected order of execution, with module being imported after other synchronous modules (see https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/issues/2097). And so, the code was changed to a `try ... catch` which now has the undesired behavior.

And there's no way that I can find to even test to make sure imported code won't prompt. So, just as the change from 'std@0.134.0' to 'std@0.135.0' unexpectedly added this prompt, any dependency change may add unexpected additional prompts which are confusing to users.

A fix would be returning to query-gating all code executed during import which requires permissions. But, to avoid the unexpected order of execution of imported modules, the query-gate would have to be synchronous.

author: asyncanup

body:
Agreed, this would be a great thing to have.  I am also importing local modules over HTTP (for the purposes of a plugin/module-federation-type architecture) and would really like to have these modules not cached.

You can _sort of_ work around caching imports by adding a query param to your dynamic import that contains a random number (saw this on a GitHub issue somewhere but forget where, sorry for no credit):
```TypeScript
const location = new URL('http://localhost:8080/myRemoteModule.ts');
location.searchParams.append('denoAntiCache', Math.random().toString());
```

The issue is that any imports required by the module that you just loaded over the network can still be served from Deno's cache...  So the above workaround is only useful for modules that have no dependencies.

It would completely solve the issue for me if Deno's caching behavior could be controlled by the presence of the [`Cache-Control`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Cache-Control) header.

Ideally, I could have the server serving my local modules set `Cache-Control: no-cache` on all its responses.  This would remove the need for me to use the query param workaround and also prevent caching the items that I don't directly `import()`.

body:
Hello @lucacasonato, is this issue still available for grabs? Thanks.

I am unsure about how to proceed though. Basically, I would like, if possible, have an idea about how different the `op_encoding_decode_single` op from the `op_encoding_new_decoder` and `op_encoding_decode` needs to be. Do we have to merge the two ops into a single one?
author: mehdiakiki

body: @randomicon00 Yup, @juank1809 never opened a PR.
author: lucacasonato

body:
> I am unsure about how to proceed though. Basically, I would like, if possible, have an idea about how different the `op_encoding_decode_single` op from the `op_encoding_new_decoder` and `op_encoding_decode` needs to be. Do we have to merge the two ops into a single one?

There is some amount of overhead when you call into an op and return from it. In the streaming case, having three ops (including the call to `core.close`) is justified because at most two of them will run in a single call to `TextDecoder.prototype.decode`, and because they perform logically distinct operations. But for the non-streaming case, you have all three op calls, and `op_encoding_new_decoder` and `core.close` just set up and tear down a resource that is only used for the `op_encoding_decode` call. The idea is to incorporate the decoder creation and the actual decoding in a single op, without storing the decoder as a resource.

For the record, when you've got something implemented, be sure to test it with the [web platform tests](https://deno.land/manual@v1.20.3/contributing/web_platform_tests). Since there are _a lot_ of WPT tests, you probably want to run just the encoding-related ones, which you can do with `./tools/wpt.ts run -- encoding`.

author: andreubotella

body:
It looks like all of those failing tests use `{stream: true}` – either directly, or indirectly through `TextDecoderStream`. Please make sure that you only call into `op_encoding_decode_single` when `options.stream` is falsy and `this.#rid` is null.

The op implementation looks good. As you point out yourself, dropping the decoder in Rust by having `decoder` go out of scope is fine. The reason `core.close()` is needed is because the `Decoder` instance is a Rust value, and it can't be returned into JS. Rather than that, what `op_encoding_new_decoder` does is store the `Decoder` together with the fatal state in a resource table managed by Deno's runtime, and it returns an ID to that resource to JS. `core.close()` is needed to remove that resource from the table. This new op doesn't store anything in the resource table, so there's nothing extra to clean up.

> Should I give it a try and make a pull request so you can have a look?

Usually reviews of an implementation happen in pull requests, not in the associated issue. Just mark the PR as a draft when you submit it.

author: lucacasonato

body:
In #13769 we are introducing automatic compression of static bodies.  @lucacasonato is suggesting that we may want to give a user an ability to wholesale disable this.  Currently, if `Cache-Control` is set to `no-transform`, it gets disabled.  In the use case above though, it is likely it would disable compression in nginx.

That being said, I can't personally see why it would be desirable for nginx to do the compression _on the fly_, it would require sending the uncompressed body over the wire to the nginx server to potentially do the same compression that would have been done in Deno.  Yes, nginx could potentially handle other cases that Deno doesn't, but Deno doing doing compression in certain situations faster and more efficiently than is technically possible with nginx would negate the ability for nginx to do it in other situations.

The biggest "challenge" might be we set the `Vary` header irrespective of if we attempt to compress or not, and that might "interfere" with downstream caches, but actually it does indicate that the CLI did consider the `Accept-Encoding` header as part of the response.
author: kitsonk

body:
> That being said, I can't personally see why it would be desirable for nginx to do the compression on the fly, it would require sending the uncompressed body over the wire to the nginx server to potentially do the same compression that would have been done in Deno. Yes, nginx could potentially handle other cases that Deno doesn't, but Deno doing doing compression in certain situations faster and more efficiently than is technically possible with nginx would negate the ability for nginx to do it in other situations.

I think the theme is that many people don't put servers in front of "the internet" since there are many cross-cutting concerns that _may_ require inspecting the request body in their cluster. These can be instrumentation/analytics/telemetry through a proxy or just plain old "load balancing". It's very common to terminate in HTTP/1.1 and leave nginx do the quic/http2.

Leaving it to the entry (nginx or whatever) or tools in the middle (like cloudflare) gives you the ability to not care about best practices and having them always compress "the best way".

body:
Spent half a day (i'm a 1x dev) trying to debug a problem where my reverse proxy was streaming back only a small part of the response. `Cache-Control: no-transform` does fix it but I don't think I can use that solution because I have multiple 'layers' downstream that could get messed up by it. This might be a bug with the proxy, but I figured that I'd post a comment here since it's maybe an example of the need to have control here, especially when you don't have much control of what happens in the layers that are downstream of the Deno server.

It's probably irrelevant, since IIUC the compression happens at the CLI level, but just in case: It's a very simple server that uses `std@0.189.0/http/server.ts`.

**Edit**: In case it's helpful for others who also found this issue via Google, in my particular case I was able to remove the `accept-encoding: gzip,deflate` header from the requests that were hitting the Deno server, which solved the problem.

Would it be best to have the `op_base64_decode` and `_encode` do all the work on Rust side?

ie.
`op_base64_decode` would return a `String` so that JS side doesn't have to do all the `Uint8Array` to `Array` to `string` mapping and joining.

`op_base64_encode` would take a `String`, check it for invalid characters and return an error if necessary (can ops directly return-throw a `DomException`? I see a lot of `DomExceptionSomething` in Rust code so I presume it's okay), convert to u8 slice and pass to `base64::encode_config`?

Deno (`main`) now outperforms Node following PRs I've landed since Friday.
We're over 4x faster on long strings, ~10% faster on short strings.

### Node
```
❯ node -v; node ./cli/bench/deno_common.js
v17.6.0
b64_rt_long:         	n = 100, dt = 2.755s, r = 36/s, t = 27549999ns/op
b64_rt_short:        	n = 1000000, dt = 0.507s, r = 1972387/s, t = 507ns/op
```

### Deno (main 788553bf2f652311a254ee03ef5f70cbf3d6003f)

```
❯ ./target/release/deno run --unstable -A ./cli/bench/deno_common.js
b64_rt_long:         	n = 100, dt = 0.626s, r = 160/s, t = 6260000ns/op
b64_rt_short:        	n = 1000000, dt = 0.451s, r = 2217295/s, t = 451ns/op
```

### Conclusions

I would consider this fixed for now. There are further improvements in upcoming deno PRs, I've also submitted 2 PRs upstream to [rust-base64](https://github.com/marshallpierce/rust-base64): https://github.com/marshallpierce/rust-base64/pull/180 and https://github.com/marshallpierce/rust-base64/pull/179

body:
Closing this as not planned.

Response body compression still runs on the main thread, but it's done incrementally per-chunk inside the response stream's poll loop (`GZipResponseStream`/`BrotliResponseStream` in `ext/http/response_body.rs`), not as a single large blocking operation. In the ~4 years since this was filed there's been no evidence that this is a real bottleneck, and moving per-chunk compression to a blocking thread pool would add handoff and synchronization overhead per chunk that could easily outweigh any benefit for typical streaming workloads.

If someone hits a concrete CPU-bound case where compression measurably starves the event loop, please open a fresh issue with a benchmark/repro and we can revisit it with data. Thanks!

author: tristan-f-r

body:
Closing this as out of scope. WebHID is fundamentally a browser API: the access model is built around a user-gesture-gated device picker (`navigator.hid.requestDevice()`), which has no meaningful analog in a CLI/server runtime. It is also a Chromium-only WICG draft that Firefox and WebKit have declined over security and fingerprinting concerns, so it does not fit Deno's strategy of tracking broadly-adopted web standards.

Raw HID access is better served at a different layer in Deno, either through FFI (e.g. wrapping `node-hid`) or a focused native API, rather than by shipping a sandbox-shaped Web API designed to restrict what a hostile web page can do.

If there is concrete demand for low-level HID device access, please open a new issue framed around that capability rather than the WebHID spec.

body:
We should consider if we want the lsp to run the tasks, in the same process.  We do "caching" and will be doing it for the testing API, but it would require some custom APIs to run the tasks and deal with the output (like writing to stdout or reading from stdin would interfere with communication of the client/server).

Wouldn't it be better to add the feature to the clients directly.  Each client has a concept of tasks, and for example for vscode_deno, there is a task provider API which allows tasks to be expressed and made available in the editor.  The biggest thing would be the "auto discovery" of the config file.  We could either mirror the logic in the extension or we could have the lsp notify the client of the discovered config file if any.

Having that one custom notification is a lot better than a handful of requests/responses and notifications for managing it.

Following it this way, the extensions would be responsible for launching and managing the `deno task` as a subprocess.  The API in vscode is pretty rich.  It already has integrations to auto-discover npm scripts, and would be a good pattern for how to deal with Deno tasks.

body:
This is not a bug with calling `WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming` with a URL, since calling it with `await` properly throws an exception:

```
> await WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming(new URL("https://google.com"))
Uncaught TypeError: Failed to call 'WebAssembly.compileStreaming': Argument 1 is not of type Response.
    at makeException (deno:ext/webidl/00_webidl.js:79:12)
    at Array.Response (deno:ext/webidl/00_webidl.js:866:15)
    at deno:ext/fetch/26_fetch.js:514:50
```

It instead seems to be a weird interaction of Deno's way of handling `WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming` with the rest of the event loop. This also happens if you pass a `Response.error()`, but oddly enough, not if you pass the promise that results from calling `fetch` with a nonexistent URL.
author: andreubotella

body:
When an exception is thrown during the processing of streaming wasm, `op_wasm_streaming_abort` is called. This op calls into V8, which _immediately_ rejects the promise returned from `WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming` with the passed exception, and immediately calls into the promise rejection handler if needed. But since `op_wasm_streaming_abort` is an op, calling it borrows the isolate's `JsRuntimeState`, which means it is borrowed when V8 calls into `promise_reject_callback`, which tries to borrow it again.

The panic doesn't happen if you call `WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming` with a promise that will reject, because apparently V8 itself takes care of awaiting for the argument if it is a promise, and handling it if it rejects. In that case, Deno's `handleWasmStreaming` function never gets called.

One way to fix this would be making `op_wasm_streaming_abort` a binding again, and making extra sure that `v8::WasmStreaming::abort` is only called after the `JsRuntimeState` borrow is dropped.
author: andreubotella

author: nayeemrmn

body:
> We may need to continue running `"auto"` when `--compat` is used.

Actually this makes perfect sense, the _execution_ semantics we have already align with using `"force"` without `--compat` and using `"auto"` with `--compat`. Those sources in the repro of #9593 _are_ executed as scripts with `--compat` is used.

(p.s. I've been using `"auto"` interchangeably with `"legacy"`, whatever the default is or will be)
author: nayeemrmn

body:
Thinking more,
- Without `--compat` we should invariably use `"moduleDetection": "force"`.
- With `--compat` we should use `"moduleDetection": "auto"` by default but allow it to be configured by a user tsconfig.
  - This is because `--compat` allows them to toggle the runtime module detection with `package.json:"type"` and they need access to the TS configuration to match their choice.
author: nayeemrmn

body: Disregard previous comments. We should just use `"moduleDetection": "force"` all the way, I misunderstood that modules refer to both esm and cjs, as opposed to old-fashioned browser scripts.

body:
I've been looking into this to support the Conda packages (conda-forge/deno-feedstock#68), and have successfully cross-built Deno for Linux aarch64 (the Conda CI is currently failing, but I have working local builds).

The things I see needing to be done (or nice to have) here:

- [ ] Fix feature defaults to enable a build without unconditionally activating `include_js_files_for_snapshotting` on dependencies [**mandatory**, the patch in conda-forge does this crudely]
- [ ] Correct `cli/ops/mod.rs` to include source code when snapshotting is disabled [**mandatory**, patch in conda-forge does this]
- [ ] Revisit feature flags for selecting or disabling snapshotting, perhaps to make snapshotting behind a feature flag that is enabled by default [patch in conda-forge has vv. incomplete start on this]
- [ ] Modularize `cli/build.rs` and gate more things behind feature flags, so it doesn't depend on Deno & the runtime when it isn't snapshotting (otherwise cross-builds compile Deno twice, once for the build arch to support the snapshots that aren't created, and again for the target arch)

Relatedly: one of the challenges is on the things behind `include_js_files_for_snapshotting`, which causes binaries to include files by reference instead of including their source code. Would it be worth considering always including the sources? A rough count finds 18M of JavaScript & TypeScript source in the `runtime`, `cli`, and `ext` trees; that would be a substantial add. Brotli-compressing the whole bundle brings it down to 2.2M while separate Deflate compression yields a 4.3M zip, suggesting compressed versions of the source code could be unconditionally included with a modest increase in binary size.
author: mdekstrand

body: A further hitch has arisen: with Deno 1.39.0, snapshotting is more deeply baked into Deno's working, so the patches I currently have to work without snapshotting cause `Deno.build` to be incorrectly populated.
author: mdekstrand

body:
`Deno.build` fields are being populated during snapshot time (improving startup time perf):

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/cdbf902499dc62a4fb9f8cbf2a47a7b446623929/runtime/ops/bootstrap.rs#L32-L46

which means its currently not possible to compile Deno CLI without snapshots.

---

Discussed this offline and will be working towards aarch64 linux builds with snapshots.

It would be useful for packagers, however, to support building the Deno CLI without snapshots — packaging environments often require cross-builds. The conda-forge CI infrastructure, for example, only supports native builds for x86-64 (I co-maintain the package there).

Regarding alternatives, there appears to be one in development: https://deno.land/x/deno_mongo_schema@v0.8.3

Mongoose, as far as I know, depends on Node's `crypto` module and although Deno has a Node compatibility mode, the support for Node's `crypto` is stated as 'partly' at this time: https://deno.land/manual@v1.20.1/node/compatibility_mode#node-built-in-modules. So it may not be possible to have Mongoose fully functional in Deno, it might require a port that rewrites it to use the WebCrypto API for example.

body:
This is resolved. Terminating a worker no longer spawns a thread.

`WebWorkerHandle::terminate()` (`runtime/web_worker.rs`) now just sets a `termination_signal` `AtomicBool` and wakes the worker through an `AtomicWaker`; it does not block the calling thread and spawns nothing. The worker self-terminates from its own event loop: `poll_event_loop` calls `terminate_if_needed()`, which observes the signal and calls `isolate_handle.terminate_execution()`. No dedicated thread, no 2s wait, and no reference to the #12658 workaround remains in the termination path.

This came in via #12831 ("don't immediately kill the isolate") and #13941 ("don't block the current thread") - the latter being the precondition this issue named. The final design differs from the OpState-set-with-`Drop` sketch above, but the goal ("don't spawn a thread when terminating a worker") is met.

I also verified the case the thread originally guarded against - a worker stuck in an infinite synchronous JS loop (`while (true) {}`). Terminating it works cleanly: the parent continues and the process exits 0, no hang. Closing as resolved.

body:
One consideration in this is that, irrespective of cache headers, the `DENO_DIR/deps` (remote modules cache) is treated as immutable, meaning that the headers (and therefore modules) are only ever retrieved once, unless `--reload` is specified.  When reload is specified, an `ETag` header is sent (if received on the original request) and not modified is handled.

The problem with the first part of this suggestion is that would make such "warning" headers not easy to implement without changing behaviour in a significantly breaking way. @lucacasonato and I discussed this when we started respecting caching headers (to a degree) with the registry completions, but left alone the deps cache.
author: kitsonk

body:
@kitsonk  got ya. this would be fixed by adding the cli params in a way similar to this ,right? (igonre the exact wording for now)


`--reload --audit-only`
 
`--reload --show-outdated-only`

--reload would trigger the fetch dependencies logic and the other flags could disable the actual overwriting of dependencies and instead refetch only the headers with a HEAD


the notion of etag is also a good point. it depends if we are using strong or weak etags: 

`If the conditional GET used a strong cache validator (see [RFC 2068], section 13.3.3), the response SHOULD NOT include other entity-headers. Otherwise (i.e., the conditional GET used a weak validator), the response MUST NOT include other entity-headers; this prevents inconsistencies between cached entity-bodies and updated headers.`

imho it would be totally fine to include the vulnerability warnings and hints for updated resources in a "strong cache validation request". If we are using weak etags, its best not to break RFC and it would be a bit trickier, but maybe switching to strong etags would then be the lesser evil?
author: lucidNTR
lucidNTR

body:
Both goals here are now covered by dedicated commands:

- **Outdated / "update all"** — `deno outdated` lists outdated dependencies, and `deno outdated --update` (a.k.a. `deno update`) bulk-updates them (`--latest` to ignore semver, plus package filters).
- **Known vulnerabilities** — `deno audit` checks installed dependencies against the registry / socket.dev advisory database, with `--level`, `--ignore <CVE>`, and `--fix` to auto-upgrade.

The specific mechanism proposed here (HTTP `Link` header hints on remote module downloads plus HEAD revalidation) wasn't adopted: Deno's dependency model moved to JSR + npm + import maps, where version and advisory metadata come from the registries directly, which also sidesteps the immutable deps-cache concern raised earlier in the thread. Since the underlying goals (surfacing newer versions and known vulnerabilities, and bulk-updating) are now delivered, closing as resolved. Thanks for the thoughtful proposal.
author: bartlomieju

body:
There's no built-in functionality for this in `deno_core`, but this can be achieved by spawning a separate thread that acts as a "timeout watchdog". 

You can obtain a thread safe handle to the V8 isolate that allows you to terminate execution using:
```rust
let thread_safe_handle = js_runtime.v8_isolate().thread_safe_handle();
```

Then, if executing a script takes too much time, you can "kill" the isolate with:
```rust
thread_safe_handle.terminate_execution();
```

body:
In the `.then` based code, you are creating an infinite amount of promises by calling `reader.read` inside of an infinite loop. You never pause to let the networking do anything, you just create a promise, then jump back to the start of the loop, then create another promise etc.

The `async/await` based code not working is an actual bug in Deno. cc @crowlKats

body:
> ```ts
> export class WebSocketStream {
> ```

One important feature of the WebSocketStream is the backpressure handling, and this simple wrapper does not support backpressure at all. It is possible to implement backpressure on the outgoing stream via `WebSocket: bufferedAmount`, but not possible on the incoming stream. Missing the backpressure support can overwhelm the receiver and potentially cause out-of-memory.

I'd expect import maps work to root modules, but the specs says the opposite: https://github.com/WICG/import-maps#remapping-doesnt-work-for-script (I guess the `<script>` elements are the equivalent for browsers).

The spec also says that inline scripts, like `<script type="module">import "./hello-world.js";</script>` should use import maps to resolve (in Chrome it works), and this is the equivalent to `deno eval --import-map=import_map.json "import './hello-world.js'"` that doesn't work in Deno.

body:
I had a proposal in mind where `deno task foo/bar.sh`, if it couldn't find a task named `"foo/bar.sh"`, would next try to resolve it as a path and read that file as a script. That way it could be used to run unabashed `.sh` scripts on Windows. I figured there could be a `deno task -c "ls | wc -l"` as well. I still think such a design would be really cool since pretty much the whole motivation for `deno task` can be traced back to not being able to run `.sh` scripts on Windows.

However, IMO `deno_task_shell` needs feature-parity with `/bin/dash` at a minimum to make this desirable and it seems a lot of that has been deemed out of scope. The thought is that more complex scripts should be written as JS/TS. Ref [brief discord convo](https://discord.com/channels/684898665143206084/684911491035430919/954223383204216852).
author: nayeemrmn

body:
This would be a hard sell because we have a scripting language in the binary already for running JavaScript and TypeScript files (also a lot of work). In the case of `deno task`, the purpose is to provide an easy way to alias commands you run on the command line—provide as close to a 1-1 mapping between what you execute on the command line and what you want to save for others to execute.

See https://github.com/denoland/deno_task_shell/issues/23 for details on executing a ts or js file with a shebang cross platform with deno task.

I think what would be neat is for someone to develop a Deno module that reduces the verbosity of doing `sh`-like stuff in JS/TS (ex. piping between processes).
author: dsherret

body:
> we have a scripting language in the binary already for running JavaScript and TypeScript files

> In the case of `deno task`, the purpose is to provide an easy way to alias commands you run on the command line—provide as close to a 1-1 mapping between what you execute on the command line and what you want to save for others to execute.

> I think what would be neat is for someone to develop a Deno module that reduces the verbosity of doing `sh`-like stuff in JS/TS (ex. piping between processes).

That's how the conversation has always gone, we are repelled by implementing a proper `sh` compat layer because we already have a scripting language, but then gravitate to that anyway in a roundabout, proprietary or incomplete way, of course because JS/TS bindings are not good at this.

The only real problem users had with "just use a `.sh` file" is Windows compat. Therefore the appeal to me of things like Git Bash and this proposal is that we bridge that incompatibility in a no-fuss way, unix users can pretend the feature doesn't exist and we pave the way forward for Windows to eventually be able run `.sh` scripts out of the box without creating unnecessary protocols.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
It's a massive problem to get shell scripts working out of the box on Windows and to do this we would have to add compatibility for many more commands. For example, neither `ls` or `wc` used in the example above work on Windows. This might be something better suited for a separate tool. Then if someone would like, they could execute this tool by adding an alias for it in the "tasks" in the config file, then execute it with `deno task <task-name>`.

> That's how the conversation has always gone, we are repelled by implementing a proper sh compat layer because we already have a scripting language, but then gravitate to that anyway in a roundabout, proprietary or incomplete way, of course because JS/TS bindings are not good at this.

Again, `deno task` is fundamentally a command aliasing/bookmarking system and the easiest way to alias commands is to try to have a 1-1 mapping. It's not intended to be a full shell or have all the features of one.
author: dsherret

body:
> we would have to add compatibility for many more commands. For example, neither `ls` or `wc` used in the example above work on Windows

Of course it would always be a subset, the appeal is being _compatible_ with the unix shell ecosystem. This shouldn't only be brought up as an issue when it is suggested to instead put task code in a file that can _also_ be run by a unix shell. Whether this way or that way, most of the time that code will be a one liner `deno` command that will just work. (Though as mentioned earlier I think it would at least need syntax parity with posix shell so we're dealing with the same language.)
author: nayeemrmn

body:
I agree that a *real shell* would be a bit out of scope from deno and yes we already have a cross plattform scripting language 🙂 

As @nayeemrmn sad, it should be still only a subset and not a full implementation of a shell and all available commands. Maybe the word **real shell** was not the best choice. I was mostly thinking about a repl and support for piping to or from a `deno shell/task -c "..."` command. The repl would be nice for trying out deno task commands.

> I think what would be neat is for someone to develop a Deno module that reduces the verbosity of doing sh-like stuff in JS/TS (ex. piping between processes).

The main motivation of this proposal was indeed to add support for deno tasks to tools like [zx](https://github.com/google/zx) and [dzx](https://github.com/c4spar/deno-dzx). And with a `deno shell -c "..."` or a `deno task -c""` command it could be easy to add support for this.

Than we could write den task in deno scripts this way:

```ts
await $`ls | wc -l`).pipe(Deno.stdout);
```

But this could be also solved with some proper deno bindings for [deno_task_shell](https://crates.io/crates/deno_task_shell) which would also remove the overhead of spawning a subprocess. I already tried to create some [deno task shell bindings](https://github.com/c4spar/deno-shell) and it arleady works to execute deno tasks in deno scripts 🙂 But i'm a total rust noop and its my very first rust project 😅. Would be nice if there were some official deno bindings for `deno_task_shell`. Anything planned in this direction?

body:
I am not super happy with the way the warning is proposed to work. I think we should have a `DENO_FUTURE_TYPECHECKING_BEHAVIOUR=1` flag or similar that will switch Deno into the "new" no type checking by default behaviour completely right now. The `deno run` message should be updated to say: "Warning: In future releases deno run will not automatically type check without the --check flag. To opt into this new behaviour now, specify DENO_FUTURE_TYPECHECKING_BEHAVIOUR=1."

In a couple of releases we can then turn this from a warning into an error, and then subsequently from an opt in to an opt out, and then finally fully switch.

This is very similar to how the `go.mod` adoption in the Go ecosystem worked (through the `GO111MODULE=1` env var).

body:
Ok seems I found the problem.

I am using the `deps.ts` pattern, where all the dependencies in the application are listed in the `deps.ts` file and re-exported to later be imported in modules. It seems if I am including any dependency directly or indirectly in the worker  from this `deps.ts` file, then things does not work.

Which is strange because this worked perfectly last year which was the last time I touched the app. Seems something has changed since then.
author: finlaydotb

body:
I ran into the same issue.
My `deps.ts` contains `export * as log from "https://deno.land/std@0.125.0/log/mod.ts"` and after importing it into worker with `import { log } from "./deps.ts"` calling any `log` method bricks `onmessage` in the worker. `log` imported directly with `import * as log from "https://deno.land/std@0.125.0/log/mod.ts"` works fine.
I spotted it for the first time in february.

Edit:
I tracked down my issue. It was caused by a function making use of [deno_python](https://github.com/denosaurs/deno_python)'s `python.import()` method that was exported from `deps.ts`. Somehow its presence is enough to make `Worker` constructor asynchronous and lead to lost messages. They arrive sent from `setTimeout()` with high enough delay.

body:
A lot of the structural work is there already, the allowlist supports separate `Name("ffprobe")` and `Path("/usr/local/bin/ffprobe")` variants. But currently the two are in entirely separate baskets.

> and the following code should work:
> 
> ```ts
> // ffprobe is checked against the allow-run permissions as both ffprobe _and_ what the path resolves it to (e.g. /usr/local/bin/ffprobe)
> const proc = Deno.run({ cmd: ['ffprobe'] })
> await proc.status()
> ```

To allow this example we can extend `UnaryPermission<RunDescriptor>::query("ffprobe")` to, after first checking for and not finding `Name("ffprobe")` in the allowlist, additionally check for `Path(resolve_exe_path("ffprobe"))`.

author: lucacasonato

body:
A few points here, when you do `deno run` code is _always_ interpreted and loaded as an ES module.  With Deno run, there is actually no way to force Deno to interpret code in script mode. On the converse, the REPL _has_ to interpret in script mode, because ES Modules are "closed" and can't be appended to.  There are a few things we do to try to make it feel more "module" like, like allowing "static" import statements and running in strict mode, and supporting top level await, but we are basically playing around with script mode.

As you pointed out, `module` is a JavaScript reserved word that is lexically parsed applying ASI rules, and the example code runs afoul of how the keyword should be parsed in script mode, and so you have hit an edge-case that only effects the REPL.

I am afraid it is an edge case that would be difficult if not impossible to support, and as you note, `module;` works around the issue.  It is a design limitation.
author: kitsonk

body:
Thanks for the explanation!

> As you pointed out, `module` is a JavaScript reserved word that is lexically parsed applying ASI rules, and the example code runs afoul of how the keyword should be parsed in script mode

Just to be clear, the only source for that claim appears to be that MDN article. I can't find any reference to it as a reserved word in the [spec](https://tc39.es/ecma262/#sec-keywords-and-reserved-words), and it's not treated as such in other environments such as the Firefox web console or node (where, of course, it's passed as a parameter to `require`d scripts), e.g.:

## Node.js

### REPL

> $ node

```
> const module = 'left-pad'
undefined
> module
'left-pad'
```

### CLI

> $ node ./script.js

```
SyntaxError: Identifier 'module' has already been declared
```

## Spidermonkey

### REPL

> $ spidermonkey

```
js> const module = 'left-pad'
js> module
"left-pad"
```

### CLI

> $ spidermonkey ./script.js

```
left-pad
```

## QuickJS

### REPL

> $ qjs --script

```
qjs > const module = 'left-pad'
undefined
qjs > module
"left-pad"
```

### CLI

> $ qjs --script ./script.js

```
left-pad
```

## esbuild

### CLI

> $ esbuild ./script.js

```
const module = "left-pad";
module;
```

body:
The panic is a bug indeed, but if you were trying to achieve something by passing a pointer to `Deno.seekSync()`, it probably won't do what you expect. As you noticed, that second parameter takes a number of bytes into the file to advance. Passing it a pointer into an `ArrayBuffer` (or the address of such a pointer, as with `Number(anchorAddr)`) will not seek by the length of the buffer, or do anything else that's reasonable.

The reason behind this panic is that `Deno.UnsafePointer` has a `valueOf` method, which returns a bigint. In `serde_v8`, the `deserialize_{i,u}{8,16,32,64}` methods (implemented using the `deserialize_signed` and `deserialize_unsigned` macros) will first try to detect if the value to deserialize is a bigint (i.e. `typeof value === "bigint"`), and if it isn't, it takes its (non-bigint) number value, converted to an integer. But in this case, `typeof value !== "bigint"`, but it doesn't have a non-bigint number value.

Looking at the documentation for `v8::Value::integer_value`, I also noticed that objects whose `valueOf` method returns `Number.POSITIVE_INFINITY` also trigger the same panic, because that number can't be converted to an integer.

cc @AaronO
author: andreubotella

body:
It seems that that specific module has some recursive values on importing types that break things in TypeScript 4.6 (which first shipped with Deno 1.20).

Both the following imports should be functionally the same and resolve the issue:

```
import { cac } from "https://cdn.esm.sh/cac@6.7.12/mod.ts";

import { cac } from "https://cdn.esm.sh/v74/cac@6.7.12/";
```

If either of these isn't "acceptable" fixes, I think you will need to report it to [cac](https://github.com/cacjs/cac/) as an issue.  While the issue _may_ not manifest itself under standalone `tsc` because of Deno resolution behaving a bit differently, at its root it is a recursive type in cac that is causing the issue.

body:
While the original safety issue was fixed, I'm not sure that there aren't any more of them. Additionally, if garbage collectable resources become a thing using `v8::External` objects, then the V8 API dictates that embedders should guarantee that either any two `v8::External` objects can be interchanged safely (ie. the external pointers can be interchanged), or alternatively it should be guaranteed that the wrong object cannot be used in an unrelated API.

The former could be possible if all `External`s internally implemented the same `Resource<T>` trait, but that cannot be guaranteed if FFI also uses `External`s to pass pointers. Then, the only choice becomes to make sure that the wrong object cannot be used. Since FFI may/will provide a way to create `External` objects (at worst, from plain numbers) it means that one cannot rely on `External` objects simply not being exposed to be used in unrelated APIs. The only choice thus becomes to make sure that those unrelated APIs are not exposed, meaning that raw ops should not be user-accessible and that internal usage of ops should always, only and ever work when called with eg. proper classes where an internal property contains the resource pointer in an `External`.

body: This was pointed out by @evanwashere when iterating on Deno.bench(); I went with conservative 1k iterations for both warmup and measured, but it should probably be an order of magnitude higher.
author: bartlomieju

body:
I think that generally the approach we are taking is not a good one, and we should rework it to be more like Go. The amount of iterations (and warmups) should be based on how long an iteration takes, and at what point the std deviation between iterations goes below a certain level (or reaches a maximum wall clock time limit).

The hard coded iteration counts and warmups really won't work very well for benchmarking longer running operations (ie I/O), or really fast operations. It will always require user tweaking either up or down.
author: lucacasonato

body: Agreed, most benchmarking tools warm up until the standard deviation is acceptable, and iterate until they have a statistically significant amount of measurements. This isn't only a Go thing, pretty much any benchmarking tool (https://github.com/bestiejs/benchmark.js, https://github.com/sharkdp/hyperfine. Anything else will be either overkill or underkill and very prone to erroneous and misleading results.

body:
IndexedDB is a web platform standard, even if the API is not _very good_.

Deno implementing something that is non-standard only goes to fracture the eco-system further, which is not a goal we have as part of the Deno core team.

There are quite a few "sane" APIs that are built upon IndexedDB that provide a much better API surface, for example [PouchDB](https://pouchdb.com/).  By implementing the web standard, it means that we should be able to allow a lot of users to use already built solutions, like PouchDB.

If you want to see a better web platform DB API, it is best to pursue that with the WHATWG.  There was an attempt by Google to bring a KV Store API built upon IndexedDB built into the web platform, but they have [abandoned it](https://chromestatus.com/feature/6428344899862528).
author: kitsonk

body: The most actionable thing that we can do is have a wrapper API in std, similar to the existing ones, but that then raises a question: why not use those then? why would it have to be part of std?
author: crowlKats

body:
> IndexedDB is a web platform standard, even if the API is not _very good_.
> 
> Deno implementing something that is non-standard only goes to fracture the eco-system further, which is not a goal we have as part of the Deno core team.
> 
> There are quite a few "sane" APIs that are built upon IndexedDB that provide a much better API surface, for example [PouchDB](https://pouchdb.com/). By implementing the web standard, it means that we should be able to allow a lot of users to use already built solutions, like PouchDB.
> 
> If you want to see a better web platform DB API, it is best to pursue that with the WHATWG. There was an attempt by Google to bring a KV Store API built upon IndexedDB built into the web platform, but they have [abandoned it](https://chromestatus.com/feature/6428344899862528).

the idea is to implement something like pouchDB directly in deno and or deno std (without be linked to indexedDB) on top of top this we build indexedDB api. the web standard and spec continue to be respected ( indexedDB in our context) but with alternative can be used in real app
author: elycheikhsmail

body:
Why force that choice on the users?

A design principle for Deno has been only to implement non-standard APIs when there is not a way to do it with existing standards, and try to keep the APIs low-level. Every time we implement a non-standard API, we enable users to create code that only works on Deno and we further fracture the eco-system. Not something we take lightly.

PouchDB is not the only higher-order sane API for local persistence built on top of IndexedDB.

The `std` library exists for things that are "generally good ideas that are commonly used but optional". Once IndexedDB is available in Deno, it might make sense to add something, but if there are sufficient solutions already available, I doubt it would make sense.

That is a main reason KV Store was abandoned. It was just an overlay onto IndexedDB, easily provided by a user if they so choose. Forcing it as another API in every browser using the same low level "ugly" APIs was considered unnecessary.

I think we have explained our reasoning and logic here.

A KV Store like API might make sense in `std` to lower the friction of persisting data locally, but that would be an issue to discuss once IndexedDB is available, and for the `std` repo. Therefore I am closing this issue.

author: Soremwar

body:
I agree that this should not happen with `deno fmt`. I'm explicitly talking about the language server. VSCode has a shortcut exactly for organising imports. It can be used when one doesn't care about the order but rather wants to have a readable import section.

Personally, I think that side-effects during module loading is an anti-pattern and I generally try to avoid it as much as possible. Hence, a different order of imports almost never introduces bugs. It would be good to be able to use this feature when explicitly requested.
author: KnorpelSenf

body:
Typescript now has a pattern for this with specific ordering being maintained via groups/comments: https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/announcing-typescript-4-7-rc/#group-aware-organize-imports

New developers picking up Deno might be surprised if this feature does not propagate through to Typescript files being processed by Deno.

As well as support for manually running the command, it does feel worthy of an addition to `deno fmt`, but with a flag to prevent breaking existing codebases.

I guess I'll go with the first idea of keeping it as it is, but adding a new line before the first console.log call and only a single line break for subsequent calls.

I don't think that disabling the console.log messages during tests is the best way, the messages could be useful to someone. Maybe the case could be adding a flag like "--no-console" to the test options, but I'm not sure of how hard this is.

body:
Currently deno does not support glob patterns.

You can ignore formatting by adding a `<!-- deno-fmt-ignore-file -->`comment  in markdown files. See https://deno.land/manual@v1.20.4/tools/formatter

author: ghost

body:
There is still a disconnect.  The version information you are providing does not match the panic stack trace, and the version of the library that is panicing does not match the version of the library that is included in Deno 1.20.4.  Panics in recent versions of Deno also provide information like what was provided on the command line as arguments.

The panic is coming from the the use of `import { type Interface } from "module"` syntax which recent versions of oak use.  Older version of Deno did not properly handle that syntax and would panic, which strongly indicates you might think you are running the workload under 1.20.4 but in fact are not.

body:
This issue isn’t stale.

The Deno APIs used in the workaround explained in https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14210#issuecomment-1088223213 are deprecated.

Deno still errors if you try to close a file that’s already closed, and it still doesn’t have an API to check if a file is closed or not before calling `.close()`.

A new workaround is this:

```js
file[Symbol.dispose]();
```

It closes the file if it’s still open, and is safe to call after the file has already been closed.

Once Deno supports the syntax `using file = ` in JavaScript (when it's available in V8; currently the syntax only works in TypeScript), then maybe there will no longer be a need to manually dispose of the file i.e. after catching an error.

author: crowlKats

body:
The result of your sample code is intended; your sample code is flawed in the same way Deno's code is. If you declare a ReadableStream as `type: "bytes"`, then you are declaring that it is able to handle the case where `controller.byobRequest` is set. Your code and Deno's specifies `type: "bytes"` but then fails to use `controller.byobRequest`.

If a `type: "bytes"` ReadableStream is in bring-your-own-buffer mode (can detect this by seeing that `controller.byobRequest` is non-null), then the `controller.close()` call must be followed by a call to `controller.byobRequest.respond(0)` (or `controller.byobRequest.respondWithNewView(view)` if the original `controller.byobRequest.view` has been detached), or else the read call will not resolve. (This is by the standard from my understanding of [ReadableByteStreamControllerRespondInClosedState](https://streams.spec.whatwg.org/#readable-byte-stream-controller-respond-in-closed-state), and that the two examples of `type: "bytes"` ReadableStreams in the standard ([1](https://streams.spec.whatwg.org/#example-rbs-push), [2](https://streams.spec.whatwg.org/#example-rbs-pull)) both make sure to have a respond call after controller.close() when used in byob-mode.)

Adding `controller.byobRequest?.respond(0);` after the `controller.close();` call in the https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14219#issuecomment-1090153325 example fixes it, and that works consistently in Deno, Chrome, and Node.

---

Given this, I think the fetch Response.body ReadableStream implementation in Deno is missing the byobRequest.respond/respondWithNewView call after the controller.close() call.

I searched for `type: "bytes"` in the Deno repo and I found 4 places where a `type: "bytes"` ReadableStream is created, and 3 of them have this issue.

[The implementation of Blob.stream()](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/57f7e07c13a1a692602022af4fc32c6ac352bb72/ext/web/09_file.js#L322) is an example of this issue. This could be solved by adding a `controller.byobRequest?.respond(0);` call after the close call, but since it's not making any use of user-provided buffers, it's probably better to just drop the `type: "bytes"` property instead so it doesn't have to work around bytes mode for no benefit.

The `readableStreamForRid` function at [ext/web/06_streams.js line 647](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/57f7e07c13a1a692602022af4fc32c6ac352bb72/ext/web/06_streams.js#L647) seems like a good correct example. Besides using respond(0) after `controller.close()`, it makes use of the user-provided buffer in `controller.byobRequest.view`, and it specifies the `autoAllocateChunkSize` property so that it always acts in byob mode (with `controller.byobRequest` non-null) and only needs one code path.

[ext/fetch/26_fetch.js#L101](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/57f7e07c13a1a692602022af4fc32c6ac352bb72/ext/fetch/26_fetch.js#L101) and [ext/http/01_http.js#L394](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/57f7e07c13a1a692602022af4fc32c6ac352bb72/ext/http/01_http.js#L394) both have the issue where they don't call respond(0) after close. But also they don't make use of any user-provided buffer, so they don't currently benefit from having `type: "bytes"` set. They could be made more consistent with `readableStreamForRid` in order to directly support user-provided buffers.

author: crowlKats

body: In that case, it seems like `type: "bytes"` should be removed from these three problem spots since the function of having it is to allow byob-mode readers. (Removing this would be consistent with Chrome which doesn't make fetch bodies be bytes type streams. `(await fetch("/")).body.getReader({ mode: "byob" })` in Chrome reveals this with a TypeError, and this change in Deno would make it act the same way.) I've made a basic PR doing this (#14388).

body: This is not a bug. `Writer#write` is a low level API that does not guarantee the entire passed buffer is written. `Writer#write` returns an integer `n` that specifies how many bytes were written (`0` <= `n` <= `buffer.byteLength`). To write the entire buffer, use `writeAll`. Also see https://doc.deno.land/deno/stable/~/Deno.Writer#write
author: lucacasonato

body:
Ah, fair enough. Thanks for the pointer! I completely missed the [notes in the docs mentioning as much](https://doc.deno.land/deno/stable/~/Deno.write). I guess at some point I saw an [example in the manual](https://deno.land/manual/examples/subprocess#communicating-with-subprocesses) and just ran with it, thinking it similar to what is available in node.

Seems even `writeAll` is deprecated in favor of https://deno.land/std/streams/conversion.ts

Thanks again.

author: lino-levan

body:
I'm not sure I'd feel confident if the errors just went away. This is clearly some timing / size issue in the underlying rust code / network layer. That happens, code's buggy, pipes break!

The fact that an underlying error cannot be caught by the calling code is concerning. If the calling code could catch these errors, then we could handle them in the application. I think reporting (and ultimately fixing these errors) should still be prioritized, but if underlying network problems leads to system crashes which cannot be avoided, then the platform is unusable.

author: bartlomieju

body:
The idea to merge all configs into a single config file seems a poor attempt to only cures the symptom instead of the cause.

The cause is that the UI is cluttered by many files. So maybe this should be changed by the UI, e.g. hide dot files. Some IDEs can do that. This shouldn’t be the job of Deno.

There are more reasons why a single config file isn’t ideal:
- finding a file is easier than a part of text, e.g. good file search is built into OS, terminal etc. while good text search requires good editors
- version history is better, e.g. history when the specific file was changed instead of all changes in single file
- configs don’t necessarily need to be JSON, so the argument to reduce clutter fails anyways if merging isn’t necessarily possible

author: dsherret

body:
It is intended, this occurs because `import "reflect-metadata"` has a dependency that uses top-level-await so it will complete after its synchronous siblings. You can confirm this by running:
```ts
import "data:application/javascript,import 'https://cdn.esm.sh/reflect-metadata?deno'; console.log(1)";
import "data:application/javascript,console.log(2)";
```
and observing that `2` is printed before `1` which it wouldn't be for a sync module. The TLA is probably somewhere in `std/node`.

The only way to ensure `Reflect.hasOwnMetadata()` is defined where it's needed is having `import "reflect-metadata"` at the top of `config.ts` and any other module which uses it at the top-level.

author: kiratrizon

body:
Closing this as no longer reproducible. The root cause was never in Deno: back in 2022, Docker Desktop on Apple Silicon emulated x86_64 Linux images via QEMU, which did not implement the `inotify_init`/`fanotify` syscalls that Deno's file watcher relies on, so those syscalls returned `ENOSYS` (`Function not implemented`, os error 38).

I re-ran the exact repro on an Apple Silicon host with current Docker Desktop:

```
docker run --rm --platform linux/amd64 denoland/deno:1.20.5 run --watch https://deno.land/std@0.134.0/examples/welcome.ts
```

It now works, even with the same old x86_64 1.20.5 binary:

```
Watcher Process started.
Check https://deno.land/std@0.134.0/examples/welcome.ts
Welcome to Deno!
Watcher Process finished. Restarting on file change...
```

Docker Desktop has since switched x86 emulation on macOS from QEMU to Rosetta, which passes inotify through correctly, so the issue is resolved at the emulation layer. If you hit a `--watch` failure on a current Deno version, please open a new issue with details.

> From a quick skim, it seems to be that this branch isn't being reached, even though it's intended to be?

That very code coincidentally comes from the PR that was intended to fix this issue.

Please do evaluate my suggested script snippet in your current version of Deno, and you will see the same. CrowlCats agreed that this is the case. So hopefully it may be fixed in due time. (Unfortunately, I don't have a means of locally building and testing Deno at the time, so my apologies for not attempting this myself.)
