Alternatively this could also be solved in a more general way by taking inspiration from .NET's `RemoteCertificateValidationCallback`[^1], Java's `TrustManager`[^2] and Go's `VerifyPeerCertificate`+`InsecureSkipVerify`[^3].
They allow the caller to define a callback that verifies the peer's provided certificate chain.
This can then be used to implement, for example, the TOFU mechanism.

[^1]: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.net.security.sslstream.-ctor?view=net-6.0#system-net-security-sslstream-ctor(system-io-stream-system-boolean-system-net-security-remotecertificatevalidationcallback-system-net-security-localcertificateselectioncallback)
[^2]: https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/docs/api/java.base/javax/net/ssl/SSLContext.html#init(javax.net.ssl.KeyManager%5B%5D,javax.net.ssl.TrustManager%5B%5D,java.security.SecureRandom)

body:
Are you sure you are catching all socket send() calls? This happened to me a lot, especially on "production" (demo) servers. I just made sure to `try { await ws.send(...); } catch(er) { ... };` everywhere, and it seems to have solved those issues. (tbh not even sure if send is async, the await may not be needed).

Indeed it appears to happen when sending to a socket that is already closed or maybe not yet acknowledged to be closed, but in unresponsive state - like when the client internet connection is dropped or unstable. It can often be triggered by repeatedly, aggressively refreshing a client browser, or detaching a physical cable connection. I might be wrong but I suspect it may happen also when a client machine goes standby, which may briefly leave the connection in limbo, unlike when a browser or tab is closed, which may result in more graceful connection closure.  Just some observations and guesses I have made trying to track down these very undeterministic issues for some time.
author: jtoppine

body:
> tbh not even sure if send is async, the await may not be needed

It isn't, the `WebSocket` API isn't promise based, but event based. Any errors caused asynchronously should be emitted through the `error` event.

author: derrekbertrand

body:
Confirmed still reproducing on current `main`. A project where one source file is never imported by any test reports 100% coverage, with the untested file omitted entirely:

```
| File           | Branch % | Function % | Line % |
| add-handler.ts |    100.0 |      100.0 |  100.0 |
| All files      |    100.0 |      100.0 |  100.0 |
```

(`subtract-handler.ts` exists but is never imported, so it does not appear at all.)

### Root cause

Coverage data is produced by V8's profiler, which only emits a coverage record for scripts that were actually **loaded/executed** during the run. Each loaded module is written out as a `.json` file in the coverage dir. The report is then assembled in `collect_coverages` (`cli/tools/coverage/mod.rs`), which simply reads those `.json` files back. A file that was never imported never gets a `.json` file, so there is nothing to read and it is invisible to the report.

The existing `--include` / `--exclude` options on `deno coverage` only *filter* the set of already-collected (i.e. loaded) entries via regex; they cannot *add* a file that was never loaded. So there is currently no path by which an unimported file can show up.

### What needs to be fixed

To report never-loaded files as 0% (the Jest `collectCoverageFrom` behavior referenced above), Deno needs to:

1. **Enumerate the intended source set from disk**, independent of what got loaded. This should be opt-in via a glob so Deno doesn't have to guess what counts as "source" (the open design question from earlier in this thread). Natural surfaces: a `deno coverage --include=<glob>` that means "these files should appear even if uncovered", and/or a coverage section in `deno.json`. Honor the existing `exclude`/ignore patterns.

2. **Synthesize a zero-coverage entry for each enumerated file that has no V8 record.** This is the non-trivial part: to emit correct `Line %` / `Function % `/ `Branch %` denominators for a file that was never executed, Deno has to load and transpile the file (TS -> the same emitted source used elsewhere in the coverage pipeline) to know how many lines/functions/branches it *has*, then mark them all uncovered. An all-zero entry without that wouldn't produce meaningful line/branch totals.

3. **Merge those synthetic entries into the collected set** before reporting, so they flow through the existing reporters (summary table, lcov, html) unchanged.

The key constraint is that this must be opt-in (a glob/config), because "every `*.ts` is a source file" is wrong for many projects (build scripts, generated files, fixtures), which is exactly why a heuristic was never adopted. An explicit include set sidesteps that and matches the prior art from Jest.

body:
> I'd like to work on this!

Deno's implementation of structured cloning is built on top of the Rust functions `serialize` and `deserialize` in `core/bindings.rs`, which get exposed in JS as `Deno.core.{,de}serialize`. You'd have to get a third JS-side argument to `serialize` (with `args.get(2)`), turn it into a `v8::Local<v8::Function>`, and pass it as a field to the `SerializeDeserialize` struct so you can get it from the `throw_data_clone_error` method.

The code sample in the OP tests that non-serialization errors are rethrown unchanged, but this code sample tests that serialization errors are thrown correctly:
```js
try {
  structuredClone(() => {});  // functions are not serializable
} catch(err) {
  console.log(err instanceof DOMException);  // should print true
  console.log(err.name);  // "DataCloneError"
  console.log(err.message);  // "()=>{} could not be cloned"
}
```

Or alternatively, you can test that your changes don't break any correct behavior by running the web platform tests for structured cloning: `./tools/wpt.ts run -- html/webappapis/structured-clone`.

Be sure to check out [the rusty_v8 rustdoc](https://docs.rs/v8/latest/v8/index.html). And feel free to ask for help if you're stuck or don't understand something.

The issue has changed now — `createRequire()` does not fail anymore in either variants described above.

However, a specific library fails weirdly:
```ts
// main.ts
new Worker(new URL("./worker.ts", import.meta.url).href, { type: "module" });
```

```ts
// worker.ts
import { createRequire } from "https://deno.land/std@0.167.0/node/module.ts";

const require = createRequire(new URL("./", import.meta.url).href);

// Deno.mainModule = new URL("./worker.ts", import.meta.url).href;       <-- [this line fixes the error]
const knex = require("knex")({ client: "mysql" });

console.log(knex("users").select("id", "email").toString());

Deno.exit();
```

Unless the `mainModule` variable is provided (as in the commented line above), Deno fails with
```
error: Uncaught (in worker "") TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'startsWith')
    if (Deno.mainModule.startsWith("file:")) {
                        ^

body:
How about killing a worker's event loop when it becomes idle, which resolves the parents' async ops dealing with that worker?

Edit: Nevermind, this doesn't work if the parent thread sends a message.
author: andreubotella

body:
How about this: rather than having two categories of async ops (ref'd and unref'd), we have four, adding worker host ops (that is, ops on the host to get messages from the worker) and worker ops (ops on the worker to get messages from the host).

- If a worker is idle except for worker ops (and unref'd ops, of course), the parent gets notified that the worker is idle, and stores that state.
- When a parent sends a message to the worker, it marks the worker as busy.
- If a worker is idle except for worker ops and worker host ops _and_ all of its child workers are idle, that worker notifies its parent that it is idle.
- Likewise, when the main thread is idle except for worker host ops _and_ all its child workers are idle, the program exits.

This only accounts for communication to workers by using `worker.postMessage()`, but it can easily account for `MessagePort` and (same-process) `BroadcastChannel` by having their ops be unref'd, and having the worker notify the parent that it's busy again when an unref'd op resolves. The busy notification would then cascade through the worker chain until it reaches a busy thread. This makes sense because once every thread is idle, `MessagePort` and (same-process) `BroadcastChannel` can't receive messages anymore.

Cross-process `BroadcastChannel`, as in Deploy, would act like a server, and would therefore keep the process alive. I don't think this can be worked around at all, short of detecting whether there are other processes with the same origin.
author: andreubotella

body:
I was thinking back on this issue, and rather than trying to detect isolates that are idle other than for cross-thread communication, we should try to detect whether the process as a whole is idle, perhaps in addition to each isolate. We could do this by having three kinds of async ops: regular ref'd (for I/O, timers, etc.), cross-thread ref'd, and unref'd. If at any point there are no regular ref'd ops in the entire process (...and we're not running any script or runtime code, see more below), then the entire process is idle.

One issue is how this interacts with the `beforeunload` event, since in Deno that event lets you start async ops *that will keep the isolate alive*. Currently I don't think this event is ever being fired when there are workers. The only sensible way to make this work IMO is to fire a `beforeunload` event for the main isolate every time there are no regular ref'd ops in the entire process, but only kill the workers before (or after?) the `unload` event.

The main challenges with this are synchronization and race conditions. For example, it's common for Deno runtime code handling I/O to process an async op after it's resolved and then synchronously start the next async op to listen to the next request/chunk/etc. If the ops checking happens in a different thread, we don't want it to somehow detect that this worker doesn't have ref'd ops in the middle of the synchronous processing. We could handle this by having a count of how many isolates are busy (i.e. have regular ref'd ops), with an isolate setting itself as non-idle at the start of `poll_event_loop` and updating the value as needed at the end.

author: aadamsx

body:
See: #13281

TypeScript decorators are incompatible with class fields.  We force the use of class fields in Deno, because that is modern and proper JavaScript and matches universal runtime behaviour between JavaScript and TypeScript.  The experimental decorators in TypeScript were built upon old TypeScript class fields, which have different runtime behaviour and semantics, and neither swc nor the TypeScript emit can get them to work together.

The use of `declare` in TypeScript means that it is not properly a class field, and therefore something can be emitted that works, because there is no class field to "obscure" the application of the experimental decorator.

Any sort of warning that experimental TypeScript decorators are incompatible with class fields would be something that should be taken up with TypeScript, and if accepted, Deno would update, and then issue diagnostics that they are incompatible.

author: jaydenseric

body:
> Why would it be more relaxed when you are explicitly running a command to check types, than when you are just running tests.

This is due to the new change that is gonna happen in Deno 2.0 (most likely). Typechecking is super slow especially once you get alot of bigger deps. Therefore it will be the modules responsibility to typecheck their code (which they already do because of unit tests, benchmarks and testing their code manually) and your responsibility to typecheck your's rather than you typechecking everything.
This should speed up start up time without having any real drawback
author: MierenManz

body:
Just trying Deno out for the first time, and ran into this.

It seems that `--compat` doesn't work at all if `dom` is listed in the `libs`. This does not seem to be new to `1.21`, I tried several `1.20.x` versions and ran into the same issue.

**deno.json:**

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "lib": [
      "dom",
      "dom.iterable",
      "dom.asynciterable",
      "deno.ns",
      "deno.unstable"
    ]
  }
}
```

**hello.ts:**

```ts
console.log("hello world");
```

Simply turning on `compat` triggers the `AbortSignal.reason` typing error - `deno run --compat --unstable --allow-read hello.ts`.

For now I've gotten around it by passing in `--no-check=remote`.

body:
This occurs because we spawn a blocking task for each write. So two threads could spawn one after the other, but then the second thread could end up writing to stdout before the first one:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/a919a5dd1167b79f267cbed7312b3a7d296d429f/runtime/ops/io.rs#L439-L441

It panicking instead of giving a descriptive error seems like a bug.

author: mehdiakiki

body: I am against. Reading files through `fetch()` is important because it brings parity to when a module is reading a sibling file as a local module vs a remote module. Writing a file through `fetch()` as a remote module is almost never going to work.
author: nayeemrmn

body: I agree with @nayeemrmn on this. Local file reads with `fetch` is already a shaky abstraction at best, but can be explained relatively simply, which is why I agreed to landing it. Local file writes via `fetch` have no browser precedent, have no established API in other systems, and also pose much more challenging questions, like listing directories via local file fetch.

body:
Looking at the code on fs_utils, it seems `test` and `bench` don't support file url, they only http://, https:// and relative paths or absolute paths.

A simple fix, in case the given file is using file:// protocol, could be to make fs_util::collect_specifiers to not change it at all, or transform it into a path, traverse the dir, if any, and then back into Urls
Plus add a descriptive error to all unwraps in that function.

If it's ok I would like to work on this for my first contribution to Deno, but it would be nice to have some comments from a contributor to know if my idea is valid
author: sirgallifrey

author: sirgallifrey

body:
While I have no objections and there are no technical challenges in supporting TOML as a configuration format, it is somewhat questionable if we should do it. If we add support for TOML, then soon we'll have to add support for YAML as well. That would mean probing file system 4 times in each location to find a configuration file (`deno.json`, `deno.jsonc`, `deno.toml`, `deno.yaml`).

I'm also pretty sure other team members are not gonna be keen on adding it.
author: bartlomieju

body:
Random programmer perspective:

It sounds cool, but adding more config formats will result in dispute about which one is "the best" + will create a need to understand multiple formats in order to get into programming with deno - learning curve will be impacted in negative way and beginner seeking help will have to understand both notations in order to figure out if they have a problem with config.

I really like idea of moving away of json, but it has very strong side - it is not relying on white-space to be interpreted in the correct way.
Adding toml maybe will fit some personal preferences but will rise overall community noise.

(I am kinda scared that more and more formats will be supported and options will make the config options bloated)

As a middle ground - it could be a special task that is executed each time config is interpreted:
1. Interpret config, check if there is "update_config" task.
2. If no - jump to point 4
3. Run this [update_config] task, interpret the config again.
4. Run initial task

This way - config will stay in json and it will be very clear that deno supports only json.
But - you could setup an "update_config" to run something that parses any file format and then outputs it in json.

I am pretty sure that there are a ton of downsides to my "middle ground", but imo it is a good starting point to:
- Does deno support **insert random config format or config method here**?
- Yes, as long as it outputs json
author: Danielduel

body: I strongly believe we should only have one configuration file format. Adding a second way of doing things complicates all the tooling that reads or programatically manipulates a configuration file (everything has to be implemented twice) and it increases the number of places we have to search for a possible configuration file.
author: dsherret

body:
@dsherret @Duelsik @bartlomieju 
yes, I see, thank you
author: ofzo

body:
> Adding a second way of doing things complicates all the tooling that reads or programatically manipulates a configuration file (everything has to be implemented twice) and it increases the number of places we have to search for a possible configuration file.

I mean one solution to that problem would just be to use a serde library that doesn't care about file format or extension.

Considering how popular the serde topic is within the Rust community, there's probably well over a double-dozen different serde libs/implementations for _that exact statement_ at this point.

Considering:

- the _primary_ format is JSON
- almost any reasonable serde format would be JSON-based (such as JSONC, YAML, TOML, etc),
- the availability of serde tooling in Rust

I, myself, am not convinced this would be hard or unreasonable to implement.

body:
> The way to switch between reporters `deno bench --reporter={console,json}` where the default will be the console.
> 
> Do we want to specify the benchmark results location? Something like optional `--file-reporter-location=./benchmarks`

+1 on the console suggestion, but I prefer the label `stdout` as it is more descriptive of the actual output stream — which could be used in a piped operation to another process or redirected to a file, for example:

```
# pipe to another deno program to parse the JSON and analyze the data:
deno bench --reporter=json | deno run analyze_bench_results.ts

# or redirect to a file on disk for future use:
deno bench --reporter=json > bench_data.json
```
author: jsejcksn
Lurk

body:
> My idea is to add one more reporter which will produce JSON output. And I like the idea of outputting it to `stdout` instead of writing it to some predefined file.

@Lurk According to [the examples in the manual](https://deno.land/manual@v1.28.3/tools/benchmarker), the benchmarking tool currently always outputs to the `stdout` stream. If you were suggesting allowing for the optional writing to other destinations (e.g. a file on disk), then I was simply suggesting using the label `stdout` in favor of `console` when targeting `stdout`.
author: jsejcksn

body:
@jsejcksn There will be no changes to the output destination, only its format. 

**Updated plan:**
* add new benchmark reporter JSONReporter, which outputs to `stdout` serialized to JSON [BenchReport](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/cli/tools/bench.rs#L72)
* add new CLI flag --reporter={console,json} to switch between the existing reporter and the new one


**Example usage:**
```
# pipe to another deno program to parse the JSON and analyze the data:
deno bench --reporter=json | deno run analyze_bench_results.ts

# or redirect to a file on disk for future use:
deno bench --reporter=json > bench_data.json
```
author: Lurk

body: Why not just `--json` like the other subcommands? Am I missing something here?
author: iuioiua

body:
> Why not just `--json` like the other subcommands? Am I missing something here?

@iuioiua I think the idea is to allow for additional future reporter serialization formats (e.g. CSV). Having a dedicated boolean argument for every future reporter could become unwieldy — and trying to use them in combination wouldn't make sense (e.g. `deno bench --csv --json` 👈 What should this be: CSV or JSON?).
author: jsejcksn

body: I'm unaware of any other subcommands supporting CSV output. Are there any plans to add support for CSV output for any subcommands? If not, I don't see why `deno bench` should support it while others don't.
author: iuioiua

body:
@iuioiua 
As @jsejcksn mentioned, the idea was not to limit ourselves to only JSON output. 

The internal implementation of reporters suggests that there can be multiple implementations. I want to add JSON, someone else will want to add CSV, YAML, XML, or whatever else is out there. If we add just a boolean flag, we are adding a breaking change for the future. 

**UPD.** 
checked all other subcommand ([lint](https://deno.land/manual@v1.28.3/tools/linter), [doc](https://deno.land/manual@v1.28.3/tools/documentation_generator)) and there is a `--json` flag. 

I still like `--reporter=json` more, but to keep it consistent, `--json` is not a bad idea.

body:
Taken from [the manual](https://deno.land/manual/tools/benchmarker#grouping-and-baselines), this

```
$ deno bench --unstable time_bench.ts
cpu: Apple M1 Max
runtime: deno 1.21.0 (aarch64-apple-darwin)

file:///dev/deno/time_bench.ts
benchmark              time (avg)             (min … max)       p75       p99      p995
--------------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------
Date.now()         125.24 ns/iter (118.98 ns … 559.95 ns) 123.62 ns 150.69 ns 156.63 ns
performance.now()    2.67 µs/iter     (2.64 µs … 2.82 µs)   2.67 µs   2.82 µs   2.82 µs

summary
  Date.now()
   21.29x times faster than performance.now()
```

could look like this where the time unit is seconds.

```json
[
  {
    "cpu": "Apple M1 Max",
    "runtime": "deno 1.21.0 (aarch64-apple-darwin)",
    "file": "/dev/deno/time_bench.ts",
    "group": "timing",
    "results": [
      {
        "name": "Date.now()",
        "avg": 125e-9,
        "min": 118.98e-9,
        "max": 559.95e-9,
        "p75": 123.62e-9,
        "p99": 150.69e-9,
        "p995": 156.63e-9
      },
      {
        "name": "performance.now()",
        "avg": 2.67e-6,
        "min": 2.64e-6,
        "max": 2.82e-6,
        "p75": 2.67e-6,
        "p99": 2.82e-6,
        "p995": 2.82e-6
      }
    ]
  }
]
```
author: iuioiua

body:
I think that's in the right direction, and here is some feedback:

> the time unit is seconds

The SI unit of time is seconds, and without some kind of self-documenting unit, seconds are very reasonable. Another reference is UNIX time.

> ```json
> "file": "/dev/deno/time_bench.ts",
> ```

Is it safe to assume that remote URLs are supported? If so, then perhaps the key could be something like `url` and include the full `file:` URL:

```json
"url": "file:///dev/deno/time_bench.ts",
```
author: jsejcksn

body:
> The SI unit of time is seconds, and without some kind of self-documenting unit, seconds are very reasonable. Another reference is UNIX time.

IMO the second is too big as a standard unit of measurement for benchmarking. In JavaScript land, everything is millisecond `Date.now()` or microseconds `performance.now()`.

If we look at the example output @iuioiua gave
```
...
Date.now()         125.24 ns/iter (118.98 ns … 559.95 ns) 123.62 ns 150.69 ns 156.63 ns
...
```
Even a nanosecond precision is not enough.

[9_007_199_254_740_991](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/serde_v8/ser.rs#L381) is the max value we can safely use in JSON. 

9_007_199_254_740_991 nanoseconds are roughly 104 days, which sounds like a pretty reasonable number for benchmarking. 

I would say we should use nanoseconds as a unit of measurement. It can be easily upscaled to seconds or even days if someone needs it, and at the same time gives more precision which is important when you are in a measuring contest.
author: Lurk

body:
> IMO the second is too big as a standard unit of measurement for benchmarking. In JavaScript land, everything is millisecond Date.now() or microseconds performance.now().

Actually, milliseconds is likely the best unit because it's consistent with other time-related APIs.

Precision isn't an issue thanks to decimals.
author: iuioiua

body: It's also worth noting that in most cases, the JSON data printed will be consumed by some script or similar and doesn't need to be human-readable. So the number of significant figures isn't super important.

body: It looks like the value -1 is being incorrectly converted to 0. In `Deno.isatty()` and `opSync()`, the value is -1, but by the time `op_isatty()` is called on the Rust side, the value has become 0.
author: cjihrig

body:
> It looks like the value -1 is being incorrectly converted to 0. In `Deno.isatty()` and `opSync()`, the value is -1, but by the time `op_isatty()` is called on the Rust side, the value has become 0.

afaik it was something with parsing `-1` to string on some level, which converted `-` to falsy to `0` to `tty0` or sth like that
(0% sure, it was on discord briefly)
author: Danielduel

body: This is causing a std library failure with the test `node/tty_test.ts:[node/tty isatty] returns false for irrelevant values`.  Not sure why it isn't causing CI to fail, but caught it locally.
author: kitsonk

body:
The problem seems to be coming from [here](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/d0dec8d36b751638d15a44ef790a36049f1bc359/serde_v8/de.rs#L104-L105). The original value of -1 becomes -1.0, which `as u32` converts into 0 (interestingly, -1 as a signed integer would be converted to 4294967295).

An easy fix for the issue is to update `Deno.isatty()` to use `rid >>> 0` to force the value to an unsigned integer in JavaScript, but the underlying problem will still exist everywhere else we deal with unsigned numbers.

A better solution might be to update `deserialize_unsigned` to cast to a signed integer first before casting to an unsigned integer so that the floating point portion of the number stops causing problems.

@AaronO any thoughts?

EDIT: Discussed internally and will look into making `deserialize_unsigned` throw if it receives a negative number.
author: cjihrig

body: `Deno.isatty()` has been removed in Deno 2.

body:
This is covered now by `node:dns` / `node:dns/promises` `lookupService`, which is the `getnameinfo` wrapper this issue asked for:

```js
import dnsp from "node:dns/promises";

const { hostname, service } = await dnsp.lookupService("127.0.0.1", 22);
console.log(hostname, service); // localhost ssh
```

That returns the exact `{ hostname, service }` shape and result the request described, and the callback form (`dns.lookupService`) works too. The original motivation, supporting the `dns`/`dns/promises` compat modules, is therefore met.

We don't need a dedicated `Deno.resolveNameInfo()` for this when `node:dns.lookupService` already provides it, so closing as resolved.

body: We should be very careful with this. It may be slower depending on what the user is doing. The bug itself seems having existed from the beginning https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/1130/files#diff-1f62e23971d4281e275c4e80b39ed60ac6f0b71b14bba6d92e4decf790ad53e1R129

(Note:
- iterateReader was renamed from iter in https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/pull/813
- iter in std was migrated from Deno.iter in https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/pull/843
- Deno.iter was renamed from Deno.toAsyncIterator in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/4848

author: jespertheend

body:
No longer reproduces on current Deno — closing as fixed.

The original bug was that under `--check`, the stack-trace URL was a content-hashed `blob:///<hash>.js` form unrelated to the `blob:null/<uuid>` URL returned by `URL.createObjectURL()`. That no longer happens — the URL in the stack trace now matches the one from `createObjectURL()` exactly, in both `--check` and `--no-check`:

```
url: blob:null/14e44d7b-77df-4adb-a807-a7eba070eb25
    at blob:null/14e44d7b-77df-4adb-a807-a7eba070eb25:3:5
```

So mapping errors in blob modules back to their original source (the fake-imports use case) works now, since the reported URL is the same one handed out by `createObjectURL()`.

If you still see a mismatched blob URL in stack traces on a current version, please reopen with a repro.

author: crowlKats

body:
Closing as stale. WebGPU has been fully rewritten since this 2022 report — Deno now ships `wgpu-core` 28.0.0 (vs. the ~0.12-era backend at the time), and WebGPU was removed and re-added/stabilized in the interim. The current implementation also can no longer segfault on a failed adapter request: `requestAdapter()` returns `null` gracefully when no usable adapter is found, which is the spec-correct behavior.

WSL2 GPU/Vulkan support has likewise matured significantly on the OS/driver side.

If anyone can still reproduce a segfault on a recent Deno under WSL2, please reopen with `deno --version` and a backtrace.

body: Doh, of course it's floating promise 🤦 while the solution to the test is fine, it's really a band-aid and won't fix it for other cases...
author: bartlomieju

body: So is the issue that we want to similarly filter frames of floating rejections when in `deno test`? The test is technically miswritten either way, though it's not a big deal.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
> So is the issue that we want to similarly filter frames of floating rejections when in `deno test`? The test is technically miswritten either way, though it's not a big deal.

Yes, I think we should filter stack frames for all errors raised and not handled by user. Good point about a test, I will open a PR to fix that.
author: bartlomieju

body:
`helix` support local config. just put this config for deno LSP in `.helix/languages.toml` in the root of the project :
```
﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿[[language]]
name = "javascript"
shebangs = ["deno", "node"]
roots = ["deno.json", "package.json", "tsconfig.json"]
config = { enable = true, lint = true, unstable = true }
language-server = { command = "deno", args = ["lsp"], language-id = "javascript" }

[[language]]
name = "jsx"
shebangs = ["deno", "node"]
roots = ["deno.json", "package.json", "tsconfig.json"]
config = { enable = true, lint = true, unstable = true }
language-server = { command = "deno", args = ["lsp"], language-id = "javascriptreact" }

[[language]]
name = "typescript"
shebangs = ["deno", "node"]
roots = ["deno.json", "package.json", "tsconfig.json"]
config = { enable = true, lint = true, unstable = true }
language-server = { command = "deno", args = ["lsp"], language-id = "typescript" }

[[language]]
name = "tsx"
shebangs = ["deno", "node"]
roots = ["deno.json", "package.json", "tsconfig.json"]
config = { enable = true, lint = true, unstable = true }
language-server = { command = "deno", args = ["lsp"], language-id = "typescriptreact" }
```
author: hemedani

body:
@kitsonk I know you're not part of deno core anymore, but could you give any quick tips / ideas on what you meant by this?

author: lino-levan

body: Fixed by #20358.
author: nayeemrmn
3210jr

body:
@3210jr It's very simple bro
It is the new `languages.toml` file:
```toml
[[language]]
name = "javascript"
scope = "source.js"
injection-regex = "^(js|javascript)$"
file-types = ["js", "jsx", "mjs"]
shebangs = ["deno", "node"]
roots = ["deno.json", "package.json", "tsconfig.json"]
# comment-token = "//"
# config = { enable = true, lint = true, unstable = true, importMap = "deno.json" }
language-servers = ["deno"]
indent = { tab-width = 2, unit = "  " }
auto-format = true

[[language]]
name = "jsx"
scope = "source.jsx"
injection-regex = "jsx"
file-types = ["jsx"]
shebangs = ["deno", "node"]
roots = ["deno.json", "package.json", "tsconfig.json"]
language-servers = ["deno"]
indent = { tab-width = 2, unit = "  " }
grammar = "javascript"
auto-format = true

[[language]]
name = "typescript"
scope = "source.ts"
injection-regex = "^(ts|typescript)$"
file-types = ["ts"]
shebangs = ["deno", "node"]
roots = ["deno.json", "package.json", "tsconfig.json"]
language-servers = ["deno"]
indent = { tab-width = 2, unit = "  " }
auto-format = true

[[language]]
name = "tsx"
scope = "source.tsx"
injection-regex = "^(tsx)$" # |typescript
file-types = ["tsx"]
shebangs = ["deno", "node"]
roots = ["deno.json", "package.json", "tsconfig.json"]
language-servers = ["deno"]
indent = { tab-width = 2, unit = "  " }
auto-format = true

[language-server.deno]
command = "deno"
args = ["lsp"]
config = { enable = true, lint = true, unstable = true, importMap = "deno.json" }
```
author: hemedani

body:
Okay I found what was wrong!! Turns out the `config-reload` command doesn't really reload everything! What i needed to do was actually close out of the session and restart it!

Cheers @hemedani ! Thanks again!
author: 3210jr
AaronO

body: @AaronO Thank you for your reply.
author: linbingquan

body: You can explicitly specify a location with --location
author: lucacasonato

body:
> You can explicitly specify a location with --location

@lucacasonato Yes. Thank you for your suggestion.

TBH, I don't know why there is an error, but I saw `Location.href: string` in `lib.dom.d.ts`

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/1d51b1649e75b8d2e8ca81c9aab08c9f119a6ab5/cli/dts/lib.dom.d.ts#L8916

Maybe it should be `href?: string` will be clearly. I don't know why it defined that way, maybe I missed some thing.
author: linbingquan

body: More like `location` can be undefined, not `location.href`

body: This is a bug. Thanks for reporting. @littledivy  probably the rust2rust streams optimization?
author: lucacasonato

body:
Yes, a bug...unfortunate that tests didn't catch this. 

@sant123 as a quick workaround: call `try { file.close() } catch { /** Pass **/ }` after sending the response.

sant123

body:
Well, in deno 1.21.2, [the workaround](#issuecomment-1114581926) breaks.

The Response body is empty, much like the file has been closed before the response is returned.

Why is that? The behavior is quite confusing... @sant123 @littledivy

body:
This is a widely used API and not having an alternative ready to go will cause a huge amount of distrust in our user base. Opening an issue to removing something widely used in a release a couple weeks will break a lot of existing solutions, causing people to not be able to upgrade.

Feels very irresponsible to me.
author: kitsonk

body:
There's no indication at https://deno.land/manual/typescript/runtime that the feature will be removed, either. I wasn't aware that this is being considered.

Now that we suddenly have to rewrite `Deno.emit` very fast in user land before 1.22 hits, can you suggest where to start with this? For example, can we wrap `ts_morph`?

What do you think about moving this to `/std` instead of dropping it completely?
author: KnorpelSenf

body:
> it’s unstable so anything can change at any time.

The [documentation about stability](https://deno.land/manual/runtime/stability) doesn't explicit state that said feature is subject to removal though:

> However, not all of Deno's features are ready for production yet. Features which are not ready, because they are still in draft phase, are locked behind the `--unstable` command line flag.
>
> You should be aware that many unstable APIs have **not undergone a security review**, are likely to have **breaking API changes** in the future, and are **not ready for production**.

It only suggest that a feature usage may change or is unsecure but will *eventually* stabilize, *not* that its support could be entirely dropped. 

I think a full removal implies a lot more than just "breaking API changes", even for unstable features, especially if there are no alternatives yet. 

Though it could be that I misunderstand what the documentation was trying to said too 🤔
author: lowlighter

body:
> This is a widely used API and not having an alternative ready to go will cause a huge amount of distrust in our user base. Opening an issue to removing something widely used in a release a couple weeks will break a lot of existing solutions, causing people to not be able to upgrade.
> 
> Feels very irresponsible to me.

yes, currently the [deno-rollup](https://github.com/cmorten/deno-rollup) is broken, because of this any library or package that is making use of it is also [snel](https://github.com/crewdevio/Snel) for example, for now the most feasible replacement seems to be esbuild, I think that in the future it would be better to add the tag deprecated before removing completely to allow time for replacement.


**UPDATE**

Since there is already a replacement [(emit)](https://github.com/denoland/deno_emit) outside of the deno namespace I think it shouldn't be too hard to update
author: buttercubz

body:
To be clear, this API was removed from the `Deno` namespace and moved to https://deno.land/x/emit -- You can update your usages to use that instead now. Sorry for the breakage, but this will provide more stability going forward since it's a versioned API.

> The [documentation about stability](https://deno.land/manual/runtime/stability) doesn't explicit state that said feature is subject to removal though:

@lowlighter in this case, the feature ended up being not really removed, but moved to deno.land/x. This is a much better situation than an API incompatible change to the unstable API because in that case the consumers might need to version sniff to figure out what API to use and then maybe implement stuff multiple times for all the Deno versions they want it to support. In this case, they can instead just update to a versioned API at https://deno.land/x/emit and it will work with many Deno versions and continue to work in the future because the API has been decoupled from the runtime version.

For other scenarios where an API is removed without alternatives, we should update the documentation to be clear that's possible.
author: dsherret

body:
> @lowlighter in this case, the feature ended up being not really removed, but moved to deno.land/x

Tbh I'm fine with it being in deno land.
The initial issue title/content suggested that it was being removed while no credible alternative were provided

On a side note, I feel like that these kind of APIs should have their own namespace on deno.land (like deno.land/std) rather than using the global deno.land/x, since these features seems to be backed by deno team. Could also avoid possible name collisions in the future

body: Deno already support high precision timing using [`performance.now()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Performance/now) API.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> Deno already support high precision timing using [`performance.now()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Performance/now) API.

"Also unlike Date.now(), the values returned by performance.now() always increase at a constant rate, independent of the system clock (which might be adjusted manually or skewed by software like NTP). Otherwise, performance.timing.navigationStart + performance.now() will be approximately equal to Date.now()."

body: No, we should instead implement `performance.timeOrigin`, because you can get a high precision unix timestamp with `performance.timeOrigin + performance.now()`. Thus duplicate of #10837.

body:
> `--check` to enable

Currently it's:
- `deno run main.ts` - Check all
- `deno run --check main.ts` - Check all
- `deno run --check=all main.ts` - Check all

I think 1.22 was supposed to be:
- `deno run main.ts` - Check with no remote
- `deno run --check main.ts` - Check with no remote
- `deno run --check=all main.ts` - Check all

(+ deprecate `--no-check=remote`)

And at 1.23 or currently with `DENO_FUTURE_CHECK`:
- `deno run main.ts` - No check
- `deno run --check main.ts` - Check with no remote
- `deno run --check=all main.ts` - Check all

(+ deprecate `--no-check`)

Is this correct?
author: nayeemrmn

body: @nayeemrmn  that looks right!
author: lucacasonato

body: `--doc` is still opt-in on current Deno, and that appears to be deliberate: enabling JSDoc/Markdown code-block evaluation by default would be a breaking change that runs many snippets not written as tests, causing unexpected failures for existing projects. Given it's remained opt-in across several years and major versions, this looks intentional rather than an oversight. Closing as not-planned — the feature is available any time via `deno test --doc`.
author: littledivy

body:
I close the issue : it's not bug from Deno but a bug from the lib xmlp.

The chunk size change since 1.21.0, and the chunk now split on an UTF8 char. I was lucky that it cut in the right place before.

For information, my fix consists in using a TextDecoderStream and writing the strings to the parser with a pipeTo, instead of letting the lib do the `TextDecoder.decode` from the chunks of Uint8Array.
```
- parser.parse(stream);  
+ stream
+    .pipeThrough(new TextDecoderStream(encoding))
+    .pipeTo(new WritableStream({ write: (str) => parser.parse(str) }));
```
It's possible because the lib can take as input a string in addition to the Reader :
`parse(source: Deno.Reader | Uint8Array | string)`

author: idranme

body: Looking further into this, it looks like `deno_core`'s `op_print` is using Rust's [`std::io::stdout()`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/io/fn.stdout.html), which seems to explicitly handle text encoding in Windows. The new `op_print` in `deno_runtime`, however, uses [`std::fs::File`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/fs/struct.File.html) wrapping the stdout file descriptor, which of course doesn't handle encoding. This is also the reason for #6001, since `Deno.stdout` also uses the `std::fs::File` wrapper.

author: c4spar

body:
This is slightly incorrect. VSCode has multiple levels of configuration, of which the most specific, is stored in the `.vscode/setting.json`, this isn't something the extension controls, the extensions simply requests certain settings to be adjusted (or a user configures the setting themselves, either through the GUI or manually) and vscode stores them.

We should not interfere with that. Adding and "lsp" section to the deno.jsonc would make the whole thing a lot more confusing and less portable. Specifically with the vscode extension, the extension and the lsp have to be in sync, so that the lsp can provide diagnostics and that the client can "mute" the built in diagnostics. If they are out of sync, there are big usability issues. The only reliable way to do this is to have the client drive the enablement (as well as other adjustments to configuration of the lsp, as it is configuration of how the editor behaves).

Just because vscode stores workspace settings in the `.vscode/settings.json` should be immaterial to us, it stores "project" settings in a different way, and stores user level ones another different way. Both the client and the server are abstracted away from these, and simply using the configuration APIs provided by the editor.
author: kitsonk

body: I agree with @kitsonk. An other issue is that deno.json is shared between developers, but not all developers likely want the same LSP configuration. LSP configuration is not just per project, but rather per developer per project.

body:
> I would expect Deno to be able to resolve sibling d.ts files without requiring a known extension.

Deno doesn't do any magic resolution on specifiers so it needs the extension. Additionally, `tsc`'s output is not compatible with Deno. Generally generating code with `tsc` for consumption with Deno is not a common pattern since TypeScript can just be imported.

That said, I would have thought tsc would maintain extensions in index.d.ts if the original file had extensions, but I guess not.

body:
```
test result: ok. 0 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out (13ms)

error: Uncaught ReferenceError: document is not defined
export const div = document.createElement('div');
                   ^
    at file:///Users/robertthomas/Programming/web/deno-test-err/index.ts:1:20
```

This is an actual runtime error and not TypeScript error. If you configure `compilerOptions` and pass "dom" it tells built-in TypeScript compiler that these types are available but it doesn't actually make `document` available in Deno, you would have to polyfill it yourself.

```

I didn't think about such case where non-`Error` values are thrown, and we're just assuming everything can be turned into an error. I believe we need to alter this code:
```
pub enum TestResult {
  Ok,
  Ignored,
  Failed(Box<JsError>),
}
```
to have an enum in `TestResult::Failed` variant:
```
TestFailure {
   error: Option<Box<JsError>>,
   object: Option<Value>
}
```

CC @nayeemrmn

> ```
> 
> to have an enum in `TestResult::Failed` variant:
> 
> ```
> TestFailure {
>    error: Option<Box<JsError>>,
>    object: Option<Value>
> }
> ```

Haha, this is almost exactly what I'm in the process of implementing
```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Deserialize)]
#[serde(rename_all = "camelCase")]
pub enum TestError {
  JsError(Box<JsError>),
  Other(String),
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Deserialize)]
#[serde(rename_all = "camelCase")]
pub enum TestResult {
  Ok,
  Ignored,
  Failed(TestError),
}
```
I don't know how we'd handle v8 values here so I'm stringifying them in JS. But it doesn't apply to uncaught errors unfortunately since they're caught in rust and downcasted from `AnyError`.

body:
I think the square brackets were intentional on their part — they were trying to access `import.meta` dynamically. However, `import` is not a real object, and `import.meta` is just special syntax for accessing metadata of the current module. Since it's not a real object and you're not really accessing any properties on anything, `import["meta"]` does not work. There should never be any reason to want to access `import.meta` dynamically anyway — doing so doesn't really make any sense. 

Regardless, Deno probably still shouldn't panic on it 😆 The correct behaviour here is `Uncaught SyntaxError: Unexpected token '['`.

Instead of:
```js
console.log(await Deno.readTextFile(new URL(import.meta.url).pathname)); // path has the leading slash

do:

body: `new URL(...).pathname` has a first slash [per the spec](https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#url-path-serializer). If you need to turn a URL into a OS-valid path, why not use [`fromFileUrl` from https://deno.land/std@0.139.0/path/mod.ts](https://deno.land/std@0.139.0/path#fromfileurl)
author: andreubotella

body:
> @bartlomieju the problem with that is that any needed modification with that `dirname` & `join` isnt doable. maybe dirname & join (and others) should maybe support accepting a `URL`?

Depends, you can always use `fromFileUrl()` first
author: bartlomieju

body:
> @bartlomieju the problem with that is that any needed modification with that `dirname` & `join` isnt doable

Correct, that's why instead of those you have to use the URL constructor (e.g. `new URL(".", url)` for `dirname`) otherwise you have to go full paths with `fromFileUrl()`. `Url::pathname` don't substitute FS paths
author: nayeemrmn

body: you three are right, completely forgot about `fromFileUrl` was a thing
author: crowlKats

body:
```ts
join(dirname(new URL(fromFileUrl(import.meta.url)).pathname), "units.txt")
```
does work, yes, but I think it's a bit concerning that it's possible to so trivially write code that works on unix systems and doesn't work on Windows (and vice versa). I cloned a Deno project from GitHub, and tried to run it, but it didn't work on Windows. It's a bit disappointing if Deno promises cross-platform portable code, yet a project might fail to run on only some systems. This doesn't feel like something that ought to be OS-dependent imo, and the proposed solution feels more like a workaround.
author: 0kku

body:
> ```ts
> join(dirname(new URL(fromFileUrl(import.meta.url)).pathname), "units.txt")
> ```

The proper way of writing this is:
```ts
new URL("units.txt", import.meta.url)
```
The URL constructor will pretty much always satisfy these use cases. In fact, forget about `fromFileUrl()`.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
> I cloned a Deno project from GitHub, and tried to run it, but it didn't work on Windows. It's a bit disappointing if Deno promises cross-platform portable code, yet a project might fail to run on only some systems. This doesn't feel like something that ought to be OS-dependent imo, and the proposed solution feels more like a workaround.

Exactly for this reason the FS APIs accept URLs so you don't have to do the conversion yourself.
author: bartlomieju

body:
In that case it might be nice if they _only_ accepted URLs so that people wouldn't accidentally write code that might or might not run depending on the OS it happens to be run on?

To elaborate: the issue I'm reporting is that it's possible to accidentally write non-portable code, not that it isn't possible to get it to work on all OSs when written correctly. The compiler shouldn't let people write non-portable code in the first place in situations like these.
author: 0kku

body:
> To elaborate: the issue I'm reporting is that it's possible to accidentally write non-portable code, not that it isn't possible to get it to work on all OSs when written correctly. The compiler shouldn't let people write non-portable code in the first place in situations like these.

That would be a breaking change, and I don't think that would be changed until Deno 2.0 (if it ever really comes).
author: andreubotella

body: URLs can not represent all valid UNIX file paths, as such we must support accepting both `URL` and `string`. This behavior is consistent with Node and the URL spec, so I think there is nothing actionable for us to do here, other than writing more docs.

body:

body:
Closing as fixed — the panic no longer happens.

The crash came from the test runner doing `.unwrap()` on the stdout write when it captured non-UTF-8 subprocess output and tried to write it to a Windows console (`cli/tools/test.rs:383` at the time). The test runner has since been rewritten: captured output is carried as raw bytes (`TestEvent::Output(Vec<u8>)`) and written in `cli/tools/test/reporters/pretty.rs`:

```rust
let filtered = filter_destructive_ansi(output);
std::io::stdout().write_all(&filtered).ok();
```

The `.ok()` replaces the old `.unwrap()`, so a write error is ignored rather than panicking the process.

One residual note: on a real Windows console, non-UTF-8 bytes still can't be written (Rust's Windows stdio converts to UTF-16 for `WriteConsoleW`), so those specific bytes are dropped rather than displayed — but this is now graceful, not a crash, and only affects console output (piped/redirected output is unaffected). `Deno.run` itself is also soft-removed in Deno 2.

If anyone still hits a panic here on a current version, please reopen with a repro.

body:
```diff
-      controller.enqueue(line);
+      controller.enqueue(line.slice());
```

The documentation of `BufReader#readSlice()` states:

> readSlice() reads until the first occurrence of delim in the input, returning a slice pointing at the bytes in the buffer. **The bytes stop being valid at the next read.**

As such, you need to take a copy of the bytes before the next read.

body:
@ivpdev Here's the root issue in Docker: https://github.com/docker/for-mac/issues/6174, specifically:
> However, attempts to run Intel-based containers on Apple silicon machines under emulation can crash as qemu sometimes fails to run the container. In addition, filesystem change notification APIs (inotify) do not work under qemu emulation. Even when the containers do run correctly under emulation, they will be slower and use more memory than the native equivalent.

author: curtisallen

body:
This is supported today via the \`DENO_LOG\` environment variable, which is the
\`TF_LOG\`-style debug logging requested here (the proposed \`deno_log=DEBUG\` is
\`DENO_LOG=debug\`).

\`\`\`console
# leveled debug output across execution phases
$ DENO_LOG=debug deno run program.ts
DEBUG RS - deno::args - Finished config loading.
DEBUG RS - deno::worker - main_module file:///.../program.ts
DEBUG RS - deno::module_loader - Building module graph.
DEBUG RS - deno::module_loader - Prepared module load.
DEBUG RS - deno_runtime::worker - received module evaluate Ok(())
...

# filter to a specific module
$ DENO_LOG=deno::module_loader=debug deno run program.ts

# or a subsystem
$ DENO_LOG=deno_core=debug deno run program.ts
\`\`\`

Log style can be tuned with \`DENO_LOG_STYLE\`. This gives the execution-phase
visibility (versions, module load/eval, worker and event-loop lifecycle) the
request was after.

Closing as completed -- if there's a need for additional structured fields or
extra log points, a new issue with the specifics would be the better place to
track it.

body:
## Notes on how we could support this

I looked into wiring memory reporting into `deno bench`. Summary of findings and a proposed approach:

### The pipeline this touches
- JS measurement loop: `cli/js/40_bench.js` (`benchMeasure` / `benchStats`)
- timing op: `op_bench_now` in `cli/ops/bench.rs`
- Rust stats struct `BenchStats` in `cli/tools/bench/mod.rs` (deserialized from JS via `FromV8`)
- reporters: `cli/tools/bench/reporters.rs` + vendored `cli/tools/bench/mitata.rs` (hand-aligned table)
- public types in `cli/tsc/dts/lib.deno.ns.d.ts`

### Primitives already in the tree
We don't need new V8 plumbing:
- **Heap measurement** — `op_memory_usage` (`libs/core/ops_builtin_v8.rs`) already calls `scope.get_heap_statistics()` and returns `used_heap_size()` / `total_heap_size()` / `external` (it backs `Deno.memoryUsage()`).
- **Forcing GC** — `isolate.low_memory_notification()` triggers a full GC and is already used in `runtime/worker.rs` and `ext/node/ops/v8.rs`. Wrapping it in an op lets us run the gc step **without** requiring the user to pass `--v8-flags=--expose-gc`.

### Proposed approach (memory)
Add a separate memory pass after the timing pass (so it doesn't perturb the time numbers), following the flow from the issue:
1. `gc()` to settle the heap
2. sample `used_heap_size()` as baseline
3. run a fixed batch of N iterations
4. `gc()` again, sample heap
5. `(after − before) / N` ≈ bytes/iter

Memory is much noisier than time, so we'd run a few batches and report the median avg, and gate the whole thing behind an opt-in (`deno bench --memory` and/or `Deno.bench({ memory: true })`) — a GC between samples can cost more than a fast bench's whole iteration, and adding a column changes everyone's output / JSON schema version.

### Object count
This is the hard part. V8 has no cheap per-iteration "objects allocated" counter — the only real sources are heap snapshots or allocation sampling, both far too heavy for a tight bench loop and fragile to diff. My recommendation is to ship **memory-only** first and treat object-count as a follow-up.

Does memory-only behind an opt-in flag sound like the right first step?


---

body:
@kyeotic how are you creating these files? Files cached by `deno cache` end up in the `DENO_DIR` and aren't intended to be executed directly—they're only created for caching purposes.

Edit: Oh, is this the `gen/file` folder in the deno_dir? Yeah, it's not intended to be executed. `deno cache` is intended to download and compile a module (cache it for future runs) without running any code.
author: dsherret

body: @dsherret  I am using [these steps from](https://github.com/hayd/deno-lambda#deno_dir-remapping) `deno-lambda`. I don't understand why these files are generated if they are not intended to be used. I want to get compiled JS so that the runtime doesn't need to to any TS overhead once deployed.
author: kyeotic

body:
@kyeotic if you do `deno cache hello.ts`, it will cache the files that you see above to the DENO_DIR. That will make it so if you run `deno run --no-check hello.ts` there won't be any ts or caching overhead once deployed if you also deploy the DENO_DIR (not so you can run the files in the cache directory on their own). The files in the DENO_DIR are internal to Deno.

Alternatively, you can use `deno bundle hello.ts` to create a bundled js file or `deno compile hello.ts` to create a self contained executable.
author: dsherret

body: Interesting, that makes sense though. I think it would be helpful if this was documented.
author: kyeotic

body:
> I think it would be helpful if this was documented.

What sort of documentation do you suggest?  The format of the `DENO_DIR` is not documented because we don't want people to couple to what is _internal_ behaviour of Deno which doesn't form part of the public API. We have changed the format before, and will likely need to change it again in the future.

body:
This is currently a design limitation, as it would require a reliable way to statically analyze a module for tests without actually running the module.  Currently the static analysis is limited to a single module and identifying the `Deno.test` symbol and tracking tests that are registered.

Things like dynamically generated tests (or test steps) as well as other libraries that build on `Deno.test` cannot currently be analyzed and I personally currently can envision a good way to accomplish this at the moment. @dsherret I don't know if you have any thoughts on the topic.
author: kitsonk

body: Definitely not an easy feature to implement but maybe it makes sense to implement it for `bdd` library only for now as it is part of std.
author: glebbash

body: I agree that static analysis for this would be very hard to do, and it is even harder to do it right. Doing it for `/std` only is the much more practical solution, albeit not as friendly towards third-party testing frameworks. However, I would argue that those frameworks are not equal to the standard library testing because, well, they're not in the standard library, and hence it is fine to only support `/std` here.

body:
It's not possible for any JS function to access this data unless you have a corresponding op that can access it. In such case a call to `Deno.core.opSync('op_name')` could access this data. 

If you provide more context on what you're trying to do we can advise some solution.

body: Be aware that all data on the same thread (and possibly in the same process) as the V8 isolate is vulnerable to leakage through side channel attacks like Spectre unless very stringent precautions relating to timing accuracy are taken.

Version 0.11.0 of the __p384__ crate was released a few days ago, and it seems to contain the functionality we require. However, in bumping __p384__ from v0.8.0 to v0.11.0 I got some Cargo version conflicts. To resolve these conflicts, I upgraded some other RustCrypto crates:

- __elliptic-curve__ from 0.10.6 to 0.11.0 (0.12.0 is the latest)
- __p256__ from 0.9.0 to 0.11.0
- __rsa__ from 0.5.0 to 0.6.1
- __spki__ from 0.4.1 to 0.6.0

Unfortunately, this broke the build (https://gist.github.com/diachedelic/a760e057d032dcc53765dda73adf863f). At this point, I realised I was out of my depth.

body: @AaronO Should we just map the unit type `()` to `undefined` instead of `null`? If someone wants `null` they can use `Option::None`.
author: lucacasonato

body:
> @AaronO Should we just map the unit type `()` to `undefined` instead of `null`? If someone wants `null` they can use `Option::None`.

This is already the case for ops (as an "optimization" to skip `ReturnValue::set()`), void functions or `Result<(), ...>` will return undefined, here it appears that @espindola wants conditionally undefined returns, which can be achieved with `core.opSync("op_foo") ?? undefined` JS-side

I get their thinking, if they're doing a key/map lookup it's natural to imitate JS obj/map lookups which return values or `undefined`
author: AaronO

body:
Right, but if all `()` values in serde_v8 were serialized to `undefined`, one could do:

```rs
#[derive(Serialize)]
#[serde(untagged)]
enum MaybeUndefined<T> {
  Some(T),
  None(()),
}
```

body: Wanted to see if I can help find the bug, but I can't even find the place where `Atomics.waitAsync` is implemented.
author: Eyal-Shalev

body:
> Wanted to see if I can help find the bug, but I can't even find the place where `Atomics.waitAsync` is implemented.

Deno doesn't implement `Atomics.waitAsync`, that's implemented in V8, the JS engine that Deno (as well as Node.js and Chrome) use. Assuming this is not a V8 bug, it's almost certainly related to Deno's event loop code not knowing about `Atomics.waitAsync` and only knowing about "ops" (Deno's word for Rust functions that are passed into deno_core so they get exposed into the Javascript VM). The relevant code is in [`JsRuntime::poll_event_loop`](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/core/runtime.rs#LL1019) (or perhaps elsewhere in `JsRuntime`), but I doubt it would be easy to fix.
author: andreubotella

body: It's implemented by V8 itself. There's probably some API in V8 to either hook into these APIs or query the number or active timers like this.

body: This issue prevents projects built with Emscripten using its ["PROXY_TO_PTHREAD"](https://emscripten.org/docs/porting/pthreads.html#additional-flags) flag from working in Deno. (I don't have a specific project using this flag in mind; I just discovered this while getting Emscripten's tests to run with Deno.)
author: Macil

body:
On the emscripten side this prevents almost all multi-thread from working since we use `Atomics.waitAsync` to communicate between worker threads and the main thread.

We fall back to using `postMessage` when `Atomics.waitAsync` is not available, so maybe better to simply hide `Atomics.waitAsync` until its working in deno (which it certainly doesn't seem to be today).

body:
Here is the temporary workaround I decided on using for now. Using deno check with an empty import map instead of the one in vendor/import_map.json when I want to type check my code. The downside to this is that my test files are unchecked.

```js
deno check --import-map=import_map.json main.ts
```

Another workaround I thought of was running my tests twice. First with `--no-check=remote` and the empty import map, then with the vendor import map and type checking disabled. That would allow me to verify types in my local code and that my code runs with my vendored dependencies. The downside to this would be that my ci would fail if my external deps broke, which kind of defeats one of my motivations for using vendored dependencies. I was having code break due to breaking changes on esm.sh when I wasn't using vendoring.

body:
Sorry about that. I was missing reproduction path for the error in the original post. The original reproduction path was just for those 2 type errors happening only with vendor import map. I figured out minimal reproduction path for the assertion error. In this example, I just added one new import than the original reproduction path I had for the two typescript errors.

Here is example.ts:

```ts
export { default as React } from "https://esm.sh/react@18.1.0?target=deno&pin=v85";
export { hydrateRoot } from "https://esm.sh/react-dom@18.1.0/client?target=deno&pin=v85&deps=react@18.1.0";
export { renderToReadableStream } from "https://esm.sh/react-dom@18.1.0/server?target=deno&pin=v85&deps=react@18.1.0";

export { Link } from "https://esm.sh/react-router-dom@6.3.0?target=deno&pin=v85&deps=react@18.1.0,react-dom@18.1.0";
```

Here is my deno.json file:

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "lib": ["esnext", "dom", "dom.iterable", "dom.asynciterable", "deno.ns"]
  }
}
```

If I run the script without vendoring, there are no errors.

If I try running deno vendor --force example.ts I get the following error.

```
$ deno vendor --force example.ts
error: Relative import path "react" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../ and not in import map from "https://esm.sh/v85/@types/react-dom@18.0.5/client~.d.ts"
```

That error is an issue with esm.sh, for some reason it is using "react" as an import map. I workaround that issue by creating and using the following import_map.json file.

```json
{
  "imports": {
    "react": "https://esm.sh/react@18.1.0?target=deno&pin=v85"
  }
}
```

When I run vendor with that import map there are no errors.

```
$ deno vendor --force --import-map=import_map.json example.ts
Vendored 34 modules into vendor/ directory.

To use vendored modules, specify the `--import-map` flag when invoking deno subcommands:
  deno run -A --import-map vendor/import_map.json example.ts
```

If I then try to run the code with that import map I get the error. It happens every time I run it.

```
$ deno run -A --import-map vendor/import_map.json example.ts
Check file:///home/kyle/Projects/deno/udibo/example.ts
error: Uncaught AssertionError: "data" is unexpectedly null for "https://esm.sh/v85/@types/react@18.0.11/X-ZC9yZWFjdC1kb21AMTguMS4wLHJlYWN0QDE4LjEuMA/index.d.ts".
    at <anonymous> (deno:cli/tsc/99_main_compiler.js:71:13)
```

I just found out the error will stop happening though if I run the file without the import map or the same import map as the vendor command. Then if I run the above command again with the vendor import map, it doesn't log the check message or an error, it just runs successfully. This makes me think the issue is related to checking when using vendored dependencies.

If I edit the file in any way, like adding new lines and try running with vendor/import_map.json, it does a check and the error returns.

body:
> It would be preferable to add all of these platforms at once, as this would be a breaking change

Why is it a breaking change? Adding new platform support should be semver minor.

> Deno runs on FreeBSD [with the help of some patches](https://cgit.freebsd.org/ports/tree/www/deno)

Why don't the patches also update the types?

Also, is there a reason not to try upstreaming the patches so that the support is more official? If the only blocker is having a platform in the CI, you can get pretty far (not necessarily 100% though) with QEMU in GitHub Actions.
author: cjihrig

body:
> Why is it a breaking change?

Code like this will break: https://github.com/denosaurs/plug/blob/e7763a13a70be136bab389b39313e7043f26d6d5/plug.ts#L35-L44.

Of course, we allow ourselves to e.g. add new optional fields to input interfaces and new mandatory fields to returned interfaces, even though these can break some awkwardly written user code with the way TS works. But this one breaks common and idiomatic code.
author: nayeemrmn

body: For what it's worth, I've seen other projects (Node.js and libuv) add support for new platforms in non-major releases. I think the reasoning has been that you won't be breaking something that was previously supported.
author: cjihrig

body: This issue is about adding entries to the type definition for `Deno.build.os` _despite_ not having official support for those platforms, not about adding support for new platforms.

I do agree with supporting more values in `Deno.build.os`.

body:
`deno bundle` is not a general purpose bundle. It's intent it to make single file JavaScript files for Deno to consume. The fact that they also sometimes work in a browser is a side effect. Adding features that are not needed for running code under Deno are out of scope for this feature.

Best to use esbuild (which supports Deno) for bundling for other platforms or consumption.

body:
I also encountered the same message.
This never happened before, but I get this error when I run `deno upgrade`.

I don't know if this is caused by macOS side or deno side.

## My Environment
My macOS version is `11.6.7`.

The version of deno is as follows.

```sh
$ deno --version
deno 1.21.0 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.0.139.17
typescript 4.6.2
```

## Error message
I have not run it with `sudo`, but the message is the same.

```sh
$ deno upgrade
error: You do not have write permission to "/opt/homebrew/bin/deno"
```

## How it was solved
Fortunately I have succeeded in solving this problem.

It was an error related to permission, so I granted the permission and the problem was solved.

```sh
$ chmod 711 /opt/homebrew/bin/deno
```

In my case, this solved the problem.

```sh
$ deno upgrade
```

@duke-git  I don't know if this will solve your problem, but I hope it will be helpful.

body:
There is already a `deno check` command:

```
deno-check
Download and type-check without execution.

  deno check https://deno.land/std/http/file_server.ts

Unless --reload is specified, this command will not re-download already cached
dependencies.

USAGE:
    deno check [OPTIONS] <file>...

ARGS:
    <file>...
```

And the name `compile` is already taken up by `deno compile` https://deno.land/manual/tools/compiler

But to transpile ts to js you can use the `deno bundle` command to bundle all of the code you use in one javascript file. https://deno.land/manual/tools/bundler

body:
Also just found this in the documentation: https://deno.land/manual/node/compatibility_mode

> In the long term, we'd like to provide ability to consume TypeScript code authored for the Node runtime.

Seems Deno is already sharing the same direction, awesome. I just tried to set up a small PoC for a Deno project targeting Node, and I notice two things:
- Deno's compatibility is better than I thought. For the PoC there's no need to target Node, because Deno can run it itself.
- It's unfortunate the `deno check` and `deno bundle` commands do not support the `--compat` flag, and Deno's VS Code extension is also complaining when I use Node imports.
author: arendjr

body: Added an issue for the latter: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14846
author: arendjr

body:
> Currently, the `--compat` option is only allowed in combination with the `run` command.
> 
> I would like to extend its use so it can also be passed to the `check` and `bundle` commands. This would allow people targeting Node.js to use Deno as a `tsc` replacement that is compatible with the ESM resolution as implemented by Deno.

I'm pretty sure adding `--compat` to `deno check` won't be enough to make it a replacement for `tsc` - Deno has different built-in libraries and doesn't allow numerous options for TS configuration that are popular in Node world. 

`--compat` in `deno bundle` might make sense, but we need to discuss how it would behave - should `deno_std/node` be included in such the output bundle or not?

`--compat` is meant to allow running code authored for Node.js, but it hasn't worked in terms of type checking.
author: bartlomieju

body:
Yeah, I realize it wouldn’t be a perfect replacement yet, but I think it might be a step in the right direction, even if more work would ideally be done as a follow-up. I was creating a small PoC to see how feasible it would be to use Deno as a `tsc` replacement and I think for that small PoC, the lack of `--compat` was the main blocker, but yeah, real projects would have more requirements.

Out of curiosity, what options that `tsc` supports do you think would be most important for such a use case?

One more I was thinking of was a `--no-std` option for `deno check`. This would then disable the `Deno` namespace, which would not only be useful for people targeting Node, but also people that want to target both Deno and web with a single codebase.
author: arendjr

body:
> --compat in deno bundle might make sense, but we need to discuss how it would behave - should deno_std/node be included in such the output bundle or not?

I asked exactly this question in the draft PR :) https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/14848
author: arendjr

body:
> --compat is meant to allow running code authored for Node.js, but it hasn't worked in terms of type checking.

It actually worked for my PoC (happy to publish it, if you like), but indeed with the caveat the user is now responsible for making sure they don’t accidentally use Deno APIs in their codebase. But that’s something an additional `--no-std` option could solve.

Of course, the PoC is really trivial and I would expect additional complications once I try to use non-builtin Node dependencies. But well, one step at a time :)
author: arendjr

body: This would be very useful to our use case. Many third-party libraries are not compatible with deno and it makes adoption a challenge. Some quick confirmation shows that we can run the incompatible libraries with the `--compat` flag. The next step is to be nice to bundle them so they could be shipped to deno deploy.

exports.static = require('serve-static');

Renaming `exports.static` to anything else like `exports.statik` fixes the error.

It is an actual character escape, yeah (`%20` was the specific one I used for minimal-repro)

author: brundonsmith

body:
If your module URL is `./%20.ts`, it must be imported as:
```ts
import "./%2520.ts";
```
so that it percent decodes to your actual resource name. If your write an import like:
```ts
import "./%20.ts";
```
That will import the resource at `<space>.ts`.
author: nayeemrmn

body: Would base64 encoding work for your use case? Percent encoding doesn't seem like a great idea, as URLs can already contain that and it stacks in a more confusing way. Or are they supposed to be readable?
author: nayeemrmn

body: The above solution worked. Thanks for the base64 suggestion, that might work better. Readability here is a nice-to-have but not essential, and the stacking is definitely really unfortunate. Basically I'm caching a bunch of remote modules and then importing them from disk, so it sounds like URI-encoding would require the write path and import path to be different (with the latter's basename double-encoded)
author: brundonsmith

body:
> For me it would make a lot of sense to run check in watch mode.
I think that adding check subcommand was a great idea, and running it in watch mode would provide the best development experience (at least for me).

Sure, makes sense; it should be fairly easy to implement.

> Also if I'm not mistaken it would check further edits faster, as typescript compiler is already warmed up after the first check.

Not really - to do so, we'd need to rearchitecture the `check` command for `--watch` mode - that's because, currently `--watch` literally restarts the whole subcommand that it's watching - there's no state preserved between the runs. To implement what you're suggesting we'd instead have to keep a TSC isolate running (and to do that we'd probably need to implement a new TS host or reuse one used in the `deno lsp`). @kitsonk any thoughts on this?
author: bartlomieju

author: ChristianSiegert

body:
No, it isn't the same. 
Currently I'm using Deno for creating web apps (running in the browser). I find it much easier to use Deno tooling compared to node.js. So `deno run` would not work in this case. I would like to use `deno check --watch` like a compiler (the typescript compiler), checking if everything is ok. Then I bundle the scripts using esbuild.

Even if I developed a console app (running it with deno run), using `deno check --watch` would suit me better. As I want to check my code frequently, but run it only when I'm fully finished with my modifications.
That's why I'm in favor of removing checking by default from run, and using checks mostly while developing.

body:
I would like to work on this issue.

In fact, I've already done some research and experiments. I found a few things:

- Different Deno commands handle `--watch` quite differently. I mean the way they collect files to watch.
- `deno fmt --watch` and `deno lint --watch` seem to not notice appearance of new files - https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/28437
- ~~`deno run --watch` and `deno serve --watch` have redundant `watcher_communicator.watch_paths(..)`~~ upd: tests are failing without it, so I guess it isn't redundant.

Currently I am focusing on the question: what is the proper way to collect watch files for `deno check --watch`.
author: 0x009922

body: This is the one and only reason I haven't moved my projects to deno yet. I really love ALL the features deno offers (and thank you very much for the work and effort you are putting into it) but running the compiler in watch mode (`tsc --watch`) on the side is such a powerful tool to be forced to give up.
author: blackdyedsheep

body:
> This is the one and only reason I haven't moved my projects to deno yet. I really love ALL the features deno offers (and thank you very much for the work and effort you are putting into it) but running the compiler in watch mode (`tsc --watch`) on the side is such a powerful tool to be forced to give up.

You can still use a generic watch tool to achieve a similar result:

```sh
watchexec -e ts,js,json deno check
```

body: Deno does not compile js/ts to machine code. ```deno compile``` merge the deno executable and the bundled script in a self-contained standalone binary. It does not improve performance but allow to obfuscate source and share only a standalone executable, in return the package size is bigger. Compiling is mainly for delilver a signle executable for the client, I think you should prefere ```deno run``` for local or server use as ```deno bundle``` provide the same with lighter file (if deno executable is already aviable) and whitout obfuscation.
author: JOTSR

body:
> Deno does not compile js/ts to machine code. `deno compile` merge the deno executable and the bundled script in a self-contained standalone binary. It does not improve performance but allow to obfuscate source and share only a standalone executable, in return the package size is bigger. Compiling is mainly for delilver a signle executable for the client, I think you should prefere `deno run` for local or server use as `deno bundle` provide the same with lighter file (if deno executable is already aviable) and whitout obfuscation.

I understand, but this compiled file (.exe) is understood by all operating systems and without the need for this operating system to have deno installed?
author: Theryston

body: Yes, it's like a zip containing the deno runtime and a obfuscated js bundle. When you run it, it execute internally ```deno run some_bundle.js args..```.  If you want to increase performance you can take a look to [Deno.dlopen](https://deno.land/manual/runtime/ffi_api) (platform dependent) or use wasm (platform independent). It is also possible to use [V8 snapshot with deno core](https://denolib.gitbook.io/guide/advanced/interaction-with-v8) (AOT js compiling), it's reduce startup time but increase execution time because code can't be reoptimized, and it's complex to use and platform dependent.

Actionable items:

- [x] Flatten op arguments. Deserializing `serde_v8` struct keys is 18% overhead! https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15723
- [ ] `TypedArrayPrototypeSlice` is 17% overhead, why do we need to do that? It also seems to trigger GC.
- [ ] `TypedArrayPrototypeSlice` deopt also causes a large `memmove` with extra 15% overhead. Maybe unrelated to 2.
- [ ] `String::NewFromTwoByte` is 2% overhead. Use v8 external string resource for `U16String` type (above a certain length threashold)
