author: billywhizz

body:
Closing as no longer reproducible — the `TextDecoder#decode` regression has been fixed by subsequent optimizations.

The original report blamed array destructuring in `TextDecoder#decode` for a ~6.6x slowdown (8.7s → 57.6s for 500,001 CSV lines). That code path has since been rewritten with dedicated fast paths, notably:

- #16593 — `perf(web): optimize single pass utf8 decoding`
- #33674 — `perf(ext/web): add hyper-fast path for TextDecoder.decode`

`ext/web/08_text_encoding.js` now has a hyper-fast single-pass UTF-8 path plus a Uint8Array fast path that skips full BufferSource validation, and the array destructuring the report pointed at is gone.

Benchmarking the exact hot path the deno-csv `CSVReader` exercises (500,001 small streaming `decode(..., { stream: true })` calls) on current canary (deno 2.8.1):

- streaming small-chunk `decode({ stream: true })`: **107 ms**
- non-streaming `decode()`: **36 ms**

The decode work that previously accounted for tens of seconds now takes ~0.1s, so there is no measurable slowdown left to attribute to `TextDecoder`.
author: bartlomieju

body:
This behaviour has always existed, but #14836 changed it to automatically add an "importMap" entry to the the deno.json file. If you want to get around this behaviour then you need to specify a different import map file or manually remove the "importMap" entry from the deno.json file. To help make this less annoying, I've opened #14998 which will ignore the import map instead of erroring in the future.

I'm marking this as a bug in order to improve the behaviour here, but once fixed it still won't be possible to use an import map in the output directory. Unfortunately it's kind of tricky to get it to work where the output directory is part of the input for the output directory.

That is just faking a custom cache into the same directory. Thats not a solution, its the same problem like with my redundant cache. The maintainer of that plugin has to monitor all changes to the deno cache forever and keep their fork in sync with it. That will never work out for long.

That library is maintained by the Deno team and is a preferred solution to interacting with Deno cache. We do update that library if there are changes to the API.

> Also that extensions concept is only working if the location of DENO_DIR is correctly determined (which is not always the case) and writable from your deno app. For example this does not work on denos own [deploy](https://deno.com/deploy).

body: Because it does not use the deno cache files, it tries to create its own folder inside DENO_DIR and then caches its files there. So its a separate cache within its own folders created inside DENO_DIR.

author: allanpaiste

body:
This works as of Deno 2.8.1. You can now import a JSON module purely as a type with no import attribute:

```ts
import type * as t from "./a.json";
// or
import type Data from "./a.json";
```

`deno check`, `deno lint`, and `deno run` all accept this — the original "Expected a JavaScript or TypeScript module, but identified a Json module" error and the `no-assert-type` lint warning are both gone. Import attributes can't be combined with `import type` (TS2857), but you no longer need them in this case.

One caveat worth noting: the type-only form resolves to `any` rather than the JSON's inferred structure. If you want the precise structural type, use a regular import with an attribute (the type import is elided at runtime anyway, but a value import does require read permission):

```ts
import Data from "./a.json" with { type: "json" }; // typed as the JSON's actual shape
```

Closing as resolved. Please open a new issue if the `any` resolution for type-only JSON imports is a problem for your use case.

body:
The LSP reflects the `Deno-X-Warning` header as sent by a server, unmodified.  This means it is open for other registries/servers to provide different information if they wish.  The LSP also provides additional information, about what is actually being resolved to.

The CLI prints the warning one time at the point it is cached.

While I can understand it can be confusing, if modules were re-cached it would be true potentially again.  It is hard to get a balance between 100% accuracy in all situations and providing information to the user that is actionable.  Because the CLI displays it at the point of caching only once, I feel "latest" is the most informative and has been this way for an extended period of time.

So at this point in time I feel this issue is unactionable.

body:
I downloaded latest version of Deno, 1.25.3, and was able to reproduce the issue.

If you'd like to reproduce it yourself, here are the steps:

1. Obtain an M1 or M2 computer.
2. Clone https://github.com/okTurtles/chel
3. Run: `deno task build && deno task compile`
4. Unzip the two `*-apple-darwin.tar.gz` files in `dist/`
5. Run `time` on the two binaries. Note that as stated in the issue, the first time you run `time` the x86 version will be slower, but on subsequent runs it will remain faster.

```
$ time ./dist/aarch64-apple-darwin/chel version
1.1.2
./dist/aarch64-apple-darwin/chel version  0.03s user 0.02s system 7% cpu 0.695 total
$ time ./dist/x86_64-apple-darwin/chel version 
1.1.2
./dist/x86_64-apple-darwin/chel version  0.03s user 0.02s system 1% cpu 2.771 total
$ time ./dist/aarch64-apple-darwin/chel version
1.1.2
./dist/aarch64-apple-darwin/chel version  0.03s user 0.01s system 23% cpu 0.178 total
$ time ./dist/x86_64-apple-darwin/chel version

body:
For the sake of getting these existing `.d.ts` files to not block checking, we probably should ignore TS2306. But our current behaviour does make sense. The `.d.ts` file is written in the cjs way so it doesn't have any esm exports,
- If it did have esm exports you wouldn't get that error.
- If you import it for side-effects without referencing any undeclared exported symbols or default, turns out you also wouldn't get that error.

In 1.22.3, you can see that `chai` is typed `any` in:
```ts
import chai from "https://cdn.skypack.dev/chai@4.3.4?dts";
```
because the typing for that import is broken.

Edit: Actually our behaviour is flawed. If you had an empty JS module, with an empty `.d.ts` header file, and namespace imported it, this should be valid but would yield this error.

body: I am in favour of keeping the current default, because it matches the quote default for strings in `deno fmt`.
author: lucacasonato

body: I don't think we need to add another option here. For testing purposes it seems easy enough to add a reusable function to do the comparison that handles the difference between the runtimes. I think there are probably more areas where they differ too though.
author: dsherret

body:
Thank you both for commenting on this issue.

I do understand the appeal of keeping the default as is, in correspondence with the `deno fmt` choice.

But the choice of quote priority is quite variant across code bases (and languages). Besides the default NodeJS choice, there are large JS code bases (Google, Angular, ...) which state that "good practice" is using single quotes when possible (while avoiding internal escapes). Given the different priorities in the wild, I believe that being able to choose would be beneficial. 

Visual accessibility would also trend toward using single quotes because double quotes are more visibly noisy than single quotes, especially in empty strings and in large batches, as you would commonly see when inspecting variables.

I also think it would be much easier to add a customization option to `Deno.InspectOptions` than to design a function that normalized the output of `Deno.inspect()` and NodeJS `inspect()`. Just dealing with the corner cases seems like a significant complexity jump over the, IMO, simple addition of a `quotePriority` array. And I don't think that adding that option would impact the complexity of the Deno `inspect()` function much at all. It could be implemented quite simply and clearly from the current implementation with just a few lines of code.

Thanks for the consideration.

author: CalvinWilkinson

body:
This is fixed by #34679 (merged), which addressed the same root cause and closed the distilled duplicate #21508 ("WebAssembly streaming API fails with inspector").

The mechanism matches exactly: deno_emit loads its compiler via `WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming(fetch(...))`. Attaching the inspector caused V8 to re-create the `WebAssembly` object and reset the isolate-wide streaming callback to its `--wasm-test-streaming` testing callback, which only accepts a `BufferSource` - so the streaming call with a `Response` failed with exactly `WebAssembly.compile(): Argument 0 must be a buffer source`. The fix installs the streaming callback natively at isolate creation so it survives inspector attach (and `node:vm` context creation), and drops the `--wasm-test-streaming` flag.

(The secondary note about the process hanging on exit under `--inspect-brk` is just normal inspector behavior - it keeps the session alive - not part of this bug.)

Closing as resolved.

body:
https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12653 is not related. It is related to FFI, which is inherently unsound.

> It is not safe to create &mut [u8] which is pointing to uninitialized memory.

Correct, which is why we don't do that. We create a fixed size uninitialized contiguous chunk of memory, then initialize it, and only once it is initialize it treat it as safe. Did you take a look at the `SAFETY` comments on top of the relevant code?

Same goes for cases of `uninit_vec`.

I am going to close this issue, because it is not actionable. You are pointing out `unsafe` blocks that are already marked with safety comments with explanations, or clippy ignores with explanations. If you have actionable feedback for how to turn some of this unsafe code safe, please comment here, or create new issues.

author: aapoalas

body:
> The `SAFETY` comments are **wrong**. They are not safe. I think this issue should be left open until they are fixed. Does `deno` follow the rules how rust treats unsoundness? Or it has its own rules?

Can you point what is wrong with these comments?
author: bartlomieju

body:
> Can you point what is wrong with these comments?

It is not safe to create `&mut [u8]` which is pointing to uninitialized memory. 
See also:
+ <https://doc.rust-lang.org/nomicon/uninitialized.html>
+ <https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/std/ptr/macro.addr_of_mut.html>
+ <https://github.com/rust-lang/unsafe-code-guidelines/issues/71>

[clippy::uninit_vec](https://rust-lang.github.io/rust-clippy/master/#uninit_vec):
> It creates a Vec with uninitialized data, which leads to undefined behavior with most safe operations. Notably, uninitialized Vec<u8> must not be used with generic Read.
> Moreover, calling set_len() on a Vec created with new() or default() creates out-of-bound values that lead to heap memory corruption when used.

And the last one:
> // SAFETY: not fully safe, since lifetimes are detached from original scope
author: Nugine

body: Thanks, if you'd like to contribute and fix these problem that would be highly appreciated.
author: bartlomieju

body:
FWIW https://github.com/rust-lang/unsafe-code-guidelines/issues/71 is about *by-value* integers/floats.
This here also involves references, so https://github.com/rust-lang/unsafe-code-guidelines/issues/77 is relevant, too.

Indeed `&mut [u8]` pointing to uninit data (as it is created e.g. [here](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/4e92f38d2ced6cbc2c7ed13d79d739dd4ddadb4c/serde_v8/magic/bytestring.rs#L61), I think) is wrong according to [the Reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/behavior-considered-undefined.html). However Miri accepts such code because it does not "look behind" an `&mut` when checking whether everything is properly initialized. It would still be better to avoid this situation, but this is much less critical than having a by-value uninitialized integer/float.

body:
Closing this out — the substantive items here have been resolved, and the codebase has moved on considerably since this was filed (deno_core is now vendored back under `libs/`, and rusty_v8 gained `write_utf8_uninit_v2` / `write_one_byte_v2`).

Status of each item:

- **`ops_metrics.rs`** (`&mut` from `&` via pointer cast) — gone; the metrics dispatch was rewritten.
- **`serde_v8/de.rs`** (`&mut [u8]` over uninitialized `Vec`) — fixed; it now writes into `buf.spare_capacity_mut()` (`&mut [MaybeUninit<u8>]`) via `write_utf8_uninit_v2` and only calls `set_len` afterward.
- **`ops_tls.rs`** (transmute on buffer slices) — gone.
- **`serde_v8/magic/value.rs`** (lifetime-detaching transmute) — still present, but intentional and localized.
- **`bytestring.rs` / `u16string.rs`** (`set_len` before writing) — still present, but now deliberate, with an explicit `#[allow(clippy::uninit_vec)]` and a SAFETY note. These could be migrated to the same `spare_capacity_mut()` pattern `de.rs` uses if anyone wants to eliminate the last `&mut`-over-uninit spot — worth a focused, separate issue/PR rather than this broad 2022 tracker.

Thanks for the original write-up — it drove real fixes.

body:
> You need to clear those out manually each time.

I don't need two clear the things manually each time. Take a look at [ESCover](https://github.com/coderaiser/escover) it produces one file for coverage: `coverage/lcov.info`, this is standard that come from first implementation of [lcov](https://github.com/linux-test-project/lcov) and supported by services like [coveralls](https://coveralls.io/) and any other and just rewrite it on each run. This is as simple :).

The format of `lcov` is also very simple and it can be used for reporting as well, and all this garbage produced by `V8` just don't needed, and should be collected :) (and removed before run).

> Please provide an example code that caused this panic.

It disappear, I can't reproduce it anymore. The thing is **Deno** begged me so much about reporting the bug, so I could not hold on.

body:
We're storing all of deno's app data in %LOCALAPPDATA%:

```
> deno info
DENO_DIR location: C:\Users\david\AppData\Local\deno
Remote modules cache: C:\Users\david\AppData\Local\deno\deps
Emitted modules cache: C:\Users\david\AppData\Local\deno\gen
Language server registries cache: C:\Users\david\AppData\Local\deno\registries
Origin storage: C:\Users\david\AppData\Local\deno\location_data
```

I don't see anything in those policies that suggests a program's executable files should be in `ProgramData` or `AppData`.

Many binaries on Windows go to `~/.<app-name>` and my home directory is full of them. If you want a different install directory you can set the `DENO_INSTALL` environment variable.
author: dsherret

body:
I'm going to close this one. As mentioned, we're storing all of deno's app data in local app data already. Also, we're not part of the Win 10 Desktop App Certification Program.

It's nice how we install our app the same way on every operating system ($HOME/.deno) so we can tell people to go there without having to say some operating specific thing. There are also many tools such as rust that install to a dot folder in the home directory. Also, again, if you really don't like this then you can specify the `DENO_INSTALL` environment variable before installing.

body:
This probably belongs in the [discussions](https://github.com/denoland/deno/discussions) section, as it's a question an not really a bug report / issue. That said i'll do my best to try to answer!
> Is deno ecosystem production ready now?

This depends on what your requirements are for production software, but Deno's been stable since v1.0, and there are plenty of [3rd party modules](https://deno.land/x) for things like db drivers, web frameworks, etc.

> Is there a git-ish registry like [cdnjs/cdnjs](https://github.com/cdnjs/cdnjs)?

Not sure what you mean by "git-ish", but Deno supports ES module imports, so you can host it on a cdn and import it via the url.

> Is there a cheat sheet, cover famous nodejs library's deno equivalents, or high lighted no equivalent, like koa to oak, listed in style like [rosetta](https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/Pacman/Rosetta)?

I don't think the Deno team has created anything like this, but if you search the third party deps, discussions & discord server, you could probably easily figure out if there's a Deno equivalent for any given node package. There's also a [compatibility layer](https://deno.land/manual/node) for running node projects in Deno.

> Is there a Hot-Module-Replacement guide?

There's `deno run --watch`, but I don't think there's hot module replacement. Looks like there's a tracking issue (#8327) for it though.

> Is there a way to alter partial dependency code, without effect other projects?

Not sure what you mean by this, sorry.

Hope this was helpful!
author: zachauten

body:
What version of Deno are you using?  It isn't reproducible with main and using `--no-check` with older versions of Deno would work around the issue.

This isn't specifically a problem Deno, it has more to do with how `esm.sh` and the package itself are handling types in an unsupported way.  Specifically `assemblyscript.generated.d.ts` tries to import a bunch of types like `types:assemblyscript/src/glue/js/index` which without an import gets translated as a local file, and therefore the error is correct.

body:
`import.meta.resolve` was standardized in the WHATWG HTML spec as a **single-argument, synchronous** function that returns a string, resolved against the current module. Deno implements exactly that, as do browsers and Node.

The optional second `parentURL` argument requested here was an experimental Node-only feature that was **removed** when Node aligned with the standard, so it isn't part of the API any runtime exposes today. Implementing it in Deno would now mean diverging from the standard rather than matching it.

Since the standardized form is fully supported and the second argument is intentionally not part of the spec, closing as not planned. Thanks!

body:
I got it: deno cache just downloads the files, in this case the file had a CRLF line ending, this was calculated into the hash.
After `git clone` this file's line ending was changed to LF and the hash doesn't match anymore...
author: andreas-wagner

body:
For anyone running into this issue as well, here's the fix:
```sh
> cat .gitattributes
# Use Unix line endings in all text files.
* text=auto eol=lf

# Never touch the cache, otherwise the hashes won't match anymore.
.deno_cache/** -text
```

body:
Turns out since the error is passed through the JS boundary, the original error obviously can't be accessed with `get_custom_error_class` anymore.

I feel like the most reasonable solution would likely be to set this error during startup in some way? This might not be the only deno CLI specific error though, so I'm not sure if a wider-reaching solution is necessary.
author: cd-work

body:
The original blocker here, not being able to detect the permission error after it crossed the JS boundary, is resolved. Permission errors now carry a stable error class, `NotCapable`:

```rust
#[class("NotCapable")]
pub struct PermissionDeniedError { ... }
```

So an embedder can catch the `NotCapable` class and render its own message instead of the default one, which is the practical way to avoid showing CLI-specific text in a custom runtime.

The default message does still reference `--allow-<name>` (it's tailored to the Deno CLI / `deno compile`), and there isn't a global hook to template that wording for embedders. But given embedders can already substitute their own messaging via the `NotCapable` class, and this is a niche custom-runtime concern with no further demand since 2022, I'm closing it.

If making the default flag wording embedder-configurable is something you specifically need, please open a focused issue describing the desired API.

author: StoneCypher

body:
While fixing swc to not use this much stack for such parsers is the arguable correct fix, I believe Deno can can avoid this (and other stack related crashes) on Windows pretty easily.

On Windows the main thread has a maximum stack size of 1 MiB by default, while Linux and MacOS will let main thread to grow up to 8MiB by default (controlled by ulimit). There are some differences with respect to spawning other threads on each platform, but all let you specify a custom maximum stack size. Threads spawned by Rust code will use a maximum stack size of 2MiB by default, regardless of the OS default, but this does not apply to the main thread.

Increasing the default stack size on Windows is possible as a linker flag. Increasing the default to 8MiB would bring Windows in line with Linux and MacOS's default ulimit and would not be unreasonable. While window's strict memory accounting often makes it act differently than Linux, for this purpose Windows actually acts more like most Linux allocations do (explained later), and thus this bigger size will not make the program use more memory, unless/until the stack is used enough to grow to that size.

The only real downside here is that changing the default stack size will also change the default maximum stack size for threads created by non-rust code, but that really is not an issue, as it is not memory but only reserved virtual address space. For x64 this really only means a few extra page table entries are created per natively created thread. In a 32 bit program the extra address space reserved could potentially be a concern, but Deno only supports x64 on windows anyway, and even if it did, it would need many dozens of threads to realistically be an issue.

Lastly Deno is already increasing the stack size for debug builds on Windows (see `.cargo/config.toml`), so switching to an 8MiB  default stack reservation size for all Windows builds instead of only debug builds is actually a simplification, and would avoid a bunch of cases where things can crash from stack limits on Windows while working on Linux and MacOS.

---
More about how stack works on Windows:

While Windows' strict memory accounting normally makes it work differently from Linux, for stack size windows actually behaves similarly to most allocations in Linux, where a larger maximum stack size does not make it use more memory unless the stack grows that big.

This is because Windows only reserves the address space, for the full stack, but does not initially map it to memory (which requires "committing" it). Instead it commits memory as the stack grows, which does mean that the program can crash with a stack overflow before reaching the max stack size, but only if Windows does not have enough physical memory plus swap to allow for more committed memory.  To allow programs to be sure they won't crash from not enough stack, Windows offers a second value that determines the minimum committed stack size.

When creating other threads on windows, you can specify a minimum committed stack size to override the default, and if this minimum is greater than the default maximum, the thread will be created with a larger maximum to accommodate. You can instead use the `STACK_SIZE_PARAM_IS_A_RESERVATION` flag when creating a thread to set the maximum stack size for a new thread, without forcing that memory to be fully committed. (Rust does this when creating threads.)

Hope this helps.
author: KevinCathcart

body:

author: StoneCypher

body:
I've heard that even with setting the stack size limit to unlimited on macOS, it will limit it at 64 MB for the main thread. Windows does not have any such unlimited concept for OS managed stacks. On Linux, if ulimit for stacks is set to unlimited, the main thread stack can continue to grow indefinitely, until to runs into part of the address space already being used (whether by malloc, mmaped files, the stack for some other thread, etc). 

It might theoretically be possible to have MacOS and Windows use a userspace managed stack instead of a kernel managed stack. In that case, it could be allowed to grow to an indeterminate size not unlike Linux's kernel manage stack can if the default limit is turned off, but that involves some rather hairy low level memory management code that I'm not so sure that the Deno team would want.

The main culprit of stack issues seems to be swc (https://github.com/swc-project/swc). swc uses a recursive descent parser implemented with recursion, which inherently means there will be some limit to the depth it can reach with any fixed stack size. I cannot see them changing this approach. They could probably reduce how much stack is used by each level of nesting, which may become enough for realistically sized parsers. Otherwise they would probably need to do something like use https://github.com/rust-lang/stacker to implement segmented stacks in the parser, which is how the rust compiler (rustc) avoids this very same problem.

Bumping the windows stack size is something the deno team can pretty easily do on their own. Fixing the problem for possible every possible parser is something the swc team would need to do. I'd suggest filing a bug over there that points to this bug. If you know of a PEG.js parser that generates output with a lot more nesting, it might not be a bad idea to link that too, so they can test against that too.

author: dirkluijk

body:
This appears to be fixed on current Deno (verified with `deno 2.7.14`). `deno compile` honors `compilerOptions.emitDecoratorMetadata` from `deno.json` and bakes the resulting `Reflect.metadata(...)` calls into the embedded sources.

Repro confirming both directions:

`deno.json`:
```json
{ "compilerOptions": { "experimentalDecorators": true, "emitDecoratorMetadata": true } }
```

`main.ts`:
```ts
import "npm:reflect-metadata";
function Decorate(_t: object, _k: string) {}
class Foo { @Decorate bar(x: number, y: string): boolean { return x > 0 && y.length > 0; } }
console.log(Reflect.getMetadata("design:paramtypes", Foo.prototype, "bar"));
```

- `deno compile -A --output app main.ts && ./app` → `[ [Function: Number], [Function: String] ]` ✅
- Same code, **no** `deno.json` (compiled with `--no-check`) → `undefined` ❌

So the config IS being read at compile time. I also verified end-to-end with `npm:tsyringe` (DI resolution works in the compiled binary).

For reference, the relevant wiring in current `main`:
- `cli/tools/compile.rs:398-402` — fetches `transpile_options` from `compiler_options_resolver`
- `libs/resolver/deno_json.rs:1753-1759` — maps `experimental_decorators` + `emit_decorator_metadata` from `deno.json` into `deno_ast::TranspileOptions`
- These flow into `eszip::EszipV2::from_graph`, so the transpiled sources embedded in the binary carry `Reflect.metadata(...)` calls

@bartlomieju — looks safe to close. Happy to add a spec test in `tests/specs/compile/` if you'd like to lock the behavior in.

body: Another solution is to call `Isolate::memory_pressure_notification(v8::MemoryPressureLevel::Moderate);` outright
author: bartlomieju

body:
This is implemented. On Linux, Deno installs a memory-trim handler (`runtime/worker.rs`, `runtime/web_worker.rs`) that responds to `SIGUSR2` by calling `v8::Isolate::low_memory_notification()`, triggering the aggressive synchronous GC this issue asked for. It's opt-in via the `DENO_USR2_MEMORY_TRIM` environment variable and applies to both the main worker and web workers.

The open question here was *when/how to know to call it*. Rather than have Deno try to detect OS memory pressure from inside the process (the hard part discussed above), the decision is externalized: an outside memory-pressure monitor or orchestrator signals the process with `SIGUSR2`, and Deno performs the trim in response. Closing as resolved.

The specific bug that I raised this issue for, related to detaching the buffer with the serialized message on the original thread, was fixed in #15133 for the case of workers. That is still an issue for `BroadcastChannel`, but it's more complicated to resolve (see https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/15129#issuecomment-1179555584).

This remaining `BroadcastChannel` bug should not affect `postMessage` on workers, though. And while there does seem to be a memory leak, the serialized buffers do seem to be being detached, at least on the sending thread. So that memory leak is not due to this bug.

author: sevapp

body:
The original issue covered workers, MessagePort, and BroadcastChannel. Workers and MessagePort were fixed in #15133 by switching to `DetachedBuffer`, which transfers ownership without copying.

The remaining problem is **BroadcastChannel only**. In `ext/web/broadcast_channel.rs`:
- `buf.to_vec()` copies the buffer on send (line 90)
- `Vec::clone(&message.data)` copies again per receiver (line 189-194)

Due to BroadcastChannel's 1-to-n nature, at least (n-1) copies are unavoidable, but the initial send-side copy could potentially be eliminated by taking ownership of the buffer directly. Narrowing this issue to the BroadcastChannel case.
author: bartlomieju

body:
Summary of where this stands today.

The original report covered workers, MessagePort, and BroadcastChannel. Workers and MessagePort were fixed in #15133 by switching to `DetachedBuffer`, which transfers the serialized buffer's ownership to the single receiver without copying (those are 1:1, so transfer semantics apply).

That leaves BroadcastChannel, and looking at the current code most of the avoidable cost is already gone:

- Fan-out is already zero-copy: `InMemoryChannelMessage.data` is `Arc<Vec<u8>>` (`ext/web/broadcast_channel.rs`), so the tokio broadcast clone per subscriber is just an Arc bump, not a memcpy per receiver.
- The serialize (once, on the sender) and deserialize (once per receiving isolate) are fundamentally unavoidable: isolates don't share a heap, so a structured clone must re-materialize the bytes in every receiver. That's the floor for one-to-many cross-isolate messaging.

The only copy left to shave is the sender-side `buf.to_vec()` in `op_broadcast_send` (one memcpy of the serialized blob out of V8 into the Arc'd Vec). It could in principle be removed by detaching the source ArrayBuffer's backing store and Arc-wrapping that allocation, but it's blocked by the same-isolate path: `dispatch()` defers `core.deserialize(data)` into queued tasks that hold a view over the buffer, so it can't be detached until those run. Removing it would require deserializing same-isolate listeners eagerly (per listener, to keep independent clones) and changing the channel message to carry a detached backing store instead of `Vec<u8>`.

Net: this is a modest, large-payload-only optimization (one memcpy per send, scaling with message size, no benefit for small messages) with real complexity and a subtle eager-deserialize semantics change. The headline issue (transferable buffers for workers/MessagePort) is resolved, and the BroadcastChannel remainder isn't worth keeping a long-lived issue open for. Closing for now; happy to revisit with a focused issue if a large-payload BroadcastChannel workload makes the remaining `to_vec()` worth eliminating.

body: Correct.  We do not generally support ambient module declarations, as the break the model of tightly coupling code to types.  That being said, there is already limited support, discussed further down in manual at: https://deno.land/manual/typescript/types#using-ambient-or-global-types

author: espindola

body:
> For that scenario the way to do it is to use `@deno-types`

In front-end development, it's common for frameworks to use files with non-standard extensions (`.css`, `.vue`, `.svelte`) the idea often being that compilers will turn them into proper js imports of known type during the build step. Thus, during development, we can use `declare module *.css` to type all css imports, for example. Using `@deno-types` would require to prepend every import which isn't really user friendly. IMHO, the lack of `declare module`, or any other similarly succinct way to support this use case, unnecessarily limits Deno's usability as full stack dev platform.

It's worth noting that it's not as easy as treating every javascript file as typescript. See https://github.com/tc39/proposal-type-annotations#omitted-typescript-specific-features-that-generate-code 

> Omitted: TypeScript-specific features that generate code 
> [...]
> - [Enums](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/enums.html)
> - [Namespaces](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/namespaces.html)
> - [Parameter properties](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/classes.html#parameter-properties)

author: identor

body: The type annotations proposal applies to the JS spec and would be enabled by default for `.js` files when it exists. TS is a different language, it doesn't make sense to apply it to `.js` files and the type annotations proposal has no bearing on that.

There is an optional `sortText` in the completion item as well as the `label` (see: https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specifications/lsp/3.17/specification/#textDocument_completion).  Vscode sorts first by `sortText` if present and then `label`.

body: It should be noted that high resolution time is not available by default in Deno due to security issues and requires a permission flag. But console should log the partial ms if available, IMO.

body: Even with -A option, and with performance.now() returning with precision, console.time is still limited to millisecond precision.

It's okay for the `JsRuntime` to crash here, but it's not okay for it to take out the Rust host process with it. 

I'm running Deno within a server which crashes with the following output:
`Main child exited with signal (with signal 'Trace/breakpoint trap')`

Is there a way for Deno to catch the trap signal and expose it to the Rust side as an `Error`?

It is likely a type dependency somewhere in `esm.sh` includes an import of a bare specifier.  With `deno run`, even with type checking, will usually be silent when trying to deal with this, as it will simply not resolve the type definition and the type checker will treat the import as `any`.

Thanks, I can reproduce it with `--inspect=` and `--inspect-wait=` as well. The problem seems to be that the placeholders in the completions file are delimited with colons and somehow having a colon in the name is not parsed correctly. Currently the line in the completions file for `--inspect` is:

Changing/escaping or removing the colon in `HOST:PORT` fixes it, e.g. `:HOST\:PORT:`. Ultimately I think this might be an issue in `clap` but Deno can probably fix it by using different placeholder values [in Arg(...).value_name](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/cli/args/flags.rs#L1956)

body:
Still reproduces on Deno 2.8.1 (V8 14.9), but this is an upstream V8 inspector limitation that Deno can't fix on its own — it reproduces in Chrome too, as the OP noted.

I verified the current behavior over the DevTools protocol (set `Debugger.setPauseOnExceptions: "uncaught"`, resume, watch for `Debugger.paused`):

- `await (async () => { await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 1)); throw new Error("oh no"); })()` — only the break-on-start pause fires; the debugger does **not** pause on the uncaught rejection.
- The same throw **without** the intervening `setTimeout` await does pause (`reason: promiseRejection`), matching the original report.

So the pause is lost specifically when the rejection comes from a top-level await whose async function suspended on a real async task before throwing. The underlying V8/Chromium issue (crbug.com/1345097) was closed as "Won't Fix (Obsolete)" under the tracker's slow-close policy, so this isn't something we can resolve downstream.

Workaround remains: enable "pause on caught exceptions" as well (at the cost of breaking on exceptions internal to Deno).

Closing as an upstream limitation; happy to revisit if V8 fixes the inspector behavior.

A small addendum to my "successful strategies" above: you can use the Skypack version with typings much more simply than what I posted originally. No `@deno-types` directive is necessary if you just import lodash like so:

```typescript
import _ from "https://cdn.skypack.dev/lodash?dts";
```

This gives full functionality as well as TypeScript interfaces very simply. It would probably be nice for this to be documented somewhere discoverable though, to save others from having to go through this investigation work.

body:
> I think the https://deno.land/x landing page would be a great place to list other resources like Skypack.

Well `deno.land/x` is the third party module registry, versus a place to learn how to use Deno.  Could be though that we would link off to parts of the manual about using 3rd party modules, maybe specifically "if you are looking to use an existing npm package under Deno..."

> I would also consider if it makes sense for all of these quirky lodash versions to be removed from deno.land/x.

That sort of runs contrary to the intent of deno.land/x, where it is up to the community to publish modules.  We are currently working on dramatically improving it.  The list sorted by GitHub stars is not really a good way of prioritising the usefulness of packages in Deno.  We are [re-working the scoring](https://github.com/denoland/website_feedback/issues/4) and [here is a current priority list](https://old-cow-65.deno.dev/) based on actual popular usage in Deno. We hope to get it on the page very soon. As you can see, it gives you a totally different view of 3rd party modules for Deno.

> Note: I didn't realize that page about Deno-friendly CDNs existed in the documentation. It's entirely possible that this is a me problem rather than a documentation problem, but I would think others probably follow a similar pattern for discovery. 🤷

Also there is: https://deno.land/manual/node/cdns.  It certainly about discovery.  It is also sort of the low friction nature of things too.  You just import a URL, it is just really hard to tell what URL gives you a good experience or not.
author: kitsonk

body:
That all makes sense, thanks for the attention!

> Well deno.land/x is the third party module registry, versus a place to learn how to use Deno.

I guess my point was that, despite having read a lot of the Deno documentation multiple times, I had still barely ever seen any host besides deno.land used for imports in Deno. It would be nice if there were more ways to make people aware of these other options without having to know exactly where to look. Part of the reason I never read the "Packages from CDNs" page is because the connotation I took from that title was that it would be about optimizing serving speed. A name like "Package repositories" might have made me think it would be more helpful for my case.

A few other possibly-related documentation pages where I'd have been much more likely to see this:
* https://deno.land/manual/linking_to_external_code
* https://deno.land/manual/examples/import_export
* https://deno.land/manual/getting_started/first_steps (or somewhere else in the Getting Started section)

But again, thanks for the attention on this. Finding the right packages is hard, and I was just surprised by how long it took me to figure this out, but it is much better documented than I realized and works great. I'm just adding all of these thoughts on the chance that it's helpful for any future changes.

body:
I don't think it's that much of an improvement in DX because very often repos have different commands for development, production, or something else.... so I'd still need to go into a repo and find out what convention they're using for "first command" before running it or ensure they're following whatever the recommended convention is. So I think going into a repo and finding out what those commands are by typing `deno task` is nice to have and removing that would be a step backwards. #14039 would be a nice improvement and reduce keystrokes.

With `make` it feels normal because the command is called `make`... `deno task` doesn't have that same feel to me personally. I think in the JS ecosystem `npm run`'s behaviour has greater precedence for prior art.
author: dsherret

body:
> I don't think it's that much of an improvement in DX because very often repos have different commands for development, production, or something else.... so I'd still need to go into a repo and find out what convention they're using for "first command" before running it or ensure they're following whatever the recommended convention is.

But you're probably going to repeatedly run the command for a repo you're familiar with more often than you'd switch between repos. Not needing to specify _on every invocation_ an arbitrary keyword specific to each project alleviates a lot of cognitive tax, even if you still have to verify once per project that the main task is what it should be (i.e. start dev server).

> #14039 would be a nice improvement and reduce keystrokes.

Well with shell completions implemented, you can regardless see a list of tasks by typing `deno task [tab][tab]` which largely supersedes the current behaviour. Or to pretty print with descriptions, I'm only proposing to move that to `deno task -l` not remove it. And for example I'm in the habit of instead using `code deno.json` so I can also glance over what commands are actually run. The point being that, listing the available tasks has too many alternative syntaxes to take the valuable spot it currently does, whereas a shortcut to "run the main task" has very few priorly-seen alternatives if any.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
I'm not so sure about having a default task that runs just by typing `deno task`. If we really want a default task kind of operation, then maybe do it similar to NPM where you have `npm run [task]` or `npm start`, where `npm start` just automatically runs the `start` task instead.

Or maybe we allow the tasks to be called similar to cargo plugins, where you just say `cargo edit` like it's a cargo subcommand, even though it's a plugin.

So if you had a `make` task you could run `deno make` and if you had a `run` task, you would have to run `deno task run` because it conflicts with the built-in `deno run` command.
author: zicklag

body:
> I'm not so sure about having a default task that runs just by typing `deno task`. If we really want a default task kind of operation, then maybe do it similar to NPM where you have `npm run [task]` or `npm start`, where `npm start` just automatically runs the `start` task instead.
> 
> [...] if you had a `make` task you could run `deno make` and if you had a `run` task, you would have to run `deno task run` because it conflicts with the built-in `deno run` command.

In yarn, the behavior of `(yarn | npm) run` is slightly different, and I believe it would increase productivity.
[https://classic.yarnpkg.com/lang/en/docs/cli/run/#toc-yarn-run-script](https://classic.yarnpkg.com/lang/en/docs/cli/run/#toc-yarn-run-script)

When running `yarn run {script}` the behavior is almost identical to that of npm. However, similar to what @zicklag proposed, `yarn start` will run a task named `start`, any arguments passed going straight to the task without the need for `-- {flags}` is a task is not found with the name of the phrase you pass after `yarn {your-phrase-goeth-hereth}` yarn returns the same error {TODO}

If you have tasks with the following names:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/79616833/193040959-bd458d20-20f8-4e86-9c81-661d40abae66.png)

The different behaviors or yarn are as so:
- If you run `yarn run`, yarn asks you what script to run, ignoring the script named `run`. if you wanted the `run` script to be executed, you would need to specify `yarn run run` as this conflicts with an internal yarn command. 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/79616833/193041273-8d78dcfb-d739-4d0a-a68c-6354fcf2542c.png)

- If you run `yarn start`, it runs the `start` script, quintessentially the same as running `yarn run start`. This is the expected behavior of a script whose name does not conflict with an internal yarn command.

I think that we could do something similar for `deno task`, receiving the benefits of fewer keystrokes while maintaining consistency across node-based toolings. This will be the most expected behavior of `deno task` for node users and certainly is less risk-involved than having deno run the first task in the JSON.

body:
There are two parts to this,
- `ImportMap::lookup()` doesn't account for relative map keys, and returns values that are relative to the import map rather than the referrer.
- The diagnostic should only be given when the suggested map key is a bare specifier. Applying it to relative specifiers is too opinionated as to which of the alternative import forms is preferred, and there can be conflicts/overlaps in the map key scopes that are too complicated to account for. Edit: I think I came up with good conflict resolution in https://github.com/denoland/import_map/pull/40. But nevertheless.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
I disagree that it should only be applied to bare specifiers.  If you have remapped stuff locally in an import map, having a hint diagnostic and a quick fix doesn't hurt anything.

@lucacasonato thoughts?

author: lucacasonato

body: The quick fix currently suggests replacing `./bar.ts` with `./utils/bar.ts`, which does not look good to me.
author: baoshan

body:
> If you have remapped stuff locally in an import map, having a hint diagnostic and a quick fix doesn't hurt anything.

Mappings between two non-bare URLs are niche and are typically used to bridge compatibility for modules you can't control. There's no reason to make suggestions for those.

An exception is the `"/": "./", "./": "./"` pattern like @baoshan is using. But as they mentioned on Discord, `./bar.ts` doesn't need to be replaced with `/bar.ts`. The preferred import form will be very opinionated.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
> An exception is the `"/": "./", "./": "./"` pattern like @baoshan is using. But as they mentioned on Discord, `./bar.ts` doesn't need to be replaced with `/bar.ts`. The preferred import form will be very opinionated.

Yes, but that is why the diagnostics are flagged as hints, indicating they can be updated.

The purpose and intent of flagging them for relative imports is for example if you are developing/refactoring code where you have moved your dependencies into a _vendor_ directory and you have an import applied that resolves to those.  The quick fix allows you to move quickly to bare specifiers.

Considering that, I can see how `./file.ts` or `./subdir/file.ts` might get annoying (but again, that import map is written to resolve there, it wouldn't provide the suggestions unless the import map resolves there), but retain them for `../` specifiers and root (`/`) specifiers.

> Maybe we could just remove the squiggly line, but keep the quick fix?

I can look into that, but the diagnostics were already set at the "lowest" priority and I think you need a diagnostic to be able to apply a quick fix.  Refactorings are a different story, and I could look into see if doing it as a refactoring might be better all around.
author: kitsonk

body:
> The quick fix allows you to move quickly to bare specifiers.

My proposed fix doesn't disable the diagnostic if the quick fix is a bare specifier, it only disables cases where one relative specifier would otherwise be suggested in place of another.

I came here looking for a fix to remove the little ellipsis [0] that indicates a suggestion for my relative imports, when using an import map.

My issue with it is that I don't want to use the import map in all cases, usually only when I'm importing from a higher directory.

[0] ellipsis shown beneath the first double-quote after the word `from` in an import:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/623736/184275112-6d1ebd72-689d-4f9c-9d46-04c9fe86731b.png)
author: ghost

body:
I've been enjoying the import-map remapping feature immensely since it arrived, it was a right PITA manually adjusting them before.

I thought I'd drop a use-case in here for consideration, I'd hate to lose remapping in my case just because it doesn't align with someone else's expectations of it.

I have an import map that maps some top-level folders to bare-specifiers as such...

```
{
    "imports": {
        "$app/": "./app/",
        "$lib/": "./lib/"
    }
}
```

So I have a `/lib` folder containing folders that you could consider as distinct _packages_, imported as such: `$lib/something/mod.ts`, `$lib/other/foo.ts`

VSCode will initially generate a relative import for these though, depending on depth: `../../lib/other/foo.ts` ... and the remap feature will offer to correct that for me. In this case the import was in a different _package_.

But, if the module is in the same _package_, and a relative import is generated, ie: `../foo.ts` or maybe `./foo.ts`, it still suggests the remapping.

I want the remap behaviour for the first case but not necessarily the second.

I'm thinking that a sensible heuristic to handle this would be for the remapper to consider what the current module maps to, compared to the target import, and if they have the same base then remapping could be considered unnecessary.

For example, in module `/lib/other/sub/thing.ts`, we have import from `../foo.ts`, so the remap can see that the module could map to `$lib/other/sub/thing.ts` and the import to `$lib/other/foo.ts`, so the base of the import (`$lib/other/`) matches that of the module, so don't suggest to remap.

Whereas, in module `lib/something/mod.ts`, we import from `../other/foo.ts`, where module maps to `$lib/something/mod.ts` and the import maps to `$lib/other/foo.ts`, the base doesn't match, so it suggests the remapping.

I'm not sure if such a behaviour would suit all projects, and so should probably be opt-in initially.
It may also not be desirable in test cases either, where you may want to always remap, even within the same _package_.

body:
There was a temporary regression in Deno that allowed accessing a resource synchronously while it's being used asynchronously. This is now fixed and is causing this behaviour. In this case, you can fix the issue by awaiting the `Deno.write` promise (`await Deno.write`) or use `Deno.writeSync` instead.

Example of it failing in Deno v1.19.2:

```
> deno run --unstable asdf.ts
Check file:///V:/dotcom/asdf.ts
hierror: Uncaught Busy: Resource is unavailable because it is in use by a promise
  console.log(Deno.consoleSize(Deno.stdout.rid));
                   ^
    at Object.opSync (deno:core/01_core.js:168:12)
    at Object.consoleSize (deno:runtime/js/40_tty.js:8:17)
    at file:///V:/dotcom/asdf.ts:4:20
> deno -V
deno 1.19.2
```
author: dsherret

body: Oh, so it's now working as expected, good to know. `Deno.writeSync` worked for my usecase however this limits ability to run `Deno.write` asynchronously (even when awaited) and use `consoleSize` in other asynchronous function or event callback simultaneously, is there a way around that?

The `mitata` library has a good explanation:

https://github.com/evanwashere/mitata#jit-bias

> "JIT bias". In simple words, v8 and JSC JIT expect us to pass the same function, so they optimize for it, but we break that promise and get deoptimized.
author: littledivy
pand-app

body:
Still a case of 'JIT bias', notice the `max` results for the first bench. V8 deoptimizes when it sees that the function is not what it expected. 

Also, adding two numbers is way too fast to correctly measure and in fact, you could be measuring the timer overhead itself. Try measuring something with meaningful overhead:

```js
Deno.bench("Date.now()", () => Date.now());
Deno.bench("Date.now()", () => Date.now());
```

```
cpu: Apple M1
runtime: deno 1.24.0 (aarch64-apple-darwin)

file:///bench.js
benchmark       time (avg)             (min … max)       p75       p99      p995
-------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------
Date.now()   34.02 ns/iter   (32.43 ns … 69.22 ns)  35.65 ns  51.09 ns  51.94 ns
Date.now()   34.94 ns/iter   (33.87 ns … 42.49 ns)  36.51 ns  39.75 ns  40.49 ns

body:
One of the big differences of Deno versus Node.js is that when using TypeScript and type checking, the TypeScript type checker (tsc) is loaded into memory.  We don't unload this once we are bootstrapped IIRC.  It is something we should look into.

Have you tried comparing a workload generated by `deno compile` against Node.js? The TypeScript type checker is never loaded in those cases.
author: kitsonk

body:
> One of the big differences of Deno versus Node.js is that when using TypeScript and type checking, the TypeScript type checker (tsc) is loaded into memory. We don't unload this once we are bootstrapped IIRC. It is something we should look into.
> 
> Have you tried comparing a workload generated by `deno compile` against Node.js? The TypeScript type checker is never loaded in those cases.

Thank you for your reply.

I just tested it with `deno compile`, and only made the following modifications. It is indeed half of the memory. It dropped from `21.11M` to `12.78M`.

I changed the dockerfile of oak like this:
```dockerfile
FROM denoland/deno:alpine-1.23.2

EXPOSE 8000

WORKDIR /app

# Prefer not to run as root.
RUN chown -R deno /app
RUN chmod 755 /app

ADD . .

# CMD deno run --allow-net mod.ts
RUN deno compile --allow-net -o oak mod.ts \
&& chmod +x ./oak

CMD ./oak
```
However, this leads to additional problems. 

First, using the above method to build will cause the docker image volume to increase. Of course, I can try docker's `as builder`, but I don't have a suitable Linux machine to test for the time being.

Another problem is that I found before that I use decorators in my project, but it does not use its information when using `deno compile` to build. Here is one of my test cases:
```ts
import { Reflect } from "https://deno.land/x/deno_reflect@v0.2.1/mod.ts";

function Prop(target: any, propertyKey: string) {
  const type = Reflect.getMetadata("design:type", target, propertyKey);
  console.log("type", type); // type [Function: String]
}
class Person {
  @Prop
  name!: string;
}
```
Normally, it should print `[function: string]`, when my tsconfig.json has the following configuration enabled:
```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "emitDecoratorMetadata": true,
    "experimentalDecorators": true,
  }
}
```
But when I use `deno compile --config tsconfig.json aa.ts` and when executing the generated file, it print `type undefined`.

Personally, I hope to make less changes when using docker, such as adding a configuration to ignore the TS check at runtime. Of course, don't forget to read tsconfig.json first so that my decorator can still take effect.

body:
@jiawei397 This multi-stage build Dockerfile is working (deployment tested with flyio). 
You have to use the same alpine variant (frolvlad/alpine-glibc:alpine-3.17 ) as [deno base image](https://github.com/denoland/deno_docker/blob/main/alpine.dockerfile).
The image size is 148 MB. 
The memory usage displayed in flyio is 58 MB.

```dockerfile
ARG DENO_VERSION=1.36.3
FROM denoland/deno:alpine-${DENO_VERSION} AS builder

WORKDIR /app

# Cache the dependencies as a layer
COPY deno.json .

# These steps will be re-run upon each file change in your working directory:
ADD . .

# Compile the main app
RUN deno compile --allow-net --allow-read --allow-env main.ts -o main 

# Use a Docker multi-stage build to create a lean production image.
# Use the same alpine image as deno one because of glibc requirement.
FROM frolvlad/alpine-glibc:alpine-3.17 as runner

WORKDIR /app

# Prefer not to run as root.
RUN addgroup --gid 1000 deno \
    && adduser --uid 1000 --disabled-password deno --ingroup deno \
    && chown deno:deno /app/
USER deno

COPY --from=builder /app/main .

# The port that your application listens to.
EXPOSE 8000

CMD ["./main"]
```

author: jiawei397

body:
Closing this out. Two things have changed since this was filed in 2022:

1. **Deno's baseline RSS has come down substantially.** The startup/idle footprint of the runtime is much smaller now than it was in the 1.x days this issue measured.

2. **The original comparison was not apples-to-apples.** The report was driven by `docker stats` RSS, which for the Deno processes folded in costs that are not part of a like-for-like server working-set comparison with Node (e.g. the TypeScript type checker being resident, plus the arch/measurement quirks the reporter themselves noted on M1). Notably, in-process `Deno.memoryUsage()` was already reporting *lower* numbers than Node's `process.memoryUsage()` in the same tests, and the reporter's own later update (Deno 1.36.2) showed the gap had narrowed a lot (deno ~30MB vs node ~18MB rss, and `deno compile` builds close behind).

Between the runtime memory reductions and the comparison not being like-for-like, there's nothing actionable left to track here. If anyone sees a specific, reproducible memory regression on a current release (ideally measured by the actual server working set rather than total `docker stats` RSS), please open a focused issue with the repro.
author: bartlomieju

body:
The issue appears to be with decoding a value returning from the built in TypeScript. The type definitions are clear on what values should be returned, and 3 is not one of them, but it is being returned.  The "good" thing is we don't use the information, it is used for Microsoft telemetrics, which we don't use in Deno.

I will remove the field, which should fix the issue.
author: kitsonk

body: Your encoded JSON is not a C String. It must be ('\0') null-terminated.
author: littledivy

body:
> Your encoded JSON is not a C String. It must be ('\0') null-terminated. 

Ah yeah that makes sense. I thought it would be done under-the-hood. So just appending \0 would fix the issue I assume.
author: berendsliedrecht

body:
After a first look, this seems to have been introduced by #14567  

`log_level` is set correctly, but `task_parse()` always uses `argv[2]` as the task name. This can be seen in the output

```
$ deno --quiet task echo
Task not found: task
$ deno --quiet --unstable echo
Task not found: --unstable
```

There is code to get the index of the task name in `argv` (or `raw_args`), but it isn't working as intended.

I'd like to work on this issue

author: ghost

body: As I was trying this out, I realized that since `JsRuntime::handle_scope` and `JsRealm::handle_scope` create a brand new scope rather than adding to the existing scope stack, `JsRealm::execute_script` can't be used in a V8 callback since there's an existing scope stack. This means that you can't really run initialization code inside the context from the `ShadowRealm` constructor – or at least not without building on the existing scopes.
author: andreubotella

body:
I either made such methods static, or moved them to `JsRealm`, in denoland/deno#16211. ~~But this seems not to run into the issue I mentioned in the second comment, even though I'm pretty sure it used to when I tried months ago.~~

Edit: Never mind, it does run into that issue, but `JsRealm::execute_script` only runs on realm creation when the isolate hasn't been built from a snapshot, so you don't end up seeing this in Deno CLI builds (which was what I was first using for testing), but you do see it in `deno_core`'s unit tests.

```

It is occurring during the module graph validation.  The problem is that it is on a "type only" branch which should be validated, but it looks like the validation logic doesn't carry on to its descendants, when in fact it probably should.  It is actually an issue with `deno_graph` and I will raise an issue there.

body:
It isn't a problem with your code. `deno compile` performs a check on the dependencies for a given program. It is only `deno compile` and `deno vendor` that currently do this and there is a logic error in the internal code in Deno which is preventing things from working.

Also, we do an `.unwrap()` instead of more elegant error handling which made the error even more confusing.

author: haiderlikesrust

body:
# Summary

to implement this feature, we need to handle cases like
```jsonc
{
  "files": { "exclude": ["npm/"] }
}
```

## The four cases

there's 4 possible combination for global `"files"` and local config:

1. **no** global `"files"` + config w/wo local `"files"`
2. global `"files"` + config w/wo local `"files"`
3. **no** global `"files"` + **no** config
4. global `"files"` + **no** config

while case 1, 2 and 3 is unambigous, i'm unsure how  to handle case 4.

### **no** global `"files"` + config w/wo local `"files"`
same as before.

### global `"files"` + config w/wo local `"files"`
```jsonc
{
  "files": { "exclude": ["npm/"] },
  "fmt": { "files": { "exclude": ["cov/"] } },
  "lint": {}
}
```
would be resolved to
```jsonc
{
  "fmt": { "files": { "exclude": ["cov/", "npm/"] } },
  "lint": { "exclude": ["npm/"] }
}
```
### **no** global `"files"` + **no** config
same as before.

### global `"files"` + **no** config
```jsonc
{
  "files": { "exclude": ["npm/"] }
}
```
how should this case be handled?

## Possible interpretion

### Implicit Resolving

Include `"files"` regardless local config. above case will be resolved as
```jsonc
{
  "files": { "exclude": ["npm/"] },
  "fmt": { "files": { "exclude": [ "npm/"] } }
}
```

### Explicit Resolving

Only Include `"files"` if local config exists. above case will be resolved as
```jsonc
{}
```

## Rust implmentation

implementation will change by how we decide to resolve files whether implicitly or explicitly.

### Global `"files"` config

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/2502a37d41e8a7f279af74d7dacc009ee1010f67/cli/args/config_file.rs#L316-L320
`"files"` is represented as `FilesConfig`.
```rs
 pub fn to_files_config(&self) -> Result<Option<FilesConfig>, AnyError>
```
there could be a `to_files_config` impl that returns optional filesconfig result.

### "Local" Configs
https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/2502a37d41e8a7f279af74d7dacc009ee1010f67/cli/args/config_file.rs#L688-L696

`FmtConfig`, `LintConfig`, `TestConfig` is generated from `ConfigFile`. via `to_{}_config` impl. it returns `Result<Option<{ConfigName}>, AnyError>`.
all three of them contains `FilesConfig` as their field.

### Resolving config type

#### use `match` boilerplate
we could create a helper function that would do something like 

[implicit resolving](#implicit-resolving):
`Option<FilesConfig>` + `Option<FmtConfig>` -> `FmtConfig`

[explicit resolving](#explicit-resolving):
`Option<FilesConfig>` + `Option<FmtConfig>` -> `Option<FmtConfig>`

the possible drawback is that we'd need to rewrite same code for each `fmt`, `lint` and `test`, which is a bit bulky:
```rs
let filesConfigOption = Option<FilesConfig>;
let fmtConfigOption = Option<FmtConfig>;
match (filesConfigOption, fmtConfigOption) {
    (Some(filesConfig), Some(fmtConfig)) => // ...
    (None, Some(fmtConfig)) => // ...
    (Some(filesConfig), None) => // ...
    (None, None) => // ...
}
```
however it's least intrusive (no need to change struct/return type).

#### use generic local config

```rs
struct WithFilesConfig<T> {
    data: T, // FmtConfig, LintConfig, TestConfig
    files: FilesConfig,
}

impl WithFilesConfig<T> {
    fn extend_files(self, files: FilesConfig) -> WithFilesConfig<T> {
        WithFilesConfig {
            files: self.files + files,
            ..self
        }
    }
}
```
it would remove the need to write duplicate code, but it could potentially break existing code and would need large refactoring.

in short, [using match boilerplate](#use-match-boilerplate) would be preferred.

### Others
since i'm new to rust, please point out if anything is incorrect. thanks in advance.

author: GJZwiers

body:
The concern here is understandable, but a dedicated timezone permission isn't something we plan to add.

The key point: the part of this threat that matters — exfiltration — is already gated. A dependency can read the local timezone, but it can't send it anywhere without `--allow-net` (or persist/exec it without `--allow-write`/`--allow-run`). A low-entropy value that can't leave the process isn't a meaningful leak on its own.

Beyond that, timezone and locale are standard, synchronous web APIs (`Intl.DateTimeFormat().resolvedOptions()`, `Date.prototype.getTimezoneOffset()`) that browsers expose without any permission. Gating them would break ordinary date-formatting code and diverge from the web platform, and it would be incomplete anyway — `Intl` locale, language, and other resolved options expose the same geographic signal, so gating timezone alone wouldn't close the inference channel.

If you want to normalize this regardless, running with `TZ=UTC` makes the reported timezone deterministic. Closing as not planned.

body:
In fact, the `transfer` field is typed as `sequence<object>` in WebIDL, and the actual type checking happens as part of the structured clone algorithm, not on the WebIDL layer. But IIRC Deno first splits buffers from `MessagePort` objects in the transferable list (since those are the only two types of transferable that we currently support), and that happens on the JS side, so the checking is done based on the prototypes.

This might be solved with my work on reworking serialization and transferring (#12067), but that has been stuck for a while.
author: andreubotella

body:
> In fact, the `transfer` field is typed as `sequence<object>` in WebIDL, and the actual type checking happens as part of the structured clone algorithm, not on the WebIDL layer. But IIRC Deno first splits buffers from `MessagePort` objects in the transferable list (since those are the only two types of transferable that we currently support), and that happens on the JS side, so the checking is done based on the prototypes.
> 
> This might be solved with my work on reworking serialization and transferring (#12067), but that has been stuck for a while.

Nonetheless, outside of this particular example, the `isPrototypeOf` can be used to cause similar issues anywhere. (`webidl.createInterfaceConverter` is merely a prototype check.) Although, does that PR intend to rewrite even non-transferable objects are host/platform object, and use something more akin to Chromium's nominal type checking? If not, the issue persists in the rest of the codebase.

body:
We've discussed "declarative endpoints" such as this in the core team a few times and we have yet to arrive at a good workable solution.

Having explicit code without "magical" exports always wins. Because there are a whole host of use cases that quickly fall outside this, like how do you do testing, how do you do setup and teardown, how do you do you close a server. Theb it becomes layer after layer of special code. It also assumes that an HTTP server us the primary/only workload for Deno.

So for me, I am not in favour personally, but others might have different opinions.

body: Looking into it, setting `"types"` in the compiler options would also cause this behaviour.  Basically, whenever there is some that is rooted to the config file, CLI synthetically imports it with the config file as the base.  This causes the graph to add it as a synthetic module and then subsequently, when the JSON import is identified, the graph says "oh, I already have that, so I will just ignore it".  Since synthetic modules cannot be "loaded", it results in the error of the unprepared module.

body:
I'm running into this as well.

This being broken is sort of a big deal.

If you want to use a library that [forces you to use import maps][1], you now give up being able to `deno install` your application.  😢

[1]: https://github.com/denoland/doc_components#twind

---

Update/Tangent: I'm also a bit worried seeing the Deno team using import maps for libraries. As the ecosystem grows, if this becomes a common pattern, every consumer of a library, all the way down the dependency chain, will have to start manually maintaining a larger and larger list of mapped imports, and manually investigating which versions work with which dependencies.

Maybe a better pattern would be to depend on a default version (so avoiding import maps) and have tooling that can (optionally) override that version with import maps as necessary?

author: justinmchase

body:
Thanks for the report, and apologies for the long silence here.

I reproduced this on the latest canary (`deno 2.8.1+61f96bf`) using the original module, so the behavior is unchanged since 1.24.0:

```
deno install -g -A --import-map=https://deno.land/x/wallpal@1.0.0/import_map.json \
  https://deno.land/x/wallpal@1.0.0/wallpal.ts
# error: Module not found "https://deno.land/x/wallpal@1.0.0/std@0.149.0/_util/assert.ts".
```

The root cause is these two self-referential entries in the import map:

```json
"/": "./",
"./": "./"
```

They map `/` and `./` onto the importing module's own root, which causes the transitive **relative** imports of your mapped dependencies (e.g. `fs/mod.ts`'s `./expand_glob.ts`, `flags/mod.ts`'s `../_util/assert.ts`) to be rebased onto `deno.land/x/wallpal@1.0.0/...` instead of staying under `deno.land/std@0.149.0/...`.

I confirmed that removing just those two entries (keeping all the `std/*` and `keypress/` mappings) makes the install succeed and every std dependency resolves correctly:

```json
{
  "imports": {
    "std/fs": "https://deno.land/std@0.149.0/fs/mod.ts",
    "std/io": "https://deno.land/std@0.149.0/io/mod.ts",
    "std/flags": "https://deno.land/std@0.149.0/flags/mod.ts",
    "std/encoding/": "https://deno.land/std@0.149.0/encoding/",
    "keypress/": "https://deno.land/x/keypress@0.0.8/"
  }
}
```

The `"/": "./"` / `"./": "./"` mappings are a known footgun and are essentially never needed. Since this comes down to the import map configuration, I'm going to close this as resolved.

For anyone landing here today: the modern, recommended approach is to manage dependencies in `deno.json` (or use `jsr:`/`npm:`/`https:` specifiers directly), which avoids this class of problem entirely. If you believe Deno should handle a `"./"`-to-root import-map mapping differently per the import-maps spec, please open a new issue with a minimal repro and we can discuss it specifically.

The thing is, I had VS Code open with the extension `rust-analyzer`, presumably building/checking in the background while also building in my external terminal? I've noticed that my CPU cores were active even after the building command was exited on my external terminal. 

I closed VS Code, and my CPU idled, the extension stopped its work I guess.

Retried the build in my external terminal, and it worked.

I ensured that I have the latest `main` branch with:
```sh
git fetch origin # This upstream (git@github.com:denoland/deno.git)
git reset origin/main
```
The building was with: 
```sh
# Build errors?  Ensure you have latest main and try building again, or if that doesn't work try:
cargo clean && cargo build -vv
```

I have also disabled `rust-analyzer.cargo.buildScripts.enable` on the extension config. While I don't know if it's the right thing to do, my CPU is no longer being monopolized by unsolicited builds while already building or coding.

body:
Tested on the latest canary (`deno 2.7.5+5b7680f`) which includes the fix from #32450 — the issue is still reproducible:

```
$ deno run atomics_test.ts
Waiting for Atomics.waitAsync with 2000ms timeout...
error: Top-level await promise never resolved
```

However, if the event loop is kept alive by something else (e.g. a `setTimeout`), `Atomics.waitAsync` does resolve correctly — so the wake mechanism from #32450 works, but the **liveness** side is the problem.

### Root cause

The event loop doesn't know it should stay alive for pending `Atomics.waitAsync` operations. When V8 handles `waitAsync` with a timeout, it internally schedules a delayed foreground task via `PostDelayedTask` on the platform. PR #32450 correctly wakes the event loop when that task fires — but by that point the process has already exited because nothing told `EventLoopPendingState::is_pending()` to return `true`.

V8's `HasPendingBackgroundTasks()` returns `false` for `Atomics.waitAsync` waiters — it only tracks things like background compilation.

### Node.js has the same bug

Tested on Node.js v24.8.0:

```
$ node atomics_test.mjs
Waiting for Atomics.waitAsync with 2000ms timeout...
Warning: Detected unsettled top-level await at file:///tmp/atomics_test.mjs:5
```

Node's platform implementation also creates `uv_unref`'d timers for `PostDelayedTask`, so the loop exits before the timer fires.

### `SetAtomicsWaitCallback` doesn't help

The V8 API `Isolate::SetAtomicsWaitCallback` mentioned earlier in this thread only fires for **synchronous** `Atomics.wait()` (kStartWait, kTimedOut, etc.). It does not fire for `Atomics.waitAsync()`.

### Possible fix paths

1. **Track outstanding platform delayed tasks in `DenoPlatformImpl`**: increment a refcount in `post_delayed_task`, decrement when the task runs via `pump_message_loop`. Expose this to `EventLoopPendingState::is_pending()`. Downside: this would also keep the loop alive for V8-internal delayed tasks (GC scheduling, etc.) unless filtered.

2. **Upstream V8 change**: have `HasPendingBackgroundTasks()` account for `waitAsync` waiters, or add a dedicated API like `HasPendingAtomicsWaiters()`.

3. **JS-level workaround**: wrap `Atomics.waitAsync` to register a ref that keeps the event loop alive until the returned promise settles.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> 2. **Upstream V8 change**: have `HasPendingBackgroundTasks()` account for `waitAsync` waiters, or add a dedicated API like `HasPendingAtomicsWaiters()`.

This seems like the correct fix to me, since AFAICT any runtimes that have an event loop that can stop running if no further events are added, and which use V8 platforms (whether the default one or not) without passing `MessageLoopBehavior::kWaitForWork` to `PumpMessageLoop`, would have this issue, meaning it's a V8 bug.

body:
Dug into this. It's a real Windows-only bug, and the root cause is in ICU (which V8 uses for `Date`):

- On **unix**, ICU reads the `TZ` environment variable when it detects the host time zone, so Deno respects it for free (verified on macOS, both at startup and via `Deno.env.set("TZ", ...)`).
- On **Windows**, ICU detects the time zone from the OS settings and **ignores `TZ`** entirely. Deno's only timezone hook (`dt_change_notif` in `ext/os/lib.rs`) just calls `DateTimeConfigurationChangeNotification(Redetect)`, which re-runs that same host detection -> `TZ` still ignored. And that hook only fires on `Deno.env.set`, so a `TZ` set *before* launch (the `$env:TZ='UTC'; deno run` case here) was never applied at all.

Node avoids this by setting ICU's default zone explicitly (`icu::TimeZone::adoptDefault`) instead of relying on host detection, which is why it works in PowerShell.

Fix in progress, mirroring Node's approach (apply `TZ` to ICU explicitly on Windows, at startup and on runtime set/delete; unix keeps the existing path):

- rusty_v8 (exposes the needed ICU binding): denoland/rusty_v8#2000
- deno: #34705 (includes a cross-platform regression test)

Both are WIP/draft for now — the Deno side is blocked on the rusty_v8 binding being released, but the approach is settled.

body: @littledivy The hybrid op solution works great if you have the sync part first. However, if you want to return `serde_v8::Value` that no longer works because now the async part is first, but you don't want to block on it. One solution to the problem is to split the op into two ops: (1) async op that returns a future on a resource ID and (2) a sync op that turns the resource ID into a `serde_v8::Value`. But this does feel bit clumsy to be honest. I wonder if there's a way to make `HandleScope` lifetime extend to the lifetime of the returned future?
author: penberg

body:
Returning `serde_v8::Value` means the HandleScope needs to be around. 

Note that we are in a Rust future, that means it will execute sometime in the future...even maybe after the scope is deallocated. So, the lifetimes are there for a good reason. 

The correct (& safe) way is to return owned Rust values. `deno_core` (using `serde_v8`) will deserialize them into V8 values when resolving the promise.

The `serde_v8` seemed very promising, but at a closer examination I found that `serde_v8::Serializer` doesn't allow for creation of JS-special types like `v8::Date` or `v8::ArrayBuffer`. (There are no methods to serialize the type and the `HandleScope` seems to be a private property of `serde_v8::Serializer` so I can't even do it manually.

I've also tried to smuggle out of the `op` a class that implements the `serde_v8::Serializable` trait, which looked promising, but the `op` macro doesn't seem to be able to handle that case and still complains that what I'm returning doesn't implement `Serialize` trait :(

body:
We investigated this and the behavior is explained by Deno's type-checking cache:

**What's happening:** When you run `deno test` for the first time (or after changing the source), Deno type-checks the file using TypeScript's `tsc`, which runs inside V8. This compilation step alone uses ~30MB of heap memory. With `--max-old-space-size=32`, V8 hits the heap limit during type-checking before your code even runs.

**Why it appears nondeterministic:** After a successful run (e.g., with `--max-old-space-size=128`), the type-check results are cached. Subsequent runs skip compilation entirely and use much less memory — so `--max-old-space-size=32` works. Changing the source (even decreasing `sizeInMb`) invalidates the cache and triggers a re-check, which OOMs again.

**Confirming this:** Running with `--no-check` (skip type-checking) succeeds with `--max-old-space-size=32` even on a cold cache:
```
DENO_DIR=/tmp/fresh deno test -A --no-check test.ts --v8-flags=--max-old-space-size=32
```

This is working as intended — `--max-old-space-size` controls V8's heap which includes the type-checker. 32MB is below the baseline needed for TypeScript compilation. If you need to run with very low memory limits, use `--no-check` to skip type-checking.

author: iugo

body:
Deno adheres to [web crypto](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SubtleCrypto) which is what browsers implement and [Node.js also supports](https://nodejs.org/api/webcrypto.html).  Those expose the APIs they expose.

If the W3C were to implement lower level APIs [in the standard](https://www.w3.org/TR/WebCryptoAPI/), we would follow suit. Implementing APIs directly in Deno outside of the standards is not desirable, as it may very well expose security vulnerabilities, but also be incompatible with browsers and other implementors of web crypto.
