body:
Functions within the Standard Library that rely on `Reader`/`Writer` interfaces are being deprecated. One must sacrifice a little performance by moving. However, the bridge is closing!

My use case is for Redis, or specifically [x/r2d2](https://deno.land/x/r2d2), which I maintain. Here are some basic benchmarks that would indicate how my client would perform when using `Reader`/`Writer` interfaces vs. the Web Streams API: https://gist.github.com/iuioiua/f1b93f1b7e3c1a055ddf6fc60a6743a2

For my use cases, it'd be great if `TextEncoderStream()`, `TextDecoderStream()` and `ReadableStream.pipeThrough()` were optimised.
author: iuioiua

body: @iuioiua Thanks for the information -- I think piping into a text encoder/text decoder could absolutely be faster.
author: mmastrac

body:
### Status update (as of `main`, June 2026)

Went through the checklist against the current tree. This is partially done, but the central optimization is still missing, so I don't think it can be closed yet.

**Landed:**
- Source branding via `readableStreamForRid` for `FsFile#readable`, `Conn#readable`, `resp.body` from `fetch`, `req.body` in `Deno.serve`, stdin/stdout/stderr, etc. (`ext/web/06_streams.js`).
- `writableStreamForRid` now exists (it was a TODO in the original design) — `ext/web/06_streams.js`.
- The "read all" specialization: `op_read_all` is wired into the resource-backed fast path for body collection (`req.arrayBuffer()` / `resp.arrayBuffer()`), `ext/web/06_streams.js`.

**Still open (the core of this issue):**
- **`op_pipe` does not exist.** There is no generic Rust pipe op.
- **`ReadableStream#pipeTo` / `pipeThrough` are not optimized.** `readableStreamPipeTo` still acquires a JS reader/writer and streams chunk-by-chunk; there's no "both ends resource-backed -> offload to Rust" path.
- **Sink branding** is largely unapplied — `FsFile#writable`, `Deno.stdout`/`Deno.stderr`, `Conn#writable`, `CompressionStream#writable`, etc.
- Several source brandings remain: `CompressionStream#readable`, `TextEncoderStream#readable`, `WebSocketStream#readable`, `Deno.Child#stdout`/`stderr`.
- `req.body` consumption in `fetch` and `resp.body` in `cache.put`.

So the incremental wins (read-all fast path, resource branding helpers) have landed, but the headline work — offloading `pipeTo`/`pipeThrough` to Rust via `op_pipe` when both source and sink are resource-backed — is still not implemented. Keeping this open unless the team considers it superseded by the later resource-unification work.

body:
New Update: 

I had HTTP_PROXY and HTTPS_PROXY set to another Env Var, named AUTOMATIC_PROXY, since I sometimes need to switch my proxy settings and wanted to do that easily. 

Could you implement a recursive Env Var Resolution for HTTP_PROXY and HTTPS_PROXY?
author: bjesuiter

body:
I just checked Deno's manual and it looks like Deno will now fall back to reading proxies from registry on Windows when no corresponding environment variables are found (see [Proxies](https://docs.deno.com/runtime/manual/basics/modules/proxies)). I'm not quite sure how your `AUTOMATIC_PROXY` works, but if you can update your proxies in Windows' system proxy settings, I think Deno might be able to use them when it runs.

By the way, I think it might be less maintainable for Deno to support (several) recursive env var looking up methods, while at the same time this functionality can be achieved with some efforts from the user/OS-side. On Windows it's just as above where the software can read settings from the system's registry (I think vcpkg and Python is doing similar things). And if you're using UNIX-like OS now, maybe run Deno with a prefix like `HTTPS_PROXY=$AUTOMATIC_PROXY`, or I think there are desktop environments that can set up `HTTP_PROXY`s for you in the terminal / we can let Deno read from those dektop environments' settings as it does on Windows.
author: kLiHz

body:
Closing this as out of scope.

Deno honors the standard proxy environment variables (`HTTP_PROXY` / `HTTPS_PROXY` / `http_proxy` / `https_proxy` / `NO_PROXY`), where each value is parsed directly as a proxy URL. Resolving a proxy var's value as the *name* of another env var (the `AUTOMATIC_PROXY` indirection requested here) is a non-standard behavior that no mainstream tooling implements, and it would add a surprising lookup rule to the proxy handling.

The same result is already achievable with shell expansion, which is the conventional way to indirect env vars:

```sh
export HTTP_PROXY="$AUTOMATIC_PROXY"
export HTTPS_PROXY="$AUTOMATIC_PROXY"
```

For fully opting out of proxy/upgrade behaviors there is also `DENO_NO_UPDATE_CHECK` and the standard `NO_PROXY`. Given the standard env vars plus shell expansion cover the use case, this isn't something we plan to build into the runtime.

body: Thinking in terms of a request URL / response URL may offer a little more flexibility here in in trying to reconcile any differences between `import.meta.url` and `import.meta.resolve`. Redirects allow the response URL (as `import.meta.url` represents) to be different to the request URL. `import.meta.resolve` then corresponds to the request URL as it is the client side resolver, before redirects are applied.
author: guybedford

body: There's a casuistry that must be taken into account: the `npm` package that is not cached (because it wasn't imported previously). If it's decided that `import.meta.resolve("npm:react")` must return the cached file, but it isn't already cached, this means Deno must download and cache the package before return the filename.
author: oscarotero

body: @oscarotero `import.meta.resolve` is synchronous uses the _current resolver cache_. This can be distinct from what the tracing loader is doing asynchronously. It is possible for `import.meta.resolve` to fail where dynamic `import()` would pass due to this distinction.

I added a warning on its use in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16953 in future versions, but just beware that it may be changed when we come up with a real solution for this problem (though likely it will still remain... it's just not something that's been discussed yet).
author: dsherret
NickTomlin

body: We're going to discuss this issue tomorrow in our weekly meeting. For now I'm not adding the warning because the environment variable is used in all our tests in order to use a test npm registry, but consider it unstable.
author: dsherret

body: Probably we should just use the same env var as NPM: `NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY`

In the meantime, we can dual-write to both variables to make a transition easier.

body:
Where I work, there are some Node microservices that could benefit from migrating to Deno. However, these microservices depend on private Gitlab NPM packages. This feature is blocking the upgrade to Deno because many of these private packages are critical to these services and cannot run without them.

The work so far on this has been excellent and I've not reported any other issues. I will just have to wait until Deno supports private packages before upgrading.

author: marcushultman

body:
Setting `NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY` *almost* does the job. In my particular case, there are scoped packages sitting in an artifactory registry that **does not proxy npm** directly. This results in two scenarios:

**`NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY` is set to my artifactory registry**

- `@myorg/my-package` is resolved correctly ✔️
- global npm packages such as `express` are not ❌

**`NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY` is not set**

- `@myorg/my-package` is not resolved correctly ❌
- global npm packages such as `express` are fine ✔️

Is there a way I could have two registries set? I tried using spaces ` ` and semicolons `;` in the string, no success. My artifactory uses an auth user with token, which is defined in `.npmrc`
author: felipemullen

body:
@felipemullen I have the same issue as you...

One workaround I found was to use a local proxy like [Verdaccio](https://verdaccio.org/) to proxy the various registries you need.

**Caveat**: I don't have prior experience with Verdaccio, or setting up my own local npm proxy; I just discovered it when looking for solutions to this multi-registry limitation in Deno 😄 

Anyway, I have Verdaccio configured with the following uplinks and package resolutions:
```
uplinks:
  npmjs:
    url: https://registry.npmjs.org/
  github_private:
    url: https://npm.pkg.github.com/
    auth:
      type: bearer
      token_env: GITHUB_TOKEN
packages:
  '@myorg/*':
    access: $all
    publish: $authenticated
    unpublish: $authenticated
    proxy: github_private

  '**':
    access: $all
    publish: $authenticated
    unpublish: $authenticated
    proxy: npmjs
```
So all `@myorg` scoped packages will pull from Github private registry, and the rest will pull from npm.

Then run `verdaccio` locally, supplying any necessary auth tokens:
```
GITHUB_TOKEN=*** verdaccio
```
Then set the Verdaccio server (localhost:4873 by default) as `NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY` and use Deno to cache your dependencies:
```
NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY=http://localhost:4873 deno cache ./my_deno_module.ts
```
author: bhb603

body:
just throwing another monkey wrench into this discussion. I have a use case that involves using npm git dependencies. My company has private npm packages that we simply create git tags for and host on github. These are then defined in package.json like so:
```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "mypackage": "git+ssh://git@github.com:myorganization/mypackage.git#3.1.1"
  }
}
```

The packages themselves contain native code, and use the npm script `"install": "node-gyp rebuild"` to compile them. This works well for us, the only requirement is that we have a relevant git ssh key added wherever the package needs to be installed. In deno however, this does not appear to work:
```ts
import * as mypackage from 'npm:git+ssh://git@github.com:myorganization/mypackage.git#3.1.1'
```

This results in the following error:
```
error: Invalid package specifier 'npm:git+ssh://git@github.com:myorganization/mypackage.git#3.1.1'. Did you mean to write 'npm:git+ssh:@github.com:myorganization/mypackage.git#3.1.1//git'?
```

Is the git ssh reference part of the npm spec supported in deno's npm specifiers?
author: andykais

body:
Thanks @bhb603, that was a great tip. I was able to get set up using docker-compose to support a self-hosted [windmill.dev](https://www.windmill.dev/docs/advanced/self_host) instance.

For anyone else struggling with the same, here are some notes:

`docker-compose.yaml`
```yaml
services:
  verdaccio:
    container_name: verdaccio
    image: verdaccio/verdaccio
    environment:
      - VERDACCIO_PORT=4873
    ports:
      - 4873:4873
    expose:
      - 4873
    volumes:
      - './path/to/local/config:/verdaccio/conf'

windmill_worker:
    ...
    environment:
      - NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY=http://verdaccio:4873
```

`my/local/verddaccio/config/config.yaml`
```yaml
uplinks:
  npmjs:
    cache: false
    url: https://registry.npmjs.org/
  artifactory:
    cache: false
    url: https://mycorp.artifactory.corp.com/api/npm/my-packages/
    headers:
      Authorization: bearer ABC123ZYX098===AABBCOLLISION

packages:
  '@mycorp/*':
    access: $all
    publish: $authenticated
    unpublish: $authenticated
    proxy: artifactory

  '**':
    access: $all
    publish: $authenticated
    unpublish: $authenticated
    proxy: npmjs
```

Docs references:
- private npm registries for windmill - https://www.windmill.dev/docs/advanced/imports#private-npm-registry
- docker networking reference - https://docs.docker.com/compose/networking/
- verdaccio docker-compose docs - https://verdaccio.org/docs/docker#using-docker-compose

The following (or some equivalent) would all be quite useful for being able to offer a great experience to Deno users of my package:

body: Update: we are now working on supporting `.npmrc` file that will allow to use different registries for different scopes.
author: bartlomieju

body:
Update: We're gonna land first pass in #23560 really soon.

The first pass will support `.npmrc` located next to `package.json` or `deno.json` - so it will work with Node.js first projects as well as Deno first projects. Please let me know if you rely on `.npmrc` in your user directory or global directory (https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v10/configuring-npm/npmrc#files).

Only `_authToken` config will be supported in the first pass - please let me know by commenting if you are heavy users of other options so we can prioritize accordingly.

Also the first pass will only support `deno run` / `deno test` subcommands - the support for `deno compile` will come afterwards - please also comment if you need it.

Thanks
author: bartlomieju

body:
Thank you @bartlomieju for working on this!

We are heavy users of [npx google-artifactregistry-auth](https://cloud.google.com/artifact-registry/docs/nodejs/authentication#get-token), which saves the `_authToken` in `$HOME/.npmrc`.

Then in each project, we have an `.npmrc` checked into git, which specifies that our internal _@certain-npm-scope_  should always be fetched from our private npm registry in GCP.

It looks something like this:

`each_project_dir/.npmrc`:

```
@certain-npm-scope:registry=https://europe-west2-npm.pkg.dev/our-gcp-project-id/our-npm-repo-name/
//europe-west2-npm.pkg.dev/our-gcp-project-id/our-npm-repo-name/:always-auth=true
```

`$HOME/.npmrc` (after having run `npx google-artifactregistry-auth`):

```
//europe-west2-npm.pkg.dev/our-gcp-project-id/our-npm-repo-name/:_authToken=…
```

Hi, for Azure Artifacts npm - user level .npmrc with the authToken is desired, see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/artifacts/get-started-npm?view=azure-devops&tabs=Other

I think this is common scenario for npm scoped registry settings - committed .npmrc with scope and registry URL, then $HOME/.npmrc with authToken for this registry.

author: bartlomieju

body:
Hi everyone, we discovered an issue in .npmrc support in Deno 1.44.0 where Deno would send npmrc credentials for the scope to the tarball URL when the registry provided URLs for a tarball on a different domain.

For example, the npm registry provides information on a package at a url like this: https://registry.npmjs.org/code-block-writer -- you can see that under `versions -> <version> -> dist -> tarball` there's a URL (ex. https://registry.npmjs.org/code-block-writer/-/code-block-writer-1.0.0.tgz). That URL can be anything and Deno was only considering the first URL of https://registry.npmjs.org/code-block-writer to get credentials instead of the tarball URL (so in other words, it would send the credentails for `https://registry.npmjs.org/` to whatever the tarball URL was)

You can read more about this here: https://github.com/npm/cli/wiki/%22No-auth-for-URI,-but-auth-present-for-scoped-registry%22 (how Deno is working is actually how npm used to behave)

I recommend to stop using .npmrc support in Deno 1.44.0 and if your private registry ever serves tarballs at a different domain to rotate your registry credentials. We have a fix for this here: https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/24111 and are going to do a release in a few hours to fix this issue.

Huge apologies on this one.
author: dsherret

body: If a `1+1 = 2` test, takes 30 seconds, this definitely is a BUG, not need any form of discussion.
author: loynoir

body:
```ts
import { deepStrictEqual } from "https://deno.land/std@0.158.0/node/assert/strict.ts";

Deno.test("reproduce", (_t) => {
  deepStrictEqual(1 + 1, 2);
});
```

```sh
$ reproduce=/path/to/reproduce.ts
$ cd `mktemp -d` \
 && echo '{}' > deno.jsonc \
 && cp $reproduce test.ts \
 && time deno test -A ./test.ts
Check test.ts
running 1 test from ./test.ts
reproduce ... ok (6ms)

ok | 1 passed | 0 failed (297ms)

deno test -A ./test.ts  12.91s user 0.26s system 43% cpu 30.102 total
```

Note: deno test type check seems to have cache system, thus, ALWAYS use a new folder for reproduce.
author: loynoir

author: KyleJune

body:
Largely these are to be expected.  For efficiency reasons, we don't always recalculate stats on all files when a change is made in a dependent file until that file is edited again.  Type checking, especially in large complex projects is expensive, and re-calculating every open file one every edit is inefficient.  It is really hard to determine if a change in one file impacts the types of another file, even if we understand the one file is dependant on the other.

As you note, once the open file is edited again, all the diagnostics "catch up" and become accurate.

At some point in the future, we might be able to improve our invalidation and re-calculation of open files, but for now, I am afraid this is a design limitation.
author: kitsonk

body: For this issue, I'm not editing any other files. Even if I save the file after the re-calculation resolves the warning, it will return when I reload my window. Why doesn't the incorrect cache of the diagnostics used when the file is first opened get replaced after new calculations are done? It seems like it keeps wanting to go back in time to using diagnostics from when maybe there was a warning.
author: KyleJune

body:
This no longer reproduces on current Deno. The panic was `deno compile` calling `.unwrap()` on a `Missing(Url)` graph error (`cli/main.rs:425` in 1.23.1); that code is long gone and missing modules are now surfaced gracefully.

I reproduced the exact scenario - a module served with an `X-TypeScript-Types` header pointing to a `.d.ts` that 404s - against a local server:

```
Download http://localhost:4599/mod.js
Download http://localhost:4599/missing.d.ts   <- 404s
Check file:///tmp/main.ts
Compile ... to ./out
```

No panic. The missing type-only declaration is tolerated, compilation succeeds, and the resulting binary runs correctly (same with `--no-check`). A plain 404 import also produces a clean `TS2307: Cannot find module` error rather than panicking.

(Reproduced with a local server rather than the original skypack URLs / Deno 1.23.1, which are stale.)

Closing as resolved.
author: bartlomieju
FuriouZz

body:
This is no longer a Deno-specific issue and can be closed.

Re-tested on current canary (Deno 2.8.1). The `TypeError: Invalid URL` still
reproduces, but **stock Node.js 24 now fails identically**:

```
# Deno
[fferr] TypeError: Invalid URL: '.../@ffmpeg/core/dist/ffmpeg-core.wasm'
# Node 24
Error: Failed to parse URL from .../@ffmpeg/core/dist/ffmpeg-core.wasm
```

**Root cause** is in `@ffmpeg/core@0.11`'s (old) emscripten loader, which picks
its wasm-loading strategy roughly like this:

```js
return wasmBinary
  || typeof WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming !== "function"
  || isDataURL() || startsWith("file://")
  || typeof fetch !== "function"
  ? syncFsRead()              // safe: fs.readFileSync
  : fetch(barePath, {...})    // fetch('/abs/path/ffmpeg-core.wasm')
```

The wasm path is a bare absolute filesystem path (not a `file://` URL). When the
runtime exposes both global `fetch` and `WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming`, every
guard is false, so the loader calls `fetch('/abs/path')`, which throws because
`fetch` requires a valid URL.

When this issue was filed (2022), Node 16 was current and had **no** global
`fetch`, so Node took the safe `fs.readFileSync` branch while Deno (which already
had global `fetch`) took the broken one. Node 18+ now also ships global `fetch`,
so both runtimes hit the same path. This is not something Deno can fix in the
runtime: making `fetch` accept bare filesystem paths would violate the spec, and
Node doesn't do it either.

Resolution is on the user side: upgrade to `@ffmpeg/ffmpeg@0.12+`, which was
rewritten and no longer uses this loader pattern (`@ffmpeg/core@0.11` is simply
old emscripten output).

Closing as obsolete.

body:
Status check on this tracking issue: almost everything is done, with one remaining gap.

- [x] Ed25519 key generation (#14119)
- [x] Ed25519 import/export (#16140)
- [x] X25519 operations (deriveBits + JWK import) and tests
- [x] **X448** — fully implemented (`ext/crypto/x448.rs`, ops wired in `lib.rs`, import/export/deriveBits in `00_crypto.js`)
- [ ] **Ed448** — still not implemented

Ed448 is the only CFRG curve still missing. There is no `Ed448` algorithm branch anywhere in `ext/crypto/00_crypto.js`; the only `ed448` references in the crate come from the `ed448-goldilocks` dependency, which is used internally for X448 (Montgomery-curve math), not exposed as an `Ed448` WebCrypto signing algorithm.

So current CFRG coverage is X25519 + X448 + Ed25519. This issue should stay open to track Ed448, or be closed in favor of a focused "Implement Ed448 WebCrypto algorithm" issue.

body:
## Plan of action: a side-effects cache for npm build scripts

We already run lifecycle scripts behind `--allow-scripts` (`libs/npm_installer/lifecycle_scripts.rs`), so the "should this script run" half is in place. The remaining gap, and the part that matters for the global node_modules story, is that build output is never cached: every project re-runs `postinstall`, and there is no safe way to share a built package across projects when files are hardlinked into the global store.

The proposal is a pnpm-style side-effects cache: run a package's build once, snapshot the built package directory as an input-keyed variant in the global cache, and link that variant into every project instead of re-running the script.

### Why the current architecture already gets us most of the way

1. Lifecycle scripts run today, in topo order, inside the project's `node_modules/.deno/` staging dir (`local.rs` around the `lifecycle_scripts_executor.execute(...)` call), with a marker file recording that scripts ran.
2. Linking is already hardlink / clonefile / copy-fallback (`libs/npm_installer/fs.rs` `clone_dir_recursive`, called from `local.rs` and `hoisted.rs`). Linking a built variant is the same code path pointed at a different source folder, so no new linking machinery is needed.
3. The global cache already has a variant-folder precedent: `package_folder_for_id` in `libs/cache_dir/npm.rs` writes `.../{version}` and `.../{version}_{copy_index}` for peer-dep duplicates. A built variant can live beside it as `.../{version}.built_{input_hash}`.
4. We already compute sha512 for tarball integrity (`libs/npm_cache/tarball_extract.rs`); the same primitive can hash build inputs.

### Phases

**Phase 1: persist built output to the global cache.**
After a package's scripts run in `.deno/`, copy that package's own directory into the global cache as a built variant. Add the variant-path builder in `libs/cache_dir/npm.rs`.

**Phase 2: redirect the link source on a cache hit.**
When syncing node_modules, if a built variant matching the input key exists, use it as the `clone_dir_recursive` source instead of the pristine extraction. This is the only behavioral change in `local.rs` / `hoisted.rs`.

**Phase 3: design the input key (the make-or-break part).**
The key must capture, at minimum: package name + version, the package's own script text, the resolved versions of its dependencies (build output can depend on them), platform / arch, and the Deno / Node ABI for native addons, plus relevant toolchain presence (Python, C compiler). Under-keying causes both wrong skips and shipping the wrong binary, so we key generously and re-check the build allowlist on every cache hit.

**Phase 4: handle the packages we cannot cache.**
Scripts that write outside their own package directory (Cypress downloads a binary to a separate cache, Puppeteer / Playwright download browsers to the home dir, some node-gyp flows) cannot be captured by a per-directory snapshot. For these we skip the cache and log "not cached, re-running" so staleness is never silent. This mirrors pnpm's guidance to disable the side-effects cache for such packages.

### Edge to formalize

On Linux, files in `.deno/` are hardlinks into the pristine global cache, so a script that edits a file in place would mutate the shared inode and corrupt the cache. Most build output is new files, so this is usually safe, but the design should make it explicit: build on a true copy, snapshot, then link, and never let a script touch a hardlinked pristine file.

### Scope

Opt-in, allowlist-gated (reuse `--allow-scripts` / an `allowBuilds`-style list), and limited to packages whose output stays inside their own directory. The remaining risk lives entirely in the input key, which is a design problem rather than an architecture blocker.

### Code touch-points

- `libs/npm_installer/lifecycle_scripts.rs` (script execution, reuse)
- `libs/npm_installer/local.rs` (snapshot output after execute; redirect link source)
- `libs/npm_installer/hoisted.rs` (same for the hoisted linker)
- `libs/npm_installer/fs.rs` (`clone_dir_recursive` linking primitive)
- `libs/cache_dir/npm.rs` (`package_folder_for_id`, add the built-variant path)
- `libs/npm_cache/tarball_extract.rs` (sha512 primitive for the input key)

body:
> Can it be useful to add importer informations on import.meta for better logs (and errors) ? For instance, a multiple times imported module can log contextualized messages (eg.: [in version checker to indicate which module require a specific version](https://github.com/JOTSR/ensure_version/blob/275d8f1213bc6b3f8b85f5a267d814a4f5014b09/ensure_version.ts#L123) ).

I don't see a way to do that - there's a single callback for `import.meta` that is called when the module is created and there's no way to change this value depending on the referrer.

> Maybe Deno.mainModule should be moved (or duplicate since import.meta is not accessible outside of modules, eg: REPL) to

`Deno.mainModule` is guarded with a permission check - moving it to `import.meta` would require to remove this check. Also this would never translate to the web as there might be multiple "main" modules there.

Thanks for the suggestion, but I'm going to respectfully decline.

body:
Did you see the last part of the manual? 
https://deno.land/manual@v1.26.1/tools/compiler

Dynamic imports aren't supposed to work when compiled.
author: Seally

body:
Wow, I didn't really see that. I'm very sorry.

Are there plans for dynamic imports to be supported?
author: EduGFon

body:
The idea with `deno compile` is that every module you import is already built into the executable, and you can't import anything else on demand. In order to enable dynamic import with `deno compile`, you'd need to have a list of modules that can be imported, and pass that as a flag when compiling. This is also why dynamic import doesn't work on Deno Deploy, since the edge servers that run your code don't know how to transpile Typescript or otherwise build a module graph.

As far as I know, there are no plans to do this. But the Deploy issue is a very common complaint (denoland/deploy_feedback#1), so it's probably somewhere on the Deno core team's todo list.
author: andreubotella

body: Okay, thank you very much for your attention.

author: dsherret

body:
I've noticed there is an inconsistency here though.

If you have `"importMap": "import_map.json"` in a local config it works, but if you have that in a remote config it will fail.

I think either `importMap` should be treated purely as URL, ie. so it must be specified with `./` (like a module specifier), or the same special case is applied if it appear to be 'bare' (ie. not a valid absolute URL).

So the logic (for both local and remote configs) could be:

If it's not a valid URL (relative or absolute), then just prefix it with `./` and use `deno_core::resolve_import`.

Or, should it just fail?
author: jollytoad

body: Anyway, I've gone for the simplest, most backward compatible fix for now, of just checking if its an absolute URL, and then falling back to the original file path resolution.
